    【Commentary: Chen Wuzhe stated: Blood collapse is not a light illness, especially postpartum; it indicates a severe injury. It is suspected that it does not solely result from excessive sour and salty intake but often arises from shock, worry, anger, or imbalance in the internal organs. Additionally, taking hemostatic medicine too early postpartum can lead to retained bad blood, resulting in fullness and hardening, which may cause collapse. The Gujing Pill is difficult to hold accountable; it is better to use a hefty preparation of Xiongzhuan Decoction with added Shaoyao, and an observation period is needed to follow up with treatments according to symptoms.】[53p]
    Menstrual Imbalance After Childbirth

    After childbirth, if menstrual flow is irregular, it is caused by deficiency in blood and qi, with external wind evils invading. When the evil and the proper qi clash, it leads to a disruption in the menstrual cycle, resulting in excessive or diminished flow that cannot attain balance.

    [Note] If the issue arises after a delayed period accompanied by pain, both qi and blood are deficient; treat with Ba Zhen Tang (Eight Treasure Decoction) plus Chai Hu and Dan Pi. If it arrives early, it suggests blood heat; treat with Si Wu Tang (Four Substance Decoction) plus Shan Zhi Zi and Chai Hu. If pain arises as the period approaches, it indicates excess blood; treat with Si Wu Tang plus Tao Ren and Hong Hua. If it is late, it suggests blood deficiency; treat with Si Wu Tang plus Ren Shen and Bai Zhu. If the blood is purple-black and forms clots, it suggests blood heat; treat with Si Wu Tang plus Chao Zhi Zi, Chao Lian, and Dan Pi. If accompanied by pain and pale color, it indicates excessive phlegm; treat with Si Wu Tang combined with Er Chen Tang (Two Aged Decoction). Treatment should be tailored to the specific case.

    [Commentary] According to "The Compendium": After childbirth, the cold and warm airs invade the meridians, alternating between cold and hot. Cold causes blood to congeal, while heat causes blood to dissipate, leading to flow that is either too much or too little, occurring before or after the expected date, hence the term "irregular."[54p]
    Lunar Water Not Flowing

    After childbirth, when the milk has been present for a year but menstruation has not resumed, this is considered normal. If menstruation begins after half a year, it indicates an excess of blood. If menstruation does not occur for one or two years without illness, there is no need for medication. If there is fatigue and a decrease in appetite with internal heat, it indicates a lack of blood, and it is advisable to strengthen the spleen and stomach; using medicine to promote flow in this case would be a mistake.

    [Note: If there is weakness of the spleen and stomach, use the Six Gentlemen Decoction. If there is accompanying angry fire injuring the spleen, use the Return to Spleen Decoction with added Dan Pi and Shan Zhi. If anger has harmed the blood, it is advisable to use the Four Substances combined with Xiao Chai Hu Decoction. For both Qi and blood deficiency, use the Eight Treasures Decoction with added Mu Dan Pi. Refer to prior discussions.]

    [Commentary: According to "The Great Compendium": When childbirth injures the blood and Qi, and the deficiency has not yet recovered, one may be harmed by wind and cold. When blood becomes cold, it congeals; thus, wind and cold harm the flow, causing blood to stagnate within the channels, resulting in the lunar water not flowing. When blood stagnates, the lunar water does not flow, leading to blood masses; if water and blood combine and encounter weakness of the spleen and stomach, this results in edema.]

    [Commentary: In women, the Chong and Ren vessels are considered the sea of channels and are all rooted within the womb. The Hand Taiyang Small Intestine Meridian and the Hand Shaoyin Heart Meridian, in this context, produce milk above and lunar water below.][55p]
    If the menstrual flow does not return after childbirth, it is due to the exhaustion of qi and blood, or excessive blood loss and the smooth flow of milk. If the child is still nursing, whether at six months or up to a year, and the menstrual cycle does not resume, this is normal and not a condition to be worried about, as the saying goes: "Milk is a false period." If menstruation returns after six months or as early as four or five months, this indicates a vibrant young person with abundant blood, where lactation is likely to be plentiful. If the child loses the ability to nurse, they will show signs of weakness in the limbs, a distended belly with prominent veins, a large and dry head, and a desire to eat dirt, a condition that is termed "innocent sickness."

    If there is an excess of menstrual blood, medication should not be used to stop it. If menstruation does not resume one or two years after childbirth without any symptoms of illness, there is no need for medication. If there is fatigue affecting the qi and blood, and the Chong and Ren vessels are weak, leading to insufficient energy for menstruation, one should instead take tonics for the spleen and stomach to nourish qi and blood, which will naturally aid in resuming the cycle.

    Using herbs like牛膝 (niu xi), 红花 (hong hua), 苏木 (su mu), 干漆 (gan qi), 蝱虫 (zhuan chong), and 水蛭 (shui zhi) to stimulate menstruation may cause great harm. If menstrual blood is depleted, there will be nothing to nourish it, and it will not flow. This can be compared to asking a beggar for a large sum of money—no matter the effort, it cannot be obtained. Physicians should take care to examine and treat in detail.[56p]
    Swelling of the Limbs

    Postpartum swelling of the limbs is due to the failure of blood that accumulates and flows into the meridians, thus the use of Xiao Diao Jing San is advised. Chen Wuzhe states that if the wind pathogen invades the Qi level, resulting in the skin swelling and feeling soft, it is due to Qi. If the skin swells like a ripe plum, it is due to water. Generally, Qi swelling should be treated by inducing perspiration, while water swelling should be addressed with diuretics.

    Note: If the condition is due to cold water damaging the earth element, it is important to nourish the spleen and lungs. If the swelling is due to Qi deficiency, it is necessary to strengthen the spleen and stomach. If the swelling is due to water-accumulating Qi, it is essential to replenish central Qi. Considerations for treating women's blood and water-related swelling are also important.

    Commentary: Guo’s perspective states that postpartum blood stagnation leads to accumulation in the limbs, resulting in a gradual onset that causes the face to appear pale and the limbs to swell. Medical practitioners who are unaware may mistakenly attribute it to water retention.[57p]
    In treating water retention, one often uses drugs like Gansui and Dajie, which are very depleting to the body. After childbirth, the body is already weakened, and then further depleting the body with medications leads to severe weakness, often resulting in stillbirth. However, taking a mild regulating medicine can help improve blood circulation and reduce swelling, leading to recovery.

    Chen Wuzhe stated that postpartum edema has various causes; some may persist from pregnancy, while others are due to improper care after childbirth, external influences like cold, heat, wind, and dampness, or internal factors like emotions affecting blood and qi, causing blockages in the meridians. It's essential to distinguish between issues related to qi and blood, and treatment should be based on the pulse and symptoms, tailored to the specific situation. The mild regulating medicine effectively addresses blood-related issues, but its action is limited; it’s better to use formulas like Wuzhuyu Decoction, Zhizhu Decoction, Duohun Powder, or the Great Regulating Decoction, which all require herbal ingredients.

    Gao also remarked that Heilong Dan can also treat postpartum edema caused by blood stasis, which is important to note.[58p]
    Abdominal Pain and Diarrhea after Childbirth

    Postpartum abdominal pain and diarrhea occur due to a deficiency of the spleen and stomach, where cold evil invades, or due to undigested food causing bowel distress and noise. If hands and feet are cold, the remedy is to use the Tuning Middle Decoction. Chen Wuze states: if caused by the six evils or seven emotions, treatment should follow the specific cause.

    [Note: If symptoms include fullness and bloating in the chest or aversion to food with sour regurgitation, this indicates food stagnation, and the remedies Six Gentleman Decoction, Bitter Orange, and Hawthorn should be used to disperse and guide. If digestion has taken place but pain persists, along with symptoms of headache, thirst, aversion to cold, and nausea, this indicates damage to the middle qi, and remedies like Tonic Decoction of the Middle and Qi, Banxia, and Poria should be used to strengthen the spleen and stomach.]

    [Commentary: Guo states: Postpartum wind and cold invade through a deficiency and flow into the large intestine, causing undigested food to create bowel distress and noise, potentially resulting in red or white discharge, or pain that migrates unpredictably; urgently administering Tuning Middle Decoction can lead to immediate relief. If a physician mistakenly treats it as accumulation of stagnation, disaster will follow swiftly. Exercise caution!]

    [Commentary: Chen Wuze emphasizes that postpartum diarrhea is not caused by just one factor. With mentions of cold drinks and wind exposure, the causes can be extensive. Cold, heat, wind, and dampness belong to external factors; joy, anger, worry, and contemplation stem from internal nature. Additionally, labor, rest, hunger, and satiety can all lead to illness. If there is bowel distress, Tuning Middle Decoction can be taken. However, for red or white discharge, treatments must be tailored to specific symptoms.][59p]
    Red and White Dysentery

    Postpartum dysentery is caused by the six evils of diet and the seven emotions, which harm the spleen and stomach, or blood leaking into the large intestine, all of which are difficult to treat. If there is an inability to take in food, it is called 'virtual dysentery'; if the qi pathway is obstructed, it is called 'qi dysentery.' The treatment method is to cool what is hot, warm what is cold; when hot and cold clash, adjust with a balance of warmth and coolness. For slippery conditions, dry it out; for deficiency, tonify. Separate the undifferentiated water and grains, and harmonize the stubbornness of character. There has never been a case without uncertainty.

    Note: The preceding symptoms indicate that white pertains to the qi aspect and red pertains to the blood aspect. If injury is from rice, use Six Gentlemen Decoction with millet powder. If injury is from wheat, use Six Gentlemen Decoction with wheat powder. If injury is from meat, use Six Gentlemen Decoction with hawthorn and shenqu; in all cases with accompanying vomiting, add patchouli. If accompanied by acid throat or vomiting, use the previous medicine with Yuanju Pills. If the liver wood overcomes the spleen earth, use Six Gentlemen Decoction with Chai Hu and roasted ginger. If cold water returns to insult the earth, use Qian's Yi Huang Powder.[60p]
    If there is prolonged diarrhea or the original qi has sunken, complement with the Bu Zhong Yi Qi Decoction to lift and promote yang qi. If the diarrhea is yellow, it indicates true qi of the spleen has been affected, so add muxiang (costus) and cardamom. If it is due to deficiency and cold of the spleen, use the Six Gentlemen Decoction with added muxiang, ginger, and cassia. If there is cold due to deficiency in both the spleen and kidney, use Bu Zhong Yi Qi and Si Shen Wan. If the fire of the Mingmen is weak and there is cold in the spleen, use the Eight Flavor Pill to nourish the earth mother. If there is difficulty urinating, gradually swelling in the limbs, or concurrent coughing and wheezing, use the Jin Su Ren Qi Wan to nourish the spleen and kidney and facilitate the water pathways. If the stomach qi is weak and limbs are swollen, prioritize nourishing the stomach. If it does not improve after a long period, or is not caused by dietary injury, it involves depletion of kidney qi. This is because the fetal membranes are governed by Ren and connected to the kidney, particularly during the ninth and tenth months, when the kidney and bladder must be nourished by Si Shen, Liu Wei, and Ba Wei herbs to replenish the kidney. Using diuretics in this case would only exacerbate the deficiency.[61p]
    According to the "Great Encyclopedia": Postpartum dysentery arises from labor injuries, insufficient visceral function, and an incomplete recovery from the day's and night's exertions. It can occur due to premature physical activity or the accidental consumption of raw and cold foods. If one rises too early, external wind and cold may invade the intestines and stomach when the body's defenses are weak. Misconsumption of undigestible raw and cold items can damage the spleen and stomach, leading to watery diarrhea that may severely progress to dysentery. If blood seeps into the large intestine, it results in a type of dysentery that is difficult to treat, commonly referred to as "childbirth dysentery." If affected by cold, the discharge may be white or resemble fish brain; if heat is involved, it may appear red or yellow, potentially manifesting as sudden bleeding. When cold and heat clash, the resulting diarrhea may be red and white, or mixed with pus and blood, while blue diarrhea indicates extreme cold. If there is a lack of appetite, irregular bowel movements, and excessive straining night and day, postpartum deficiency is exacerbated, leading to prolonged diarrhea that does not cease, weakness and emaciation, a condition known as "deficiency-related diarrhea."[62p]
    There are cases of postpartum qi stagnation leading to discharge of red and white diarrhea, known as qi diarrhea. If diarrhea after childbirth is accompanied by thirst, it indicates a depletion of bodily fluids, or that the stomach qi is deficient and cannot produce fluids. If we simply address the thirst, the diarrhea will resolve on its own.

    Note: If the symptom of thirst occurs without a desire for cold drinks, it is associated with stomach qi deficiency, which cannot generate fluids; in this case, the Seven Flavor Atractylodes Powder should be used. If there is nighttime fever and thirst, it indicates weak kidney water failing to moisten; the Six-Flavor Pill should be used, accompanied by a tonifying qi decoction to nourish the source of digestion.

    Commentary: According to the "Great Compendium," the essence of food and drink transforms into blood, qi, and bodily fluids, nourishing the organs. When the organs are deficient and dry, it leads to diarrhea and thirst. If one drinks excessively, it becomes hard to stop and results in overflow of water and qi. When the spleen and stomach are both deficient, they cannot handle water, leading to overflow that soaks the skin, causing swelling. Simply stopping the thirst will allow the diarrhea to heal.[63p]
    Constipation After Childbirth

    Postpartum constipation occurs due to weakness of the intestines and stomach, along with insufficient fluids. If there is fullness and bloating in the lower abdomen, it is advisable to take Ma Ren Wan to moisten it. However, using cold medicines may worsen the situation. If one calculates the duration of the symptoms and has eaten excessively, taking medicine to relieve constipation becomes a great risk; it is essential to wait until one feels a bloated abdomen and the urge to relieve oneself but cannot, as this indicates blockage in the rectum, in which case pig bile should be used to moisten.

    If one takes bitter and cold remedies to alleviate this, they may harm the central qi, leading to further complications, such as increased difficulty in passing stool, diarrhea that cannot be controlled, or the development of a "plum pit" feeling. If there has been excessive blood loss, take Shi Quan Da Bu Tang. For blood deficiency with fire dryness, use Jia Wei Si Wu Tang. For both qi and blood deficiency, apply Ba Zhen Tang. Even if there are several days without a bowel movement, if the diet remains normal and the abdomen feels the same, continue with Ba Zhen Tang combined with Tao Ren and Xing Ren. Miscalculating the duration and taking remedies for excessive eating can lead to further issues.[64p]
    According to Guo, postpartum discharge of both water and blood, along with weakened intestines and stomach, causes a deficiency of fluids, which leads to constipation. If after five or six days there is abdominal distension, it indicates that dry feces are blocked in the organs due to their hardness and inability to pass. It is advisable to take ma ren wan to moisten. If one mistakenly believes there is heat and administers cold medicine, it will result in the depletion of yang and the proliferation of yin, leading to various adverse conditions that can jeopardize life.

    Fluids are the byproduct of blood, and due to the depletion from childbirth, fluid loss leads to diminished qi, which is why constipation occurs. In milder cases, consuming tangerine peel or apricot kernel can help lubricate and promote passage. However, if after six or seven days there is still fullness and pain in the abdomen with no relief, this indicates that dry feces are present and cannot be expelled. Take ma ren wan to facilitate bowel movement, and expelling the dry feces will bring recovery. Do not mistake it for heat and use strong cooling agents to counter it, as this will further damage the stomach qi and lead to multiple complications.

    Postpartum, when both large and small intestines do not function, it is due to the weakness of the stomach and intestines and the dehydration of fluids. Note that previous ailments should be referenced when discussing various symptoms of diarrhea and constipation. According to the "Great Compendium," the intestines and stomach are originally burdened by heat; with the loss of blood and fluid postpartum, the dryness creates stagnation, causing blockage in both bowel movements.[65p]
    Defecation After Childbirth

    After childbirth, for defecation issues, take dried grass from old swallow nests and burn it into a powder. You may also use dried alum, oyster shell, white belamcanda, and peony, each in equal parts to make a powder, and mix it with wine for consumption. Alternatively, you may use the solidifying intestinal pill formula.

    Note: If there is weakness in the spleen and kidney, use a lesser supplement and focus on the "Buzhong Yiqi Decoction." For deficiency cold, add nutmeg, cuscus, or use the "Sijunzi Decoction." If there is spleen and kidney deficiency with cold, use "Bawuwei Wan" along with "Sijunzi Wan," while still supporting with the previous two formulas.

    Various Urinary Issues

    After childbirth, various urinary issues occur due to heat affecting the bladder; if deficiency, there will be frequent urination, while if heat is present, there will be urgency and pain. If qi deficiency accompanies heat, blood can enter the bladder, leading to blood in the urine, resulting in hematuria.

    Note: If there is a virtual heat in the bladder, use "Liuwu Wan." If there is yin deficiency without corresponding yang transformation, use "Ziyin Shenqi Wan." The earth generates metal, and metal generates water, which should nourish and transform the source, while also addressing frequent urinary issues.

    Commentary: According to "Daquan," heat from childbirth can invade the bladder, leading to pain during urination, thus termed "lin." Additionally, if childbirth depletes qi causing heat to inhibit the blood, the blood can leak into the bladder leading to hematuria. Lin signifies dripping.

    Further Commentary: "San Yin Lun" states that for urinary blockages after childbirth, focus on blood expulsion as a primary treatment. For issues arising from heat, stones, or cold blockages, tailor the approach according to deficiency and sufficiency. Chuanxiong and Puhuang are particularly essential medicines following childbirth. By thoroughly investigating the causes, the key methods may be revealed.[66p]
    Frequent Urination

    Frequent urination after childbirth occurs when the Qi is deficient and cannot be controlled. Note: If the condition is caused by the midwife's negligence leading to bladder damage and dripping urination, use the Eight Treasures Decoction to replenish Qi and blood. If the frequent urination is due to deficient Qi in the bladder, one should tonify the spleen and lungs. If it's due to yin deficiency in the bladder, one should nourish the lungs and kidneys. Refer to treatments for women with similar conditions of frequent urination.

    Incontinence

    The "Guangji" suggests using charred chicken tail to treat postpartum incontinence. The "Qianjin Yi" recommends using powdered white peony and root of the peony, taken with warm wine three times a day; alternatively, half a tael of soft-shelled turtle and one tael of dragon bone powdered, with each dose taken with two qian mixed in congee. Note: If the condition is due to Yang deficiency of the spleen and lungs, use the Bu Zhong Yi Qi Decoction. If it is due to Yin deficiency of the liver and kidneys, use the Liu Wei Di Huang Wan. If it is due to Qi deficiency and cold in the liver and kidneys, use the Ba Wei Di Huang Wan. According to Chen, if a woman experiences difficulties in childbirth, it can harm the bladder, leading to involuntary urination.[67p]
    Blood in Urine After Childbirth

    If blood appears in the urine after childbirth, it is due to deficient heat causing blood to seep into the bladder. Grind clean and burn wild vegetables into a powder, and take it mixed with rice drink; alternatively, take one drachm of talc powder mixed with the juice of fresh rehmannia.

    Note: If the patient exhibits symptoms of excessive heat from rich foods, add the modified Qingwei Powder. For damp-heat from alcoholic beverages, use the Ge Gen Jiexin Decoction. For emotional disturbances causing liver fire, employ the modified Xiaochaihu Decoction. If there is spleen Qi stagnation, use the modified Guipi Decoction. For worries and overthinking harming the heart, use Miaoxiang San. If there is wind-heat in the large intestine, combine Siwu Decoction with arborvitae and sophora flowers. For blood-heat in the large intestine, use Siwu Decoction with stir-fried lotus root and sophora flowers. If there is deficiency in the intestines and stomach, use Liu Junzi Decoction with cimicifuga and chaihu. If there is Qi sinking, combine Buzhong Yiqi Decoction with poria and pinellia. If there is weakness in stomach Qi, use Liu Junzi Decoction with cimicifuga. For blood deficiency, use herbs combined with cimicifuga. If there is both Qi and blood deficiency, use Bajin Decoction with chaihu and cimicifuga. Generally, for prolonged illness or weak vital energy leading to various symptoms of fever, all relate to the damage of spleen and stomach, presenting as true cold internally and false heat externally. Simply using Liu Junzi Decoction or Buzhong Yiqi with roasted ginger can help restore spleen Qi, alleviating all symptoms. If the limbs are cold, it is due to Yang Qi deficiency and should urgently incorporate aconite.

    Commentary: According to the "Comprehensive Classic," when blood and Qi are deficient and heat attacks, the blood becomes scattered and seeps into internal spaces, thus leading to blood in the urine.[68p]
    Miscarriage and Bleeding

    After a miscarriage, there may be a loss of meridians and continuous bleeding. In severe cases, it could lead to distress and even death, primarily focusing on tonifying and supplementing stomach qi.

    Note: If the condition is due to heat in the liver meridian, use Four Substance Decoction, ginseng, white atractylodes, and gardenia. For liver wind-heat, apply Fangfeng Huangqin Pill. For liver anger-fire, use Jiawei Xiaoyao Powder. For spleen qi deficiency, administer Four Gentlemen Decoction, Rehmannia, and earth. For spleen stagnation, try Jiawei Guipi Decoction. If qi and flavor are imbalanced, use Zisu Drink. If stomach qi is sinking, employ Buzhong Yiqi Decoction.

    Commentary: According to Xue Ji, postnatal bleeding may result from dietary habits or the six excesses and seven emotions, leading to depletion of original qi and external injury to yang meridians. Treatment: If due to large intestine wind-heat, prescribe Four Substance Decoction with arborvitae leaf, fine chrysanthemum, fangfeng, bitter orange peel, and pagoda flower. For anger causing liver fire, use Six Gentlemen Decoction with chuanxiong, peony, and rehmannia. For cold and deficiency in the intestines and stomach, modify Six Gentlemen Decoction with nutmeg and costus. For large intestine blood-heat, add gentian and coptis to Four Substance Decoction. This treatment is similar to those for urinary bleeding.[69p]
    After childbirth, if the yin (the female organ) descends and does not close, it is caused by the exertion during labor and movements related to sexual activity. There may also be symptoms like prolapse of the anus and bulging of the yin causing pain and swelling, with clear fluid discharging and urination being dribbly.

    Note: If the yin does not close, it indicates weak Qi and blood; use the Shiquan Dabu Tang (Ten Complete Great Tonifying Decoction). For swelling and painful burning due to deficient liver heat, add modifications to the Xiaoyao San (Free and Easy Wanderer Powder). If caused by worry and anger leading to injury to the liver and spleen's Qi and blood, use modified Gui Pi Tang (Restore the Spleen Decoction). If due to intense anger injuring the liver's fire and blood, use Longdan Xiagao Tang (Gentiana Decoction to Drain the Liver).

    According to "The Discussion of Three Causes," it's stated that women can experience prolapse of the yin after labor due to excessive straining during delivery, and this can lead to conditions resembling anal prolapse or the bulging of the yin causing pain and heaviness, especially when lifting heavy objects or during sexual activities, which can trigger these symptoms.[70p]
    Theory of Termination of Pregnancy

    The Book of Changes states: “The greatest virtue of heaven and earth is life.” However, women sometimes face difficulties during childbirth, and may desire to terminate the pregnancy if complications arise. Therefore, remedies have been recorded for such purposes. If one uses mercury, worms, or leeches, not only will conception not recur, but also disaster will be imminent.

    [Note: It was previously mentioned to burn silkworm waste into a powder and take it with wine after childbirth. For those with blood deficiency, they will never conceive again. In general, most remedies used for termination are often drastic and can lead to failure, indicating that the harm of attempting termination is greater than the risks associated with childbirth.]

    Pulse Method

    The pulse of a woman after giving birth is slow and smooth, a sign of auspiciousness. If the pulse is strong and rapid, it signifies imminent death.

    [Note: Generally, if the pulse after childbirth is slow and smooth, it indicates balanced and harmonious qi and blood, which is a favorable omen. Conversely, if a strong and rapid pulse is detected, it is a dire sign and indicates that death is likely.]

    If the pulse is heavy and weak, it is auspicious; if it suddenly feels hard and firm, then life cannot be sustained.

    [Note: If a postpartum woman is diagnosed with a heavy and weak pulse, it reflects a harmonious correspondence of the physical state, signifying an auspicious omen. If a sudden hard and firm pulse is found, it implies a strong pulse coupled with a weak state, meaning life cannot be preserved, and death is certain.]

    A pulse at the cun position that is thin and irregular indicates death.[71p]
    Notes: If the pulse felt at the wrist after childbirth is thin, rapid, and irregular, this indicates a severe deficiency of blood and vital energy; hence it is called "death."

    If pressing down firmly results in a pulse that is deep, thin, and attached to the bone, persistent and strong, this is a sign of life.

    One should carefully observe and clearly remember these signs, and always reflect on them in the heart and mind.

    It is advisable for every physician to thoroughly examine the pulse and keep a clear record in their heart and mind.

    (Note: This differs from what is stated in previous discussions and "Pulse Diagnosis Errors"; observers should verify this.)

    Discussion on Postpartum Use of Heishen Powder and Chuanxiong Decoction

    After a mother gives birth, it is customary to take Heishen powder and Chuanxiong decoction to help expel stagnant blood and promote the generation of new blood. If the lochia does not fully pass and remains in the uterine channels, various complications may arise. In mild cases, there may be distension, pain, cold, or heat; in severe instances, menstrual flow may be irregular, blocked, or obstructed; prolonged stagnation can lead to blood masses, resulting in weakness and emaciation.[72p]
    Abdominal Pain and Diarrhea

    Postpartum abdominal pain and diarrhea are caused by the loss of blood and energy during childbirth, leading to a deficiency in both the body and the intestines. If it has not been a month since delivery and there is exposure to cold and wind, pathogens may take advantage of this vulnerability, settling between the flesh and spreading within the intestines and stomach. This results in pain in the abdomen and flanks, sharp as a knife, with symptoms like rumbling intestines and persistent diarrhea, producing red and white stools. In such cases, it is advisable to take the regulating soup for the middle. Additionally, some may consume meat too early or eat excessively, causing stagnation and undigested food, resulting in pain in the navel and abdomen, leading to diarrhea. It is indeed true that treating stagnation will lead to recovery; however, one should avoid using harsh purgatives like Qian Niu and Ba Dou, as they weaken the blood and energy. It is best to see a doctor. If this is not immediately manageable, one can utilize the formula for the regulating soup with added sand ginger for decoction.

    Blood Collapse

    For a new mother experiencing excessive bleeding, there is a sudden loss of blood and energy, inhibiting recovery. This may occur due to labor or due to shock or anger, resulting in a drastic blood loss. Additionally, both the Ying and Wei energies may be impaired, leading to a weakened state of blood. If there is fullness and pain in the lower abdomen, this indicates damage to the liver meridian and is difficult to treat; in this case, one should utilize tonics to stop the bleeding. If the lower abdomen is distended and swollen, this suggests the presence of stagnant blood, and it should not be stopped. Stopping it would not only fail to resolve the persistent bleeding but would also increase tension in the abdomen. For fullness in the lower abdomen, one should take Xiong Huai soup and He Long Dan. If the lower abdomen is not full and there is urgency, it indicates that there is no stagnant blood, and one can take tonics to stop the bleeding. For those who react poorly to heat medications, taking Shi Huai Yuan is also acceptable.[73p]
    Bloating, fullness, and vomiting

    The stomach receives water and grain; the spleen governs the transformation and transportation of these substances, producing blood and vital energy, and nourishing the internal organs. Due to postpartum insufficiency of the internal organs, with little lochia and stagnant blood taking advantage of the deficiency, it disperses to the spleen and stomach. When the spleen is affected, it results in abdominal bloating; when the stomach is affected, it leads to vomiting. There may also be excessive lochia with energy being ungoverned, accumulating in the spleen and stomach. The result is the same: abdominal bloating and vomiting; therefore, one should use the Zhi Sheng Decoction. However, when treating excessive lochia, remove the herbs Zexlan and Chishao from the Zhi Sheng Decoction, and increase the amounts of ginger and tangerine peel for better effect.

    Stroke

    Postpartum stroke may present as body weakness or rigidity, numbness, misalignment of the mouth and eyes, or sudden faintness and confusion of the spirit, which are signs of a stroke; one should take the Xiao Xu Ming Decoction.

    Chest Pain

    The heart rules the blood. If someone has lingering cold that obstructs the blood due to postpartum extreme deficiency, the blood cannot disperse, and its energy rushes towards the heart’s vessels, resulting in chest pain. This should be treated with the Da Yan Mi Decoction; once the cold is removed, the blood vessels warm up, and the meridians open, alleviating the pain. If one mistakenly treats the perceived injury, then the deficiency and cold will intensify. If the cold greatly affects the pericardium and transmits to the heart’s main channels, it can transform into true heart pain, which may lead to sudden death in the morning or evening. If the pain results from emotional distress, accompanied by both blood and qi, one should take the Yan Hu Suo Decoction to stop the pain.[74p]
    General Discussion on Diagnosis and Treatment

    After childbirth, if menstruation is interrupted and there are symptoms of different conditions, such as limb twitching, clenched jaws, and dizziness, it is advisable to adjust the Chai Hu decoction. Once the initial symptoms have resolved, the next step is to take Qin Jiao decoction to dispel wind pathogens.

    If after childbirth, there are wind conditions on the surface, leading to swelling of the face and limbs, it is appropriate to use the Qi Sheng pills for treatment, adjusting based on the severity. If there is swelling accompanied by coughing and shortness of breath, add Mu Xiang and Bing Lang for a stronger effect, indicating excess qi. If there’s swelling with dizziness, add Qiang Huo and Chuan Xiong, indicating excessive wind.

    If there is prolonged weakness and fatigue after childbirth, and the pulse is floating and rapid even after some time, it is advisable to take San Yuan decoction. For prolonged weakness with mild chills and fever, with a sinking but floating pulse, Chai Hu Si Wu decoction should be used. If prolonged weakness and fatigue do not respond to acupuncture or small medicines, three-part powder should be taken. If prolonged weakness leads to inability to eat, Shen Quan powder is recommended.

    For various accumulations after childbirth, they should not be aggressively treated; nourishing yin and reducing heat will allow the condition to improve. It is appropriate to take Shao Yao decoction.

    For postpartum abdominal distension with a sensation of obstruction in the chest, indicating an inability to pass gas, Zi Jin Dan should be used.

    For postpartum headaches caused by blood deficiency, phlegm, cold, or spasms, the Four Substance decoction should be adjusted. If sweating occurs, it indicates a weak qi headache; in this case, add three taels of Shao Yao, one and a half taels of Gui, and ginger decocted together. For headache due to phlegm, add three taels of Ban Xia and one and a half taels of Fu Ling, also decocted with ginger. For heat-induced headaches, additionally include three taels of Bai Zhi, three taels of Shi Gao, and one and a half taels of Zhi Mu.[75p]
    For cold-induced headaches, add three taels of Tianma and one and a half taels of Fuzi, boiled with fresh ginger.

    For postpartum blood circulation issues, where blood coagulates in the chest or shifts to the lower abdomen, or connects to the sides, use four taels of Siwu Decoction, double the amount of Donggui and Chuan Shao, plus one tael each of Guijian, Honghua, and Yanhua, all ground into a powder. Boil to extract the liquid and mix with myrrh to be taken as a powder.

    For various ailments after childbirth, simply take the Shuangjie powder for relief. This will promote the circulation of qi and blood throughout the body, alleviating all diseases. However, Shuangjie is a miraculous remedy that should not be given to pregnant women or those with excessive postpartum bleeding and diarrhea.

    It is commonly unknown that postpartum loss of fluids damages blood, leading to pain, fear, and chaotic emotions, which weaken the yin energy and allow excess heat to cause various heat syndromes. Failure to study the "Simple Questions" and recognizing the natural order results in misunderstanding symptoms of yin and yang, leading to the mistaken belief that postpartum conditions are purely deficiencies leading to cold without heat. As a result, hot medicines for warming and tonifying may be incorrectly administered, or if patients are thirsty, they might be forbidden to drink water. Although this may come from good intentions, it causes more harm than good and is not the way to treat illness! The proper approach is to thoroughly assess the internal organs, the six qi states of deficiency and excess, and clarify the principal and secondary conditions, then treat them accordingly.

    Pregnant women nearing delivery may take Yiyuan Powder to cool the fetus. After childbirth, continue with this. If the bleeding does not stop, combine Liangge with Siwu Decoction for boiling to regulate the menstrual flow. In severe cases, use Da Chengqi with Siwu for both purging and tonifying effects.[76p]
    Theory of Fetal Delivery

    When a woman is still young and her heavenly essence has not yet manifested, she is under the influence of the Shaoyin. Once the heavenly essence manifests, it aligns with the Jueyin in terms of discussion. After the heavenly essence ceases, it becomes associated with the Taiyin channel. Treating illnesses related to pregnancy and delivery that derive from the Jueyin channel connects to the source of ancestral formation. Jueyin and Shaoyang complement each other, thus the treatment method must avoid harming the stomach qi and the two upper burners, adhering to three prohibitions: no sweating, no purging, and no promoting urination. Inducing sweating can harm those with early-stage cold damage, promoting bowel movements results merely in a rapid pulse due to spleen involvement, and promoting urination leads to internal depletion of fluids and dryness in the stomach. In terms of medicinal preparation, one must not violate these three prohibitions; thus, the nourishment and defense harmonize, and when they are in harmony, cold and heat will cease. Externally, harmonize with the nourishment and defense; internally, regulate the cleansing and excretion of waste. This method should first be employed as an initial treatment, then further elaborated upon; by observing the evidence of case studies, we treat conditions similar to cold damage with caution.[77p]
    Some may experience difficulty in urination, or constipation, or excessive heat in the stomach and intestines, or even develop fistulas, or the dispersal of blood and Qi leading to swelling. This reflects the complexity of postpartum issues. Hence, it resembles the complications of typhoid fever. For instance, when there is thirst, one may use Bai Hu decoction; if there is weak Qi, Huang Qi is recommended; for stabbing pains in the blood, Dang Gui is appropriate; and for abdominal pain, Shao Yao should be added. The examples given do not violate the three prohibitions and pertain to prolonged postpartum conditions. However, if one suffers from acute illnesses postpartum and violates prohibitions, one should not be overly restrictive. For instance, if postpartum heat enters the blood chamber, treatments like Tao Ren Cheng Qi or Di Dang Tang can be used. If the stomach is hardened and dry, Da Cheng Qi should be used without considering it merely a purgative. Generally, many in the postpartum period excessively utilize Wu Jin Si Wu without understanding the seasonal variations of heat and cold, or failing to discern between deficiency and excess of blood and Qi. Using medicines blindly in such cases may aggravate the condition, leading to errors by practitioners. Overall, postpartum illnesses vary according to the fluctuations of Chai Hu, while complex conditions may require the addition of Si Wu.[78p]
    In spring and summer, one should use Chai Hu; in autumn and winter, one should use the Four Substances. The properties of the medicines, whether cold or hot, and the conditions of diseases, whether deficient or excess, must not be overlooked. The Four Substances Decoction is often taken for various ailments, with each season having its specific increases and decreases, which I will outline here:

    In spring, increase Chuan Xiong; for spring, the pulse is string-like, and the main symptom is headaches. In summer, increase Shao Yao; for summer, the pulse is surging, and the main symptom is diarrhea. In autumn, add Di Huang; for autumn, the pulse is thin, and the main symptom is blood deficiency. In winter, increase Dang Gui; for winter, the pulse is deep, and the main symptom is coldness causing a lack of appetite.

    Those who regularly take these in harmony with the seasonal气 (qi) and have persistent symptoms are said to have lost their aid. In spring, prevent wind with the Four Substances plus Chai Hu and double the amount of Chuan Xiong; in summer, use Huang Qin, adding it to the Four Substances and doubling Shao Yao; in autumn, add Tian Men Dong, combining it with Di Huang; and in winter, use Gui Zhi, combining it with the Four Substances and doubling Dang Gui. This is to be used according to the specific conditions each season.[79p]
    If there is blood deficiency accompanied by abdominal pain, slight sweating, and aversion to wind, use Four Substances plus Cinnamon, referred to as "Abdominal Pain of the Six Harmonies." If there is wind deficiency causing dizziness, add Qin Jiao and Qiang Huo, known as "Wind of the Six Harmonies." If there is qi deficiency with weakness upon rising, leading to faintness and collapse, add Hou Po and Chen Pi, termed "Qi of the Six Harmonies." If there is fever and restlessness resulting in inability to lie down, add Huang Lian and Zhi Zi, called "Heat of the Six Harmonies." If there is deficiency cold with weak pulses, difficulty in breathing, no thirst, and normal bowel movements, add Gan Jiang and Fu Zi, known as "Cold of the Six Harmonies." If there is dampness with heaviness, weakness, slight coolness, and light sweating, add Bai Zhu and Fu Ling, termed "Dampness of the Six Harmonies." These are remedies commonly used for women’s ailments and postpartum conditions. To treat women’s deficiency and fatigue, as stated in "Jufu Fang," the primary and secondary of the six harmonies, such as Da Sheng San or熟干地黄丸, is used for non-fever deficiency, focusing on nourishment, and is a middle-path remedy. Mu Dan Jia Wan should be taken on an empty stomach before meals, while Ren Shen and Jing Jie San should be taken after meals before bed, as these are remedies for fever-related deficiency. This text is found in "Complete Collection of Medical Works," Volume 391.

    Postpartum Matters for Women
    Confucian Etiquette Regarding Parents 【Yuan Dynasty, Zhang Congzheng】

    Postpartum, it is advised to avoid the use of warm and hot medicines.[80p]
    After a woman gives birth and suffers lingering ailments, they are all due to bad blood and harmful substances, manifesting as chills and fevers, painful cramps in the abdomen and around the navel, and a drying up of breast milk, with a slight decrease in appetite. If a physician fails to notice this and rashly claims that postpartum bleeding amounts to several buckets, leading to both qi and blood deficiency, they will then prescribe warming and nourishing remedies to replenish blood and treat cold. This is common practice everywhere. Little do they know that a woman's pregnancy is akin to the creation of life by heaven and earth! Just as things arise from the harmonious interplay of yin and yang, so too does a person come into being through the balance of these forces. How can there be conception without an appropriate balance of yin and yang? Is this not similar to grains and fruits? If there is drought or excessive rain at the wrong times, both flowers and fruits will fail. What is the difference from those who conceive yet fail to bear fruit? On the eighteenth day after the beginning of autumn in July, grassy plants that do not yield are due to sudden cold from the heavens. Now, if a woman is pregnant and successfully gives birth without difficulties after ten months, and yet it is said to be cold—why do they not understand the profound reasoning behind this?

    I liken this to the making of bricks: when the fire is beneath and water is poured over the top of the kiln, bricks are formed. Once the bricks emerge from the kiln, does the kiln become suddenly cold? Society eagerly promotes the black spirit powder and similar remedies for treating the eighteen postpartum ailments. Not only do these not heal, but they also lead to the drying and closing of channels, resulting in blockage both before and after, accompanied by vomiting, coughs, and sputum, producing various febrile conditions. The deaths resulting from such mistakes are countless. Why not prescribe the Four Substance Decoction and the Cool Diaphoresis to tackle the fundamental issues? By facilitating the flow, all harmful substances will be eliminated, and later taking mild and sweet remedies will lead to self-healing.

    After a significant birth, if the heart fire has not yet diminished and the kidney water has not yet risen, supplementing with black spirit powder could lead to danger in mild cases and death in severe ones.[81p]
    Postpartum Distension and Inappropriate Use of Emergency Pills

    Emergency pills, made from the ingredients of Castor bean, dried ginger, and rhubarb, mixed with honey into pills, are laxatives. However, they should only be administered to those who are engaged in demanding labor with coarse and spicy diets, or for symptoms like abdominal fullness, pain in the sides, and persistent sharp pain. Taking five to seven pills or ten can induce a strong purge for urgent relief. However, using them for postpartum distension may lead to severe complications, if not death.

    Umbilical and Lower Back Pain

    After childbirth, women may experience pain in the umbilical area and lower back, caused by the stagnation of loosed blood and harmful substances. Physicians who diagnose it as a deficiency and treat it with drying and warming medicines have long been mistaken. The "Nanjing" states that all pains are real. A real condition is associated with heat, so one may use Water-Guide Pills or Yugong Powder to induce three to five purges, followed by Yuzhu Powder to invigorate blood flow and regulate menstruation, with medicines to reduce heat and increase water. It's crucial not to recklessly use the heating Black Spirit Powder, as the treatment should resemble that of postpartum conditions.

    Heart Wind

    For women experiencing heart wind postpartum, one should not hastily treat it as a wind condition. Instead, it is advisable to prepare a stomach-regulating Chengqi Decoction of two taels, adding half a tael of Angelica, finely ground, and boil it with three to four cups of water, straining out the residue, and dividing it into two doses. A strong purge of three to five actions will lead to recovery. If there is no improvement, use San Sheng Powder to induce vomiting. Indeed, a wind condition leads to hyperactivity and is associated with yang.[82p]
    Postpartum Heat

    A woman’s body gradually becomes hot and her mouth dry one or two days after giving birth. One can use freshly drawn water to mix with the Jade Candle Powder, or sweet dew powder mixed with water, which is also effective. Do not treat it as a deficiency of cold.

    One or two days after childbirth, if there is tidal heat and dry mouth, fresh drawn water can be mixed with Jade Dew Powder, or it can also be taken with ice water. Alternatively, taking a Xiao Chai Hu Decoction with Angelica, or Chai Hu Yi Zi is also acceptable. One must not treat it as a deficiency of cold.

    On the Misuse of Cold and Cool Medicines

    After childbirth or during postpartum discharge, women may experience faintness and unawareness, resembling a state of insensibility. This is likely due to a rapid loss of blood; when tangible blood is lost, the heart and spirit have no sustenance. The heart and pericardium, which govern the fire within, are calm when nourished with blood. Without blood, there is peril. When the fire is intense, it leads to unconsciousness. The fire overwhelms the lungs, causing faintness and loss of awareness; this indicates a rapid loss of yin blood that cannot soothe and stabilize.

    With blood already deficient, using substances such as talcum, licorice, or gypsum—which are spicy and sweet yet very cold—will cause a depletion of the heat in the body, exacerbating the loss of blood and leading to both yin and yang suffering, resulting in a condition of insufficiency and exhaustion. In cases of unconsciousness, it indicates heat in the upper burner (heart and lungs), which, being an intangible heat, requires cold and cool medicines to be directed downwards.[83p]
    Do you not know that the illness of the upper jiao is all related to the exterior and is thus a yin condition; sweating it out will lead to recovery? Now, by countering it downward, if fortunate not to die, you will severely deplete qi and blood—how can life persist? Furthermore, it is noted in the "Inner Canon" that when disease qi is insufficient, one should tonify rather than purge. As for eye conditions, they are all related to yin and should be treated with sweating rather than purging. Additionally, it is known that for conditions like fever and exterior pathogens, when sweating occurs, recovery follows; thus, cold and cooling medicines should be avoided. During childbirth, if the fundamental qi is not affected, but blood is violently lost, to recover blood while also losing blood—what doubt can there be? If you replenish the blood, the spirit will thrive. When blood has normally fallen or been lost, now it should be replenished and raised; when the heart has blood and nourishes, the spirit will not be muddled. If blood descends violently, it indicates the seasons of autumn and winter overly strong, so now by raising and enhancing it to support yang, the eyes will brighten and the spirit will not be confused. Now, a full living blood soup is established to tonify blood, nourish blood, and generate blood to aid the deficiency of the hand and foot jueyin.[84p]
    Edema

    Postpartum edema should be treated with Zhongman Fenxiao Pill or Siwu Decoction.

    General Discussion

    Postpartum conditions should not be allowed to become vacuous; it is essential to first greatly tonify the qi and blood. Although there may be miscellaneous symptoms, they should be treated as secondary issues. Most illnesses stem from blood deficiency, and none should be treated by dispersing. Postpartum, it is inadvisable to use peony as it is sour and cold, hindering the vital energy of growth. After childbirth, blood circulation is affected by deficient fire causing blood to rise; only then can burnt deer antler be ground into a fine powder and mixed with good wine and children's urine to take, which will promptly invigorate the blood flow. For postpartum wind stroke, one must not treat it as wind but focus first on greatly tonifying the qi and blood, then treat phlegm. Treatment should be based on the pulse in both hands, determining the amounts of qi and blood for appropriate treatment. In postpartum wind stroke with slanted mouth and eyes, one must not take the Minor Renewing Life Decoction. For postpartum water retention, it is essential to primarily use strong tonification of qi and blood, with a minor addition of herbs like Cangzhu and Fuling to facilitate the discharge of water. In cases of significant edema, high doses of Baizhu should be used to tonify the spleen; if there is fullness and obstruction, Banxia, Chenpi, and Xiangfu may be used to monitor it. For postpartum high fever, one should definitely use dried ginger; in lighter cases, use Fuling to gently drain the heat. Any bitter-cold and dispersing medications should not be used. Postpartum fever and aversion to cold are both due to blood deficiency.[85p]
    After childbirth, if there is fever, obstruction or swelling of breast milk, one must address these issues if there are no children. Use two taels of wheat bran, toasted and ground into a fine powder, mixed with clear broth and taken in four doses. For those with children, use a decoction of Mume fruit, Tongcao, and pig trotters. In all cases of postpartum illness, one must first stabilize and support the vital energy.

    The previous section states that for postpartum high fever, dry ginger must be used. Someone asks: Why ginger? The answer is that this heat is not excess heat, but rather internal heat arising from yin deficiency. Therefore, one should take large doses of yin tonics. Moreover, dry ginger can enter the lungs to harmonize lung qi and is capable of guiding blood tonic herbs to generate blood. However, it must not be used alone but in conjunction with yin tonics. This is a natural principle of creation; who but the most transcendent can compare with this?

    For postpartum diarrhea, if lochia does not flow, it involves residual blood seeping into the large intestine causing diarrhea, with passages that are unrestrained, and with output being blue, white, or black. Take four to five spikes of Jiang Jie, burn them to ash in a cup, avoiding contact with oil or flames, then mix with ground musk and broth. Although this medicine may seem slight, it can treat serious illness; the formula is named "Miraculous Powder."

    For women postpartum with edema, scant urination, thirst, chills, and weakness, with a pulse that is deep, this indicates bodily weakness with accumulation of damp-heat, which must lead to fullness and oppression in the upper burner. It is advisable to tonify the center and guide water and regulate qi.[86p]

    General Discussion

    After childbirth, one should clear heat and supplement blood and qi.
    If there is a rise in body temperature post childbirth, one must not induce sweating. Fever with chills indicates a deficiency of qi and blood. If the left pulse is weak, one should use more blood tonics than qi tonics. If the right pulse is weak, one should use more qi tonics than blood tonics. For chills, fever, and abdominal pain, one should expel harmful blood. If there is abdominal fullness, it is not the issue; if there is abdominal pain, it is.

    Discussion on Bladder Injury

    It is often seen that injuries during childbirth can lead to bladder damage, resulting in a drip disease, which thus becomes a chronic ailment. One day, there was a middle-aged woman with the surname Xu who suffered from this condition. She thought that muscle injuries could be healed externally, and even though the bladder is in the abdomen, it might still be treatable. Upon diagnosis, her pulse was very weak, indicating that the cause of difficult labor is often qi deficiency. After difficult labor, both blood and qi are especially deficient. I tested a strong supplementation approach. I used ginseng and atractylodes as the main ingredients, angelica and chuanxiong as the assistants, along with peach kernel, dried tangerine peel, astragalus, and poria as the aides, simmered in a broth of pig and sheep bladders, to be consumed when extremely hungry. The dosage was generally one tael, and after a month, she was stable. This indicates that qi and blood can flourish rapidly, and the bladder can regain its form; although the process may be slightly delayed, success is still achievable.[87p]
    On the Prohibition of the Black God Powder and Five Accumulations for First-Time Mothers

    Some say: For women who have just given birth, the good blood has surely diminished, and stagnant blood still remains; is the Black God Powder not a necessary medicine? I say: How profound is the essence of Earth, from which all things are birthed; this is its natural order. A first-time mother may not necessarily have diminished blood or accumulated filth, nor must her organs be cold. Why rely on medicine? With diligent care in diet and daily living, what illness can arise?

    If there is indeed stagnant blood, accompanied by a timid and cold body, a few simple treatments would suffice. If there are other ailments, one must seek the root cause of the illness, identify the afflicted meridian, treat qi with qi, blood with blood; warm the cold, clear the heat, promote circulation for stagnation, replenish for deficiency, stop excessive blood if there is too much. Why impose unnecessary formulas, causing illness to arise where there was none?

    As for the Black God Powder, it uses the warmth of dried ginger and Angelica, the sweet and mature black beans, and the slightly cold rehmannia to replenish the blood's deficiency; combined with the bitter fried cat-tail pollen to prevent excessive bleeding; the sour and cold peony root both gathers and disperses, aiding the four medicinal components. The strong spicy heat of cinnamon promotes the flow of stagnant qi and resolves coagulated blood, while sweet licorice harmonizes it all, making its application appear quite meticulous.

    However, the approach of expelling and nourishing seems difficult to apply in the same prescription. For those who are impatient, naturally thin, harboring much anger, or sitting during the hot summer months—these times belong to the fire element; ginger and cinnamon would thus be forbidden medicines. If the principles of the Analects are not adhered to, who can be blamed?

    Regarding methods of nurturing, it is particularly illogical.[88p]
    The essence of meat juice ignites the internal fire, easily leading to internal injuries. The ancient sages provided warnings—why then consume rich sauces made from lamb and chicken, while regularly taking angelica pills, Angelica Jiazhong decoction, and Sishun Lizhong pills? Though these are nourishing, they all include warming ingredients like cinnamon, prepared aconite, and dried ginger that induce heat. If the organs have no cold, where will the excess heat be handled?

    As for a newborn, when the mother's abdomen expands, one might eat eggs and consume hot salt, without realizing that the eggs are difficult to digest and the hot salt generates heat, eventually leading to illness. If doctors don’t recognize this and mistakenly prescribe medication based on other symptoms, isn't that a mistake? Whenever I encounter a healthy postpartum woman, I always advise her to avoid black medicinal powders and all kinds of meat, in addition to guiding her to consume bland rice porridge interspersed with a bit of white goby fish, boiled to be mild and gentle. After about half a month, she may then have a little meat. Eggs should also be opened and boiled lightly to better nourish the stomach and prevent illness.[89p]
    In a family of wealth and nobility, a proud and indulgent woman eventually suffers from symptoms such as a white discharge, wind in the head, pain in the chest, full feeling, phlegm rising and dry mouth, irregular menstruation, hair loss, and body heat—all indications of excess yang and deficient yin. The natural blood and qi are originally in harmony; if there is an excess or a deficiency, how can we not consider whether these are delusions arising from such imbalances?

    For the treatment of postpartum pain from retained blood using the formula "Wujisan" (Five Accumulations Powder), the chief herb is Atractylodes, and the minister herb is Ephedra, with Magnolia and Fructus Aurantii as assistants. Although there are herbs like Peony and Angelica for replenishing blood, they only account for one-third the strength of Atractylodes. Furthermore, the formula addresses women with imbalances in blood and qi, as well as pain in the heart and abdomen—conditions that are stifled and stagnant, which also necessitate its use. Why do we not think about what cold a postpartum woman might have? With blood and qi insufficiently nourished, it seems hard to induce sweating. Saying it promotes the old to bring forth the new, the herbs used are gentle and warming; how can one recklessly apply a formula with Ephedra, along with Atractylodes and Fructus Aurantii? When one is already deficient, rushing to use these herbs leads to disaster, yet they hastily rely on remedies without deep consideration.

    Essentials of Diagnosis and Treatment 【Ming Dynasty, Dai Sigong】

    Skin Itchiness

    A woman may experience itching all over her body or on her face, like insects crawling under the skin—this occurs during menstruation due to wind exposure; otherwise, it may be caused by ingesting food that has wind properties during the postpartum period. It is advisable to use the "Siwu Decoction" (Four Substance Decoction) to harmonize and disperse the wind.[90p]

    Fever

    After childbirth, a woman may experience various ailments, including chills and fever, but sometimes only fever occurs. There are three types of isolated fever: if there is retained blood, one will experience abdominal pain and fever; if an external pathogen is involved, there will likely be headache and aversion to wind accompanying the fever; in cases of blood deficiency, there is merely fever without other symptoms, known as "ru lao," and treatment should be similar to that for blood deficiency.

    Abdominal Pain

    Postpartum abdominal pain with persistent retained blood that does not respond to various medicines should be treated with Xiong Gui Decoction, adding five flavors, Ling Zhi, and Yan Hu Suo for decoction.

    Dark Face with Wheezing

    If blood enters the lungs postpartum, resulting in a dark complexion and wheezing, one should use Shen Su Decoction.

    Constipation

    After childbirth, if there is a loss of blood, and constipation occurs where the stool is not moist, one should use Ju Xing Yuan or Ma Ren Yuan, as constipation due to blood loss can be treated with these.

    Fever with Dizziness

    If postpartum fever is accompanied by dizziness, commonly referred to as a warming of the blood, one could use hot water to prepare a decoction of Du Xing San at one coin weight; or take Dou Lin liquor. If it is too warm, using children's urine for dilution is especially appropriate. If necessary, a crushed powder can also be decocted.

    Retained Lochia

    When a woman becomes pregnant and takes too many herbs to stabilize the fetus, whether in full or partial labor, even if the fetus is delivered, there may be retained blood that does not immediately clear. If lochia continues to drip after two or three months, this is not comparable to putrid blood, but is due to the presence of stabilizing herbs, which cause blood stasis that does not resolve. Therefore, one should promote blood flow and facilitate qi, avoiding the accumulation of blood and stagnation of qi.[91p]

    Injury from Excessive Eating

    In the case of excessive eating, commonly known as a full stomach, one may suffer from heat due to overeating, resembling the symptoms of food-related fever. It is advisable to first take one or two doses of red round pills, then proceed with Xiao Chai Hu Decoction. This discussion is based on established principles.
    — Medical Guidelines, Ming Dynasty, Lou Ying

    Constipation

    After childbirth, women may experience three types of illness. If there is stagnation, it often leads to excessive sweating, and with excessive sweating, constipation occurs, making it difficult to administer medicine. In such cases, a congee made with sesame seeds is the most reliable remedy.

    Edema

    Postpartum edema with shortness of breath can be alleviated by facilitating urination; this condition is treated with Duohun San.

    Retained Lochia

    If the postpartum lochia diminishes suddenly and comes to a halt, accompanied by heavy pain in the abdomen, this signifies blood stasis. In this circumstance, Tao Ren Tang should be used. If there is severe pain, it is likely to develop into an abscess, which should be treated according to the methods for abscesses.

    Profuse Sweating and Disorientation

    If postpartum sweating occurs suddenly, making one unable to recognize others, this is due to an acute deficiency.

    Malaria

    Postpartum malaria often arises from stagnant blood coupled with cold and heat. It is essential to treat it with Chai Hu Si Wu Tang. If there is more heat, a drink made with grass fruit should be administered; if there is more cold, a drink made with raw and cooked ingredients should be used.

    Fever

    Postpartum fever is generally due to deficiency and cold. Adding dried ginger to tonic medicines is particularly effective; this is a remarkable method from Danxi.

    Fever with Headache and Body Pain[92p]

    After giving birth, if there are symptoms of fever, headache, and body aches, one should not hastily treat it as a cold. These are often due to blood deficiency or stagnant blood. It is advisable to use gentle and harmonious remedies, which will certainly be effective. For example, one may use Jade Dew Powder or the Four Substances with added Northern Bupleurum, prepared and consumed in equal parts. If one hastily employs Xiao Chai Hu Decoction or other similar remedies, failure to remedy the situation is common.

    Xu Ming Decoction, Great Bean Purple Decoction, and Ju Xiang Gu Bai San are all medicines for the Taiyang and Jueyin. If the pulse is floating, wiry, and strong, it is certainly appropriate, but for those postpartum with excessively deficient blood and qi, one should not easily disperse the exterior. Instead, using Fang Feng and Dang Gui Powder is preferable.

    For postpartum stroke, employing Xu Ming Decoction along with Qiang Huo and dispersing medicines is essential. One must pay attention to the condition of qi and blood, and may use equal parts of Si Wu and Si Jun Zi to be taken in two doses.

    Regarding postpartum spasms, they are always caused by deficiency encountering wind and phlegm. Appropriate measures include consuming ginseng or bamboo sap and similar remedies.

    For women postpartum experiencing urinary incontinence, with a slightly puffy face and mild afternoon fever, this is due to bladder injury from the midwife. It is advisable to use Huang Qi, Dang Gui, and Shao Yao at one and a half qian each, with Bai Zhu at one qian, and Ginseng and Chen Pi at five fen, along with two fen of roasted Licorice, and brew to drink.

    For women experiencing shortness of breath postpartum, the situation is extremely urgent and often leads to death.

    — Medical Correct Transmission (Ming Dynasty, Yu Tuan)[93p]
    On the Prohibition of Using Peony

    When asked about the various ailments women experience after childbirth, ancient prescriptions often utilize the Siwu Decoction with adjustments for treatment. However, Mr. Danxi uniquely asserts that peonies are sour and cold, capable of undermining the qi necessary for recovery, and thus should be prohibited. Why is that? He explains: A newly delivered woman is exceedingly deficient in both blood and qi, akin to a state where heaven and earth do not interconnect, leading to descent without ascent. She retains only the forces of autumn and winter's cold and death, while the spring and summer's nourishing energies have not yet returned. Consequently, many postpartum conditions are adversely affected by cooling medicines and should instead favor warm and temperate remedies to support the essential processes of renewal and recovery. Indeed, our predecessors formulated the Siwu Decoction, combining the warmth of Chuanxiong and Donggui with the coolness of Baishao and Dihuang, thereby achieving an exquisite balance suitable for treating women’s ailments. If this is used postpartum, one must select white peony that has been specially processed with wine for re-frying, to eliminate its sour and cold toxicity while preserving its ability to invigorate and nourish the blood. Why then should it be unusable? As later generations transcribed these texts without preserving the processing notes, Mr. Danxi was concerned that common doctors, in their carelessness, might utilize it improperly, hence he highlighted the reasons for its potential harm as a caution. If one follows the prescribed method, what harm could arise?

    On Retained Placenta

    The retention of the placenta is a matter of great concern; various methods should be employed to expel it. It is extremely inadvisable to allow a rough and untrained woman to remove it. I have witnessed cases where a broken bladder led to lifelong issues, and instances where the detachment of liver lobes caused mothers to die shortly after childbirth. Should one not be cautious?
    [94p]
    Fever

    When a woman experiences blood deficiency after giving birth, the Yang has no support and therefore disperses outward, resulting in fever. The treatment method is to use the Four Substances Decoction to replenish Yin blood, and to add roasted ginger for its bitter warmth to treat this condition, gathering the dispersed energies back to the Yin. However, if there is a deficiency in the spleen and stomach post-delivery, often there are cases where the consumption of food exceeds and causes stagnation, leading to fever; in such cases, misjudging this as blood deficiency would be ineffective.

    When encountering fever after childbirth, if there are symptoms like chest fullness and discomfort, belching, aversion to food, or diarrhea, one should treat it as food injury. If the fever occurs but the appetite is self-regulated, then one should apply the method to nourish the blood.

    [Note: After childbirth, the Yin blood is severely injured, and without the support of Yang, there is external heat, hence it is advisable to use the Four Substances Decoction and roasted ginger to nourish Yin and support Yang. If the fever is due to mistakenly taking cold and damp medicines, causing external heat, it indicates that cold air is blocking the Yang outward; in this case, one should use the Four Gentlemen Decoction with ginger and cassia; if ineffective, immediately add aconite. If there is skin heat, a red face, and thirst prompting drinking, this indicates a loss of blood leading to restlessness; thus, it is advisable to use the Angelica Decoction to replenish the blood. If there’s chest fullness, discomfort, belching of foul food, or acidic vomiting and diarrhea causing fever, this is due to food stagnation; hence, use the Four Gentlemen Decoction with Magnolia bark and hawthorn. If there’s a sensation of fullness in the chest with little food leading to fever, or difficulty in digesting food, this indicates weak spleen Qi; in this situation, use the Six Gentlemen Decoction with roasted ginger. If harsh medications cause abdominal pain, fever, thirst, and vomiting, this indicates injury to the central Qi; immediately use the Six Gentlemen Decoction with roasted ginger. To regard it as heat and administer other medicines would be a mistake.][95p]
    Introduction to Medicine
    By Li Zhan (Ming Dynasty)

    On the Elimination of Stagnant Blood

    When a woman has given birth and is in good physical condition without illness, no medicine is necessary. However, if she experiences difficulty in labor leading to a weakening of the vital energy and stagnation of blood, medication becomes essential. According to ancient practices, immediately after childbirth, one should use "Guo Xuangui Decoction" — take half of it mixed with children's urine; if children's urine is not available, it is also effective to grind a small cup of light vinegar into the previous decoction.

    Postpartum ailments are mostly due to blood deficiency and excessive internal heat, resulting in stagnant blood moving erratically. Occasionally, there may be internal injuries from food or external influences of wind and cold, but the primary approach must always be to eliminate stagnation and replenish deficiency first.

    Once stagnant blood has been resolved, then one can proceed with tonification. If the left pulse is weak, add blood-enriching medicines; if the right pulse is weak, add qi-tonifying medicines. If stagnation is not cleared and one hastily uses ginseng or astragalus in excess, this could lead to fatal conditions caused by stagnant blood attacking the heart. Likewise, consuming meat too early is also problematic.[96p]

    Blood Circulation

    Excessive bleeding after childbirth, with symptoms of dizziness and headache, often leads to irritability and agitation. If there is sweating from the head, use Guo Qiong Gui Decoction with child urine; for severe cases, add roasted black dried ginger and ginseng, and if there is excessive sweating, add astragalus. Alternatively, use Eight Flavor Black Spirit Powder, Dan Wuling Zhi Powder, or Resurrection Pill. For those with weak stomachs and blood deficiency causing convulsions, use Cangong Powder or Baiwei Decoction. For exertion during labor, those with weak heart energy can use one ounce of ginseng and five qian of rosewood, decocted in water and mixed with child urine for consumption. If both qi and blood are deficient, leading to phlegm and fire rising upwards, use Eight Ingredients Decoction combined with Erchen Decoction, removing white peony. If fire causes blood to rise and results in dizziness or accompanies wind evil, use Qinghun Powder. For those who are scared, use Baodan Pill or Zhu Sha An Shen Pill.

    Heart and Abdominal Pain

    Postpartum pain in the heart and abdomen is due to stagnant blood. Use Eight Flavor Black Spirit Powder, Four Flavor Powder, or Shixiao Powder. For those with cold and heat, use Dang Gui Xu Powder. For food stagnation resulting in cold or heat-related heart and abdominal pain, use Shuliang Wujisan with curcuma.

    Heart Pain

    For heart pain due to cold after childbirth, use Lizhong Decoction. For heart pain caused by emotional distress, use Mubing Decoction. For pain due to deficiency and cold, use Guixin Decoction.

    Lower Abdominal Pain

    If there is pain in the lower abdomen after childbirth, termed as "child pillow pain," use Dan Wuling Zhi Powder, or add peach kernel, making it into a pill with vinegar. For qi deficiency, use Four Gentlemen Decoction; for blood deficiency, use Four Substances Decoction.[97p]
    Lower Abdominal Pain

    After childbirth, if there is a feeling of emptiness and pain below the navel, use a large warming decoction or mutton soup.

    Cold and Heat

    After childbirth, if there is a heat caused by blood deficiency; or if there is qi deficiency with aversion to cold; or if there is a combination of qi and blood deficiency, presenting with both heat and aversion to cold, one must not induce sweating. For those with weak blood, use the Four Substance Decoction; for mild heat, add poria as the king herb, and for severe heat, add fried dry ginger as an adjunct. In cases of excessive bleeding, external heat combined with internal restlessness, and shortness of breath, use the ginseng and angelica powder. For lactation-related heat, use the Four Substance Decoction with added ginseng, astragalus, white atractylodes, and tianhua fen (floyoychus root). If fever is mild during the day but severe at night, remove peony from the Four Substance Decoction and increase the dosage of bupleurum. For combined qi and blood deficiency with alternating cold and heat, use a nourishing decoction. Care should be taken to avoid anything cold after childbirth.

    Ru-lao (Excessive Labor)

    Excessive labor after childbirth is called ru-lao. Its symptoms include weakness, alternating between sitting and lying down, poor appetite, occasional coughing, head and eye pain, thirst, night sweats, and chills and fever similar to malaria, with rigidity in the arms. One should take the Complete Nourishing Decoction, removing the cyperus, adding dismodium, achyranthes, turtle shell, mulberry mistletoe, and peach kernel ground into powder. Use a pair of pig kidneys with fat membranes removed, one slice of ginger, three jujubes, and two cups of water, decocting until reduced to one cup. Then mix in two qian of the previously ground powder, three inches of green onion, half a dried black plum, and five sprigs of schizonepeta, boiling together in water, taken on an empty stomach. For body pain with alternating cold and heat, use the angelica and mutton soup or kidney soup.[98p]
    Coughing and abdominal pain or bloating

    For those who have postpartum food stagnation from eating meat too early, use Wu Ji San, adding Gao Er Zhu to alleviate severe pain, and for vomiting add Sha Ren; for diarrhea, use ginger, Fu, and Ren Shen. If diarrhea persists with umbilical pain, add Rou Dou Kou to Li Zhong Wan. If there is abdominal pain and intestinal gurgling with clear urine and no thirst, combine Si Jun Zi Tang with Wu Ling San, adding Rou Dou Kou and Chao Bai Shao. If there is heat with constipation, fluctuating pain and thirst, combine Si Jun Zi Tang with Si Ling San, adding a little wine-fried Huang Lian and Mu Tong, or use Yi Yuan San. If there is dampness with a heavy body and abdominal bloating, use Wei Ling Tang. If there is stagnant blood entering the stomach causing vomiting and distention, use Liu Jun Zi Tang with Ze Lan Ye, Chi Shao, and Gan Sheng Jiang. For abdominal bloating with disharmony of stomach Qi, take a decoction made from Jie Geng, Ban Xia, and Chen Pi in equal parts. If the spleen pulse is wiry, use San Bai Tang with Gan Jiang, Chen Pi, Huang Qi, Hua Shi, and Gan Cao. For food accumulation causing distension and pressure, use Nei Zhi San or Xian Xuan Wan.[99p]
    Cholera

    For postpartum cholera with vomiting and diarrhea, accompanied by thirst and cold limbs, administer a modified Lìzhōng Tāng with dried tangerine peel and Ophiopogon, decocted with ginger. For cold limbs, add Aconite; for thirst, use Wǔlìng Sǎn; for muscle cramps, use Mùyú Sǎn.

    Weakness

    For postpartum wind and weakness, with muscle spasms and lack of strength, administer Xuèfēng Wán or decoct it.

    Thirst

    For postpartum thirst due to qi deficiency, use Shēngmài Sǎn. If there is blood deficiency, use Sìwù Tāng with added Tiānhuāfěn and Ophiopogon. For both qi and blood deficiency causing thirst, dizziness, weakness in the legs, and tastelessness in food, use two qian of Ginseng, one and a half qian of Ophiopogon, seven fen of Rehmannia, three qian of Tiānhuāfěn, five fen of Licorice, with glutinous rice, ginger, and jujube decocted. It is also suitable for those with heart deficiency and palpitations.

    Spontaneous Sweating

    For postpartum fever and spontaneous sweating, use Gǔguīqí Tāng. If sweating is severe, add Atractylodes, Ledebouriella, Oyster, Ophiopogon, Rehmannia, Poria, and Licorice, or use Huángqí Jiànzhōng Tāng.
