If spontaneous sweating is accompanied by swelling and fullness, use Dàodiàojīng Sǎn. If spontaneous sweating is accompanied by body aches, use Dānguī Yángròu Tāng.

    Inability to Speak[100p]
    After childbirth, if there is stasis of lochia with blood retention affecting the heart, causing blocked heart qi and inability to speak, one can use the Seven Treasures Powder or the Four Flavor Powder. If there was an excess of herbal decoctions taken prior to delivery leading to gastric dampness, one should use the Shujia Wujisan or the Six Gentlemen Decoction. If phlegm-heat clouds the heart and inhibits speech, the Phlegm Resolving Decoction should be utilized. If phlegm-qi stagnates, causing a person to close their eyes and refrain from speaking, one can take one qian of raw alum powder mixed with water.

    For anxiety after childbirth, when blood volume is low and sleep is disturbed, the Ten Flavor Warm Gallbladder Decoction, or the Calming Spirit Ointment or the Settling Will Pill can be recommended.

    For postpartum urinary issues, the Bai Mao Decoction is indicated. If there is continuous dripping of lochia, the Wu Jin Powder should be used. If the urinary issue is persistent with weakness and heaviness in the limbs, the Oyster Powder is recommended.

    In cases of postpartum edema due to stasis of lochia and blood transforming into water that circulates into the limbs, the Small Regulating Channels Decoction should be employed. For those with qi and blood deficiency, the Four Gentlemen decoction with added Cang Zhu or the Women's Golden Elixir can be beneficial. If blood is deficient, the Replenishing Deficiency Decoction with slight additions of Cang Zhu and Fu Ling is advised to promote excretion of water. Avoid harsh purging agents.

    For postpartum dysentery where lochia has not fully expelled, due to accumulation and stagnation in the intestines and coinciding with blood stagnation after menstruation, the Four Substances Decoction with added Tao Ren, Huang Lian, and Mu Xiang should be administered. If there is severe urgency, the Tong Yuan Er Ba Dan may be used.

    In cases of cough after childbirth, often due to stagnant blood entering the lungs, the Ancient Er Mu Powder with additions of Tao Ren, Xing Ren, Ren Shen, and Fu Ling should be decocted with water. Any other conditions may be understood according to context.[101p]
    Black Nosebleed from Mouth and Nose

    After childbirth, the qi and blood become disordered, affecting the channels and unable to return to their original state, resulting in blackening of the mouth and nose, and ultimately causing nosebleeds. This is all due to the deficiency and heat following childbirth, leading to stomach exhaustion and lung failure. It can be treated with Rhino Horn and Rehmannia Decoction.

    Prolapsed Perineum

    When a woman exerts herself too much during childbirth, causing the perineum to prolapse, administer the Four Substance Decoction with a small amount of crushed Dragon Bone. This should be taken for two doses. Externally, apply a paste of hemp seeds to the area. Remove it once it has slightly adhered.

    Inability to Retain the Intestines

    In cases where there is an inability to retain the intestines after childbirth, use the Eight Substance Decoction with Fangfeng and Shengma, utilizing fried Astragalus as the main ingredient. Externally, wash with a decoction of Schizonepeta, Agastache, and Malanthus.

    Common Exterior Illness

    After childbirth, if one gets an exterior illness from getting out of bed too soon or changing clothes and exposing oneself to the cold, it may present with alternating chills and fever resembling malaria, and persistent headaches. For those with blood deficiency, use the Ancient Chuanxiong and Angelica Decoction with Ginseng, Perilla, and Dried Kudzu. For those with both qi and blood deficiency, use the Qi Supplementing Decoction with Chenpi and Dried Ginger. If the chills and fever are severe, apply the Cooked Wujisan. If the fever persists, Huanglong Decoction is indicated. If there is severe deficiency with fever and aversion to cold or symptoms of malarial dysentery, combine Chaihu Decoction with Four Gentlemen and Four Substance Decoction adding Astragalus, referred to as the Three-Part Powder, noting that treating it as a cold affliction is inappropriate. If there has been excessive use of warming herbs leading to significant heat symptoms, and prolonged constipation occurs, use Chaihu Break Stasis Decoction, or Four Substance Decoction with Rhubarb and Glauber's Salt for temporary relief, followed by tonification.[102p]
    Febrile Illness from Postpartum Cold Damage

    In the case of febrile illness following childbirth, it is advisable to use a modified Si Wu Decoction, increasing the amounts of Angelica sinensis (Dang Gui) and Chuanxiong, and adding softened Bupleurum (Chai Hu), roasted dried ginger, and ginseng as assistants. If the lochia has not cleared, use the Chai Hu Breaks Stasis Decoction. All medicines must incorporate Si Wu Decoction as the main component, for it is the source of nourishing blood. This is confirmed by the Six Meridians, using the same drugs as before the fetus.

    Methods for Treating Miscellaneous Diseases

    Miscellaneous diseases postpartum can occur similarly in women as in men, but one must simultaneously replenish and dispel stasis; thus, no illness remains untreated. Danxi stated: "Greatly nourishing the Wei and Ying is paramount; even with miscellaneous diseases, using them in minute quantities suffices."

    Methods for Nursing Mothers

    If a nursing mother feels that her milk supply is insufficient, it indicates the onset of illness, necessitating the use of medicine to regulate the spleen, stomach, liver, and kidneys. If not resolved, there must be Qi stagnation and reversal. Indeed, women should not act independently in all matters; excessive worry, reflection, and anger lead to Qi stagnation and blood stasis; excessive anger causes Qi and blood to reverse, which can lead to hard breasts, rib pain, and fever. All ailments in women stem from Qi and blood being congested; thus, ancient prescriptions often included ingredients like Cyperus Rhizome (Xiang Fu), Amomum (Sha Ren), Wood Aromatic (Mu Xiang), Areca Nut (Bing Lang), Green Peel (Qing Pi), and Bitter Orange Peel (Zhi Qiao) to promote Qi flow.[103p]
    Xue's Medical Case Notes
    (Ming Dynasty - Xue Ji)

    Postpartum Tetanus

    After childbirth, if tetanus occurs, greatly nourish the blood and Qi, and ensure safety. If one attacks the wind pathogen, death is certain.

    Cold and Heat

    Postpartum cold and heat arise from weakness in Qi and blood, or from deficiency in the spleen and stomach, indicating insufficiency. It is said: if Yin is deficient, heat arises; if Yang is deficient, cold is felt. If accompanied by constipation, it especially indicates weakness in Qi and blood, and one must not use methods that expel and reduce heat. If the pulse at the inch position is weak, this indicates insufficient Yang energy; if the Yin energy rises into the Yang and causes cold, use the "Bu Zhong Yi Qi Tang." If the pulse at the cubit position is weak, this indicates insufficient Yin energy; if the Yang energy descends into the Yin and causes heat, use the "Liu Wei Di Huang Wan." Generally, if Yin is deficient, Yang follows it, causing Yang to sink and produce heat; if Yang is deficient, Yin follows it, leading to Yin rising and causing cold. This imbalance of Yin and Yang results in the struggle between cold and heat, hence the feeling of cold accompanied by heat; in such cases, "Ba Zhen Tang" should be used. If after the illness the limbs are hot, or if the body feels weary, it signifies that the original Qi has not yet recovered, and damp heat has taken hold; "Bu Zhong Yi Qi Tang" is suitable. If there is significant heat, great thirst, desire to drink, and redness in the eyes and face, this indicates heat arising from blood deficiency, and "Dang Gui Bu Xue Tang" should be used, for thinking it is cold would be erroneous.[104p]
    Diarrhea and Dysentery

    Postpartum diarrhea may arise from dietary damage to the spleen or from spleen deficiency unable to digest food; treatment should be careful and appropriate.

    Job's Tears Decoction Discussion

    Abdominal pain with fever, or bloating with loss of appetite, and sluggish water channels often occur postpartum; Job’s tears decoction is gentle in its ingredients and quick in efficacy.

    Prolapse of the Rectum

    In women, excessive straining during childbirth often leads to prolapse of the rectum. Treatment must warm the lungs, the intestines, and the stomach, and with time, it will naturally resolve.

    Miscellaneous Conditions

    Women with strong attachments in character and inability to release emotions frequently suffer from the seven emotions, resulting in widespread pain, swelling in limbs and joints, chest fullness, or feelings like a plum pit obstructing the throat, causing difficulty in swallowing, or excessive salivation and phlegm leading to shortness of breath. Severe cases may present with nausea and intense thirst causing distress. Mothers often experience this condition; it is advisable to take a Qi-regulating decoction followed by blood-nourishing medications. If due to worry, leading to cloudy urination, this medicine should be taken with Qingzhou white pills for repeated effectiveness.[105p]
    Excessive Heat from Deficiency

    I suspect that the preceding symptoms indicate that the yang follows the yin and disperses, leading to both qi and blood deficiency. If there is a feeling of aversion to cold and a fever, alongside irritability and thirst, one should urgently use the Shi Quan Da Bu Tang (Ten Complete Great Tonifying Decoction). If the fever becomes more intense, promptly add gui (cinnamon) and fu (astragalus). If there is thirst and a flushed face, one should use the Dang Gui Bu Xue Tang (Angelica Tonifying Blood Decoction). If mistaken for a fire condition and treated with cooling agents, disaster is at hand. The great physician Wang Tai Pu states: when there is extreme cold yet no heat, it is a sign of no fire. When heat comes and goes, appearing by day and hiding by night, and shows no consistent heat, it indicates no fire and should be treated at the heart. When there is extreme heat yet no cold, it signifies no water. When heat stirs and then subsides, suddenly coming and going, showing inconsistent patterns, it indicates no water and should support the kidneys. Thus, an exuberant heart generates heat, while an exuberant kidney generates cold. A kidney deficiency leads to internal cold, while a heart deficiency results in internal heat. Furthermore, heat overcoming cold signifies no fire; cold overcoming heat indicates no water. Treatment methods: for cases without water, use Liu Wei Di Huang Wan (Six Flavor Rehmannia Pill); for cases without fire, use Ba Wei Wan (Eight Flavor Pill); for cases with both qi and blood deficiencies, use Ba Zhen Tang (Eight Treasure Decoction) and Shi Quan Da Bu Tang.[106p]
    Blood Thirst

    I suggest that if there is excessive blood loss accompanied by ascendant fire of deficiency, one should use the urine of a young boy, or prescriptions such as Siwu Decoction, Bai Zhu, Mai Men Dong, and Dan Pi. If there is weak stomach qi with heat, one should use Zhuye Guizhi Decoction. If there is blood deficiency leading to heat, one should use Ba Zhen Decoction supplemented with Mai Men Dong and Wu Wei Zi. If blood loss causes heat and agitation, one should use Danggui Bu Xue Decoction. If there is weakness of stomach qi, one may use Bu Zhong Yi Qi Decoction or Qiwei Baizhu San.

    Those with excessive blood are often ill. Symptoms include pain in the eyes making it difficult to see, sensitivity to light, dry eyes, weak eyelashes, and soreness in the brow and temples, for which one should administer Danggui Yang Lao Decoction, Danggui Bu Xue Decoction, Chufeng Yisong Decoction, or Ziyin Dihuang Pill as necessary. If there is heat, add Huang Qin. If there is disharmony in the spleen and stomach with nausea and loss of appetite, add fresh ginger. For women experiencing postpartum bleeding, add Ejiao to replenish blood and promote recovery. However, one must avoid salty foods. As stated in the "Treatise on the Five Qi," salt moves blood, and one should not consume excessive salty foods in cases of blood disorders.[107p]
    Principles of Diagnosis and Treatment
    By Wang Kengtang (Ming Dynasty)

    Regulation Methods

    For women suffering from coldness and emptiness below the navel after childbirth, this is due to the convergence of energies within a hundred days postpartum; one must be cautious! Within seven days after childbirth, if there is lingering lochia, do not take herbal decoctions until the blood clots have dispersed, then consume mutton soup. If there is severe pain, this does not apply. After two days, assess the condition and you may take Zexlan pills. It is best to continue until a full month with the medicine exhausted. Otherwise, deficiencies cannot be restored. For those extremely emaciated and beyond help, take five hundred Zexlan pills to replenish. The regimen must wait at least seven days; it is not advisable to begin too early.

    Emotional Distress

    Typically, for various forms of anxiety and heat, bamboo leaf and gypsum soup or Warm Gallbladder decoction are used. However, it is important to note that postpartum conditions differ; medications like gypsum should not be used lightly, as misuse can be fatal.[108p]
    Sudden Heat and Sudden Cold Distinctions

    After childbirth, sudden cold and sudden heat occur, with disharmony in the defense and nourishment, making it difficult to discuss lightly. If the stagnant blood is not treated with respect, could it not affect both the spleen and lungs? Generally, when one yin is closed and one yang is active, cold and heat arise: cold arises from an excess of yin, and heat arises from an excess of yang. It can be said that stagnant blood moves along the channels; if it closes off all the yin, it results in cold; if it closes off all the yang, it results in heat. When blood and vital energy are reconciled, there will be rest, but upon reencountering the condition, it may recur. The great regulation of channels and the Five Accumulations decoction with vinegar are beneficial.

    On Treating Beriberi with Xiao Xu Ming Decoction

    Chen Wuze said: Beriberi is indeed a common ailment, but I have not heard of it transforming after childbirth. Often, when reading the "Thousand Gold," one sees many postpartum women suffering from this disease, which is simply taken as evidence; the wording harms the intent, and it can be seen how misinterpretations arise. If this is due to heat and oppressive Qi rising, how can one take the Xiao Xu Ming decoction? This medicine primarily addresses wind in the Shaoyang channel and is not intended to treat beriberi across all channels. One must discuss it in relation to the yin and yang of the channels. Although Chen Wuze has this argument, modifications of Xiao Xu Ming Decoction yield effective results. Thus, "Hundred Questions" states: When cold is in the Three Yang, the affliction must be cold; the Xiao Xu Ming decoction addresses it, and adding ginger juice hastens recovery. When heat is in the Three Yin, the affliction must be hot; for this, the Xiao Xu Ming decoction must omit Aconite and halve the dosage of Cinnamon. For significant agitation, Zixue (Purple Snow) is the best. If Zixue is unavailable, use genuine mint boiled in cold water and chew it down. However, all prescriptions must be taken in equal parts with Si Wu Decoction for optimal effect.[109p]

    Constipation

    After childbirth, if there is no relief, it is said that only one in a hundred survives. Excessive blood loss and dryness in the organs lead to severe constipation, which should certainly be treated with smoothing remedies. While Da Huang (Rhubarb) seems difficult to use lightly, mixing scallion juice with La tea to form pills, and then taking it with La tea will surely provide relief. I often use the Four Herbs Decoction, substituting raw Dihuang for cooked Dihuang, and using Qingpi (Bitter Orange Peel) decocted and taken orally shows great effectiveness.

    Spasm

    Chen Linchuan states: In cases of postpartum mouth tightness and stiffness of the waist and back, and where there is arching of the back, it is called spasm, also known as convulsion. The ancients emphasized the need to check for sweating to distinguish hard from soft and balance yin and yang in treatment. Nowadays, books such as “Chan Bao” have sections on wind-related mouth tightness and arching, but they are essentially one and the same. If there is a dislike of cold and fever, similar to typhoid, it should not be treated lightly, nor can it be resolved with just one or two medicines. Linchuan further states: Chen Wuze said that excessive sweating after childbirth leads to spasms; thus, one should take the Continuation of Life Decoction. This statement is hard to believe. If there is much sweating, how can one further take Ma Huang, Guo Gui, Fang Ji, and Huang Qin? It would be better to use the Great Soy Sauce Decoction. The Great Sage Powder from the classic formulas is also a good medicine. I foolishly observe that Zhu Fengyi suggests both hard and soft spasms can be treated with the Small Continuation of Life Decoction, with adjustments applied as needed. If soft spasms occur with sweating, advice to remove Ma Huang and add Ge Gen (Kudzu) must have basis in Zhu Fengyi's understanding. Though Great Soy Sauce Decoction and Great Sage Powder are good, one should not adhere too rigidly to their interpretations, lest it detracts from the intent of the ancients.[110p]
    Urinary Incontinence

    Postpartum urinary incontinence is due to the insecurity of kidney qi; it is advisable to treat it with the Wuweizi Pill.

    Child's Occiput and Abdominal Pain are Different

    If there are blood clots present in the womb during pregnancy and they do not discharge with the child postpartum, causing abdominal pain, it is termed as the child's occiput. If the lochia does not discharge smoothly and causes pain, it is not considered the child's occiput if there is no accumulation in the uterus. If the lochia has completely discharged, and there is abdominal pain due to other reasons, such as in the case of the Zhengjing Zhi Shi Shao Yao San, or due to blood deficiency as described in the Danggui Sheng Jiang Yangrou Tang, this should be discussed separately. If the Zhi Shi Shao Yao San does not alleviate the condition, one should look for stagnant blood. If signs of blood deficiency occur and nourishing remedies are used, one should apply them appropriately.

    Eye Disorders

    Postpartum, if the eyes are not red or painful, and there are no other illnesses, but there is a tendency to continuously shed cold tears, and in severe cases, vision becomes dimmed, this is not like cold tears due to wind; it is caused by deficiency leading to external pathogens. When the condition is mild, it is often overlooked. The essence and fluids become depleted, leading to weak liver and gallbladder qi, and insufficient kidney water. If left untreated, it could lead to internal disorders such as cataracts or poor vision. Those who excessively weep postpartum frequently suffer from this ailment. Moreover, the condition progresses slowly, and people do not heed the warning, often falling victim to its harm, resulting in regret when it is too late. As childbirth causes all channels to move, qi and blood are both harmed, resulting in great deficiency and vulnerability to pathogens. The qi generated in the liver becomes weak, there is insufficient blood leading to the gallbladder losing nourishment, and the essence fails to thrive, thus leading to an increase in eye disorders.[111p]
    Indeed, the severity of internal and external conditions differs: some experience labor pains and sorrow, crying without the usual seasonal warmth or cold, and suffer from internal obstructions leading to dimness of vision; some have unsealed orifices that allow wind pathogens to enter, resulting in humid, decayed head winds; some suffer from internal conditions due to deficiency where dampness returns to the brain, causing various internal illnesses; others, from excessive toil and indulgence in spicy and hot foods, or from fevers that injure the blood of the eyes, experience external obstructions; all of these arise from internal insufficiency. Those who understand and take care will notice subtle changes without fluctuation. Those who neglect maintenance and instead indulge in harmful habits will find their condition deteriorating, leading to severe and inconsistent symptoms. Generally, postpartum illnesses should be treated early; one must not wait until they linger too long, as prolonged conditions lead to solidified qi and blood, making treatment difficult. External symptoms are easily recognizable, and everyone knows the harm and treats them promptly; internal symptoms, causing gradual harm, are often overlooked. Consequently, many regret their lack of timely intervention.[112p]
    On the Importance of Replenishing Qi and Blood After Childbirth

    It has often been said by Danxi that after childbirth, it is essential to greatly replenish qi and blood. Any complications that arise should be treated last. Most ailments are primarily due to blood deficiency, which should not be addressed with venting methods. This implies that the loss of blood and qi during childbirth leads to significant deficiency, hence all conditions should prioritize substantial replenishment, while other treatments can be approached more gradually. In my early years, I sincerely admired this approach, yet when I practiced it, I encountered numerous difficulties. After experiencing it several times, I began to realize that while there is reason in these words, they may still overstate the situation. Nowadays, the obstetric field exclusively advocates for this method. Having witnessed its misapplications and personally intervening to remedy them, I have observed many issues. Thus, I dare to analyze this further, as I have genuinely seen the problems, and I must speak out, not with the intent to flaunt or to discredit the wisdom of past sages. After childbirth, it is indeed common to experience both qi and blood depletion, leading to many deficiency conditions.[113p]
    There are those who are deficient, those who are not deficient, and those who are entirely strong. In all of these cases, one must follow the evidence based on the person, discern their deficiency or strength, and treat according to standard methods. One must not engage in reckless attempts to overly nourish, as this may exacerbate the illness; thus, discernment is essential.

    Postpartum deficiency is typically correlated with the person’s vital energy and is often seen in those who are inherently weak. Some may experience a complete loss of blood and energy after childbirth, making them even weaker. In such cases, the pulse should be examined in relation to the condition. If all aspects of pulse, energy, form, and illness are insufficient, one must treat it as a complete deficiency. If the physical form is insufficient but the illness persists, whether due to internal heat, external pathogens, or dietary stagnation, this indicates a state of deficiency with some excess, requiring careful examination and treatment. The intricacies of this cannot be fully expressed, so only the discerning may grasp it.

    As for the non-deficient cases postpartum, this may be due to individuals who were healthy before, those who are young, or those with a resilient constitution who are accustomed to hard work. These individuals typically do not have deficiencies, and when they become pregnant, the introduction of the fetus may cause obstruction of blood and energy, leading to bloating and nausea, which are excess conditions. Upon delivery, they will first experience relief and return to their prior state.[114p]
    The production of common people is abundant, so how can there be any void? However, there may be internal injuries or external influences; after childbirth, illness can arise that is difficult to guarantee will not occur. If there is any fallacy, removing it will lead to recovery. If one universally employs great tonics, can the body withstand it? Even during labor, the blood and qi are somewhat depleted, yet what remains is merely supportive for the fetus. What must leave will depart, and what is born will soon return—within a few days, it must revitalize. This is the principle of natural creation; how can it be that all childbirth is void? In treating such conditions, one should only use remedies according to the specific symptoms. If one insists that postpartum care must involve great tonics for qi and blood, then true ailments cannot be avoided; the mild will become severe, and the severe may endanger life. From this, we see that articulating such principles is indeed not easy, and practicing them isn't simple either.

    In cases of complete real symptoms after childbirth, it can be likened to an external attack of wind and cold, resulting in headaches and body heat, with fullness in the abdomen, a tight pulse that is rapid and strong—this is a manifestation of exterior evil.[115p]
    If there is excessive heat, one will surely feel hot, thirsty, restless, and irritable, which may lead to abdominal pain, foul breath, and a black tongue. There is a strong desire for cold drinks, eye discharge, painful and bloody urination, along with a rapid and slippery pulse; this indicates an excess of internal heat. Furthermore, if one experiences anger that stirs the liver, there may be chest and side distention, pain, difficulty with bowel movements, and a wiry and slippery pulse; this indicates a true manifestation of qi stagnation. Also, if there is incomplete discharge of lochia and blood stasis rising, one may feel swelling and pain in the abdomen that resists pressure, with difficult bowel movements and easy urination; this indicates a true manifestation of blood stagnation. In wealthy families, excessive protection or overuse of ginseng, astragalus, and atractylodes can lead to qi congestion; excessive consumption of sweets, alcohol, and heating foods can cause internal heat; or using powerful tonics when the body is not genuinely deficient can exacerbate illness; this indicates a true imbalance in regulation. Additionally, postnatal food intake, fearing fatigue and overexertion, may lead to stagnant accumulation that fails to disperse, indicating true internal injury. When there are external pathogens, one must resolve them; when there is internal heat, one must clear it; and when there is internal injury and stagnation, one must open the pathways for digestion and elimination. Can we be rigid in our approach, ignoring the teachings of Danxi?[116p]
    On the Three Prohibitions

    According to the "Essentials of Disease Mechanisms," when treating diseases related to childbirth, one should base it on the symptoms of the Jueyin. It is important to avoid harming the stomach qi and the upper two Jiao, which makes up the three prohibitions: one must not induce sweating, must not purge, and must not promote urination. If one adheres to these three prohibitions, the Ying and Wei will harmonize, and the cold and heat will naturally cease.

    For treatment methods, if there is thirst, one should use Baihu Decoction; if there is Qi deficiency, Huangqi should be employed; for blood pain, Danggui; and for abdominal pain, Shaoyao. Generally, for childbirth-related illnesses, one may adjust and modify Chaihu based on the situation, while mixed symptoms should follow the increases and decreases of the Four Substances.

    One should observe the pulse and symptoms closely when applying these methods. Although this approach is indeed a fundamental principle for childbirth, since illnesses may vary, if certain symptoms arise, it is necessary to use the appropriate remedies. Therefore, healing requires adapting to the illness. However, one must not neglect these fundamental principles, and the ability to adapt should not be rigidly confined to a single approach.[117p]
    Abdominal Pain

    After childbirth, abdominal pain must be differentiated between excess and deficiency. If there is retained blood causing pain, it is classified as excess pain; if there is no blood but there is pain, it is considered deficiency pain. Generally, the pain is accompanied by fullness or distension, may radiate upwards to the chest and sides, and may resist touch so that one cannot approach it; all these indicate excess pain, which should be treated with dispersing methods. If there is no distention, or if the patient prefers to be massaged, enjoys warmth, or finds relief after eating, these all belong to deficiency pain, and one should not recklessly employ pushing or purging methods.

    In the case of women after childbirth, there is often abdominal pain from the retained fetal position; upon palpation, there may also be a palpable mass, and resistance to touching may be slightly present. Therefore, the ancient prescriptions referred to this as "fetal position," indicating retained blood in the womb, which is a significant misunderstanding. Once the fetus and placenta are expelled, how can blood remain alone? The uterus, having housed the fetus for a long time, suddenly empties, causing the blood sea to become vacuous, hence the pain arises. Injuries to the birth canal can lead to swelling, which may also feel like a mass, but it is not a true mass. Since the swelling has not yet subsided, there may also be resistance to touch. The treatment for this should focus on nourishing the internal organs, and it will heal in no time. The Danbao Decoction is the best choice, followed by the Four Spirits Powder and Five Ingredients Decoction, both of which are also excellent. If one mistakenly identifies this as blood stasis and recklessly uses peach kernel, safflower, donqian (horny goat weed), or other such substances, it will damage the maternal qi and undoubtedly worsen the deficiency condition. For those whose bodies are originally weak and have little blood, there will not have been much blood during childbirth, hence they are not suffering from blood stasis.[118p]
    If there is pain, it is advisable to treat it with previous methods, or utilize decoctions of large and small yams, or a soup made from yellow hen. After childbirth, if there is a weakness of yang energy and cold generated from within, or cold entering from the outside, causing pain in the heart and abdomen, vomiting and lack of appetite, and coldness in the limbs, decoctions of nine honeys or the great rock honey soup, or the regulating yin decoction should be administered.

    If postpartum lochia is not fully expelled and causes lingering pain, this is also commonly encountered. However, this differs from a deficiency pain; it must develop gradually, possibly accompanied by difficulty urinating or defecating, or with constipation leading to distension, extreme pain unapproachable to touch, or pain radiating upwards into the heart and abdomen, or extreme pain causing clenched jaws. In this case, the blood must be expelled quickly. For issues closer to the upper body, the Laughing Powder is suitable; for those nearer to the lower body, the Stagnation-Clearing Decoction, Life-Rescuing Pill, or Reviving Pill should be used. If there is no effect, a good solution is the Decision on Fluids Decoction.

    If there is abdominal pain due to spleen deficiency or kidney deficiency, this is not caused by childbirth but by insufficient internal energy. If there is a deficiency of spleen qi with cold, leading to vomiting, reduced appetite, and abdominal pain, decoctions such as the Five Gentlemen Decoction, Six Gentlemen Soup, or Warm Stomach Drink should be used. If there is deficiency of kidney qi with diarrhea or dysentery accompanying abdominal pain, a decoction for stomach connection or regulating yin decoction should be administered. If there are food stagnations and qi causing pain, they should also be treated accordingly. Decoctions like the Gas-Releasing Drink or the Great Harmonizing Drink can all be used as needed.[119p]
    Postpartum Fever

    After childbirth, there can be fevers caused by external wind-cold, internal fire, deficiency of water and yin, fatigue and vexation from labor, or excessive blood loss leading to confusion and heat. Each condition is different and should be diagnosed accordingly.

    For those experiencing external wind-cold during postpartum, it is common to expose oneself while straining during delivery, paying no attention to the surrounding conditions. If cold pathogens are encountered at this time, they can easily invade. Symptoms may include headaches, body aches, aversion to cold, fevers, or tightness and discomfort in the lower back, with a tight and rapid pulse indicating an external invasion. However, these external invasions are merely incidental; they do not equate to an established injury from cold. Hence, mild treatments can often lead to recovery. One should not assume that dispersing is inappropriate after childbirth; the approach should be adjusted according to the patient’s condition.

    Generally, for postpartum cases with some remaining qi, a formula of San Chai Hu Yin should be given. If qi deficiency and spleen weakness are present, then Si Chai Hu or Wu Chai Hu Yin should be used.[120p]
    If there is a deficiency in the liver, spleen, and kidney yin leading to a pathogen invasion, one should use a nourishing yin and invigorating qi decoction. If there is severe cold deficiency, a regulating yin decoction is suitable. For a robust postpartum woman with sufficient qi who is experiencing symptoms, the proper remedy is the Zheng Chaihu Decoction. If there is also internal heat present while the external pathogen remains unresolved, one should use a single Chaihu Decoction. If there is simultaneous wind and cold with stagnation in both the exterior and interior, the Wujisan is appropriate.

    In cases of fever postpartum with signs of heat, external heat is usually superficial while internal heat pertains to deeper levels. This is often due to excessive adjustment, seasonal heat, excessive consumption of alcohol, inappropriate use of greatly tonifying herbs like ginseng, atractylodes, ginger, and cinnamon, overuse of coal fire, excessive sealing of windows, or overly vigorous activity when the body is robust. Any excessive actions can generate internal heat. When internal heat is strong, symptoms may include tidal fevers, internal heat signs, thirst with a preference for coolness, frequent headaches with sweating, hard stools, red urine, and erratic blood heat, but without superficial signs. The pulse will show a soft and slippery quality without tension while exhibiting fever. This indicates a heat pattern, which should be treated with cooling and transformative decoctions, like Qinghua Decoction or Baoyin Decoction.[121p]
    If the fundamental essence is not empty, or if there is intense fire and urgent conditions, then it is customary to use the methods of shifting and withdrawing the fuel; there is no need for doubt.

    In cases of postpartum yin deficiency with fever, it is essential to realize that this is often due to innate deficiencies in the spleen and kidney, along with the overall exhaustion of qi and blood after childbirth, thus it is quite common. The symptoms often come and go suddenly, occurring sporadically, either day or night, without fixed patterns. There may be feelings of mental fatigue, disorientation, or confusion; however, upon examination, there are no external symptoms present, and the pulse is noted to be string-like and rapid, or floating and string-like with a large amplitude, or weak and fine. The onset is gradual, unlike other conditions that appear abruptly, indicating a state of yin deficiency.

    The treatment should primarily focus on nourishing true yin, utilizing formulas such as Xiao Ying Decoction, San Yin Decoction, or Wu Yin Decoction as needed. If there is mild heat accompanying the yin deficiency, the Yi Yin Decoction is appropriate. If there is severe heat alongside significant yin deficiency, adjustments can be made to the Yi Yin Decoction. If there is intense fire heat with excessive sweating, then Dang Gui Liu Huang Decoction should be used.

    If there is yang deficiency within the yin, causing the fire not to return to its source but to generate heat, then formulas such as Da Ying Decoction, Li Yin Decoction, or You Gui Decoction should be applied.[122p]
    If there is blood deficiency and yang does not adhere to yin, accompanied by restlessness and thirst, one should use Ren Sheng Dang Gui Tang. If both qi and blood are deficient, with fever, agitation, a flushed face, and thirst, one should use Ba Zhen Tang or Shi Quan Da Bu Tang. If there is severe heat and a weak pulse, one should urgently add Gui and Fu. If it is thought to be fire, disaster is at hand.

    In cases after childbirth where there is excessive blood loss and fever, the symptoms will definitely include thirst, shortness of breath, headache, confusion, and internal heat, which also belong to yin deficiency; Ren Sheng Dang Gui Tang should be used to address this.

    For postpartum fatigue and exhaustion, Zhu Yao Tang works wonders, or one can use Huang Chi Ji Tang or Bai Fu Ling San. If there are persistent spontaneous sweats, a hen soup is advisable. If there is also internal cold of the organs, mutton soup is preferred. For both qi and blood deficiency, one should use Wu Fu Yin or Shi Quan Da Bu Tang. If there is an external pathogen with fever, one should use Bu Yin Yi Qi Jian or Bu Zhong Yi Qi Tang. If there is an external pathogen with fever accompanied by internal cold and chills, a modified Li Yin Jian should be used. If there is also yang deficiency and internal cold, Wu Jun Zi Jian or Li Yin Jian is indicated. If there is excess yang with yin deficiency accompanied by internal heat, one should use Wu Fu Yin with ingredients like Shao Yao, Huang Qin, and Di Gu Pi, as appropriate.[123p]
    After childbirth, there are two types of shortness of breath: one due to extreme Yin deficiency, and the other caused by cold pathogens in the lungs. After childbirth, there is already significant deficiency, so how can there be abundant Qi and yet shortness of breath? If there is no cold pathogen in the lungs and shortness of breath is present, it is due to blood depleting Yin, leaving solitary Yang without support, resulting in exhausted, short, and easily escaping Qi. This indicates a severe warning of potential detachment of the root due to the liver and kidneys not being connected. The text states: "The liver suffers from urgency, eat sweet food to alleviate it," which pertains to this situation. The Zhenyuan Decoction is an extraordinary remedy for this.

    If there is a combination of Qi deficiency and cold, one should use the Great Nourishing Decoction or the Regulating Yin Decoction. If there is external wind-cold causing the invasion of evil Qi into the lungs resulting in shortness of breath, it will definitely present as coarse breathing and chest fullness, or possibly excessive coughing, which is distinct from shortness of breath where the upper and lower Qi do not connect. The treatment should focus on dispersing and also tonifying, with the Jinshui Liu Jun Decoction or the Six Gentlemen Decoction as options. If only cold pathogens have invaded the lungs, and there is ample Qi without deficiency, then the Liu An Decoction or Er Chen Decoction with the addition of herbs like Suye should be utilized.[124p]

    Lochia

    Postpartum lochia that continues without end, if due to heat in the blood, should be treated with Baoyin Decoction or Qinghua Drink. If there is damage to the Chong and Ren vessels leading to persistent bleeding, one should use a modified version of the Gudan Decoction. If there is deficiency in both the liver and spleen qi, unable to control the blood leading to unceasing bleeding, one should use Shoupi Decoction or Buzhong Yiqi Decoction. If both qi and blood are deficient, with pale blood continuously flowing, one should use Da Bujuan Decoction or Shiquan Dabu Decoction. If anger has harmed the liver and blood cannot be contained, one should use Jiawei Siwu Decoction.

    Convulsion

    Postpartum convulsion indicates a significant deficiency of yin fire. The symptoms include arching of the lower back, staring straight ahead, or limbs becoming tense with the body convulsing. Although there is a distinction between rigid and soft convulsions in the context of cold damage, fundamentally, they all stem from dry or depleted blood conditions and primarily concern the foot Taiyang and Shaoyin meridians. This occurs because the bladder and kidneys are interrelated, with the kidneys governing essence and blood, and the two Taiyang meridians run along the head, neck, and back, leading to such symptoms.

    The causes include mismanagement of conditions such as sweating excessively due to cold damage leading to loss of fluids, excessive purging leading to loss of yin, or after a significant discharge of pus and blood from ulcers. Therefore, during the postpartum period, excessive loss of blood or significant sweating and diarrhea contributes to this condition, indicating extreme depletion of original qi and desiccation of blood. Upon encountering this condition, it is imperative to assess the balance of yin and yang quickly, and to replenish qi and blood using Da Bujuan Decoction or Liyin Decoction, and similar preparations to safeguard the patient's life. Incorrectly treating it as wind-phlegm and using dispersing or resolving medications would surely lead to death.[125p]
    Afterbirth Not Expelled

    When the vital energy and blood are weak, causing the afterbirth to become stagnant and not expelled, the symptoms observed are generally just a lack of strength, with no accompanying pain or bloating. The treatment should focus on replenishing qi and invigorating blood, utilizing remedies such as Juejin Decoction, Slippery胎 Decoction, Baosheng Wuyou Powder, or similar formulas.

    If the lochia flows back into the womb and causes swelling and obstruction, it is because once the child is born, the afterbirth tends to descend. Therefore, the placenta remains in the abdomen, shaped like inverted leaves, accumulating blood and water, which causes difficulty in expulsion. Experienced midwives often use their fingers to press on the bottom of the placenta to facilitate blood circulation, or they may touch the upper opening to create an opening for the lochia to pour out, allowing the abdomen to empty and the placenta to fall out naturally.

    If blood stagnates inside the womb for too long, leading to bloating, pain, shortness of breath, or urgency, it is necessary to expel the blood forcefully. It is advisable to use the Duoming Dan, or Shixiao Powder, mixed with hot wine to disperse the blood and relieve the swelling, allowing the afterbirth to descend on its own. If both qi and blood are deficient, Juejin Decoction is also a suitable option.[126p]
    Blood Movement

    Once the placenta is expelled, both qi and blood are lost. Suddenly, one experiences blackening of the eyes and dizziness, with a stupor, mouth closed, and an inability to recognize people. The ancients often said that the retained lochia can attack through a void, leading to blood movement. There are two types of this condition: one is blood movement, the other is qi collapse. If one mistakes qi collapse for blood movement and uses spicy and aromatic remedies to dispel blood and transform phlegm, death can occur immediately; great caution is required.

    In the case of qi collapse, during childbirth when blood flow is profuse, both blood and qi deplete, often resulting in unconsciousness. If there is slight deficiency, recovery will occur shortly; if there is great deficiency, immediate collapse will lead to death. Observing a pale face, closed eyes, open mouth, cold hands, and very weak pulses indicates qi collapse. One should quickly use one or two taels of ginseng, boil it into a strong decoction, and slowly administer it. As long as it can be swallowed, it can save a life; any delay may be fatal.

    There have been instances where people prohibited the use of ginseng and subsequently died, arguing that it should not be used after childbirth, as it will nourish the retained morbid blood, leading to harm. Even when advised otherwise, they refused to use it and waited until it was too late to regret. Additionally, there are claims that ginseng can only be used after seven days postpartum. These types of ignorant rumors have persisted, and their source is unknown; they mislead many and are utterly untrustworthy.

    The blood movement condition primarily arises from qi deficiency, resulting in transient dizziness. However, it may also occur in cases of blood stagnation and phlegm accumulation.[127p]
    If both the physical and vital qi are excessive, resulting in chest and abdominal distension and pain radiating upwards, this is a condition of reversed blood flow and should be treated with Shixiao San. If phlegm is abundant and qi is coarse, then Erchen Decoction is appropriate. If there is no such distension, pain, or coarse qi, it indicates a deficiency of qi, and one should administer a large dose of Xianggui Decoction or Eight Treasures Decoction.

    In cases of sudden fainting or collapse, where there is not enough time for medicine to take effect, one can use vinegar to wet a scale weight, or apply vinegar to the mouth and nose, or burn old lacquerware and dry lacquer, methods that are only suitable for light and abrupt conditions. If there is severe qi deficiency leading to fainting, merely using tonics will not be beneficial.

    According to Xue Lizhai, in all cases of difficult childbirth and labor that drain the vital energy of the fetus, there are often instances where the child may be born disconnected but weak or on the verge of death, or even already deceased. One must not hastily cut the umbilical cord; instead, one should urgently use a large paper wick soaked in sesame oil to apply to the umbilical cord and burn it to sever it, drawing upon its vital energy to sustain the fetal essence. In a moment, if the child lets out a cry, it indicates revival, and it can also help prevent cold-induced stomach issues and diarrhea. In all such occurrences, if the umbilical cord is cut with a knife, both mother and child are likely to face great difficulties; this theory is very sound. However, the method of burning the cord should only be employed in cases where there is significant deficiency of yang or when the qi is detached during delivery, to aid in restoring yang energy. If the mother's qi yang is strong, or if the child's voice is loud, this method is not recommended, for fear that fire may enter through the umbilical cord and later cause heat toxicity, resulting in considerable harm.[128p]
    The Secret Record of the Stone Chamber
    [Qing Dynasty, Chen Shiduo]

    Discussion on Postpartum Replenishment

    The ailments following childbirth are countless, but primarily focus on replenishing qi and blood. While various treatments can be devised, they should not deviate from this core principle. After childbirth, it is common to experience headaches, fever, abdominal pain, or cramps in the hands and feet; there may also be fullness in the sides with vomiting. Wind pathogens can lead to a shift to a cold and yin condition, and cool air invading can cause reversed qi. These conditions can lead to life-threatening situations at any moment, creating urgency in an instant.

    If one were to treat external symptoms, administering medicine could be fatal; therefore, caution is paramount! The formula consists of five qian of ginseng, five qian of baizhu, one liang of cooked rehmannia, two liang of angelica, one liang of chuanxiong, and two qian of fried schizonepeta (charred). This is the main formula. If there are wind symptoms, add six fen of chaihu; if cold has entered, add one qian each of aconite and cinnamon. Other symptoms should not result in arbitrary additions; following this formula, the effects are invariably miraculous. One can reduce the amounts slightly, but the medicinal properties must not be omitted. It is because the blood of a postpartum woman has been significantly diminished, rendering her skin and tissues thin, making them susceptible to both invasion by pathogens and release of illness.[129p]
    Therefore, in using a large dose to replenish the essence, one might add slightly to dispel pathogens, with a subtle flavor, so that the pathogens can exit the body. How could this be compared to a regular, healthy person, worrying that the pathogens would be difficult to disperse, and thus heavily relying on methods to expel them? The essence of the formula lies entirely in nourishing qi and blood, without paying attention to the pathogens, focusing solely on supporting the upright. With plenty of upright energy, the pathogenic energy naturally retreats. Moreover, the formula originally contains the wonderful herb jIng jie, which not only guides qi and blood back into the meridians, but also leads pathogenic energy out through the skin. This is what makes the formula extraordinary and miraculous.

    If a child has a painful pillow area, and gentle pressure causes slight pain, one may add ten grains of mountain hawthorn and five peach kernels; one dose will resolve the issue, and no other medicine should be lightly added.

    Question: Can cooked Rehmannia be taken within three days? Answer: One day is perfectly fine to consume.

    For various postpartum conditions, the core treatment is to replenish qi and blood, using three qian of ginseng, one liang of angelica, five qian of ligusticum, one qian of black-fried jIng jie, and one qian of motherwort, decocted in water for oral intake. If there is wind, add half a qian of bupleurum; if there is cold, add one qian of cinnamon; if there is unclean blood, add ten grains of hawthorn; if blood circulation is poor, add half a qian of fried black ginger slices; for nosebleeds, add two qian of ophiopogon; for night heat, add half a qian of reptan; if there is food stagnation, add five grains of hawthorn and one qian of barley sprouts; if there is little phlegm, add half a qian of white mustard seeds. Other ingredients must not be added lightly. This formula purely nourishes qi and blood and does not treat the exterior, which is why it is exceptional. I have personally treated postpartum conditions with it, and it has always been remarkably effective.[130p]
    Blood Circulation

    After childbirth, if the blood is dry and circulation is poor, and the person becomes unresponsive, this is a critical moment of life and death. This condition arises from the excessive loss of blood; the old blood has already been released, and new blood cannot be generated rapidly, causing a disconnection between yin and yang. Therefore, a sacred formula for restoring circulation should be used, which includes one ounce of ginseng, two ounces of angelica, one ounce of chuanxiong, one ounce of white atractylodes, one ounce of cooked rehmannia, and ten pennies of roasted black ginger, boiled in water for consumption. Ginseng aids in recovery from collapse, angelica and chuanxiong promote blood circulation and the generation of new blood, cooked rehmannia and white atractylodes benefit the lower abdomen and replenish the spleen and kidneys, while black ginger directs blood back to the meridians to stop the poor circulation. One dose can yield immediate effects, rescuing from death to life—a truly miraculous formula for reviving the spirit.[131p]
    Feeling the Chill of the Wind

    After childbirth, if one is struck by wind-evil from the Taiyang (greater yang), experiencing severe coughing, vomiting, and retching, this is an untreatable condition. When coughing, the vital yang is on the brink of extinction; how much worse is it when the cough is severe? When vomiting occurs, the stomach's energy is on the verge of collapse; how much more so with severe vomiting? When retching happens, the spleen's energy is about to fail; how dire then with severe retching? After childbirth, the blood and energy are significantly weakened; how can one withstand these three? It is indeed a certain sign of death. If you wish to seek life from death, what method will you use to save them? You should greatly tonify energy and blood while slightly administering medications to stop vomiting, retching, and coughing; addressing the Taiyang wind-evil merely superficially, thus leaving it untreated.

    Therefore, use the formula "Zhuanqi Jiu Chan Tang" (Transformed Qi Rescue Decoction): three liang of ginseng, three liang of ophiopogon, one liang of atractylodes, one liang of angelica, three qian of chuanxiong, one qian of schizopepetalum, three fen of cinnamon twig, and decoct in water for consumption. After one dose, the coughing may cease and vomiting will stop, thus allowing for the possibility of life; otherwise, death follows.

    Ginseng seizes vital energy at the precipice of collapse, while ophiopogon stabilizes lung energy at the point of preservation; atractylodes saves spleen and stomach energy on the verge of disintegration. Angelica and chuanxiong mainly generate blood. Schizopepetalum still guides blood back to the channels while simultaneously dispelling evil, assisting cinnamon twig in expelling wind while directing downward and preventing it from reverse flow. The proportions in the formula hold deep significance; they should not be used carelessly or in excess. In general, if this remedy is used for seven or eight patients with this condition, one should not skimp on the amount of ginseng used.

    The postnatal wind-cold illness is a manifestation of Taiyang, with the patient expectorating pus and blood.[132p]
    Headaches feel as if they will break, and the heart is endlessly troubled. The abdominal pain feels like death, or there may be a sensation of chest binding, all of which are beyond rescue. This is due to a significant loss of qi and blood after childbirth; evil cannot be expelled, and the illness becomes severe, leading to a failure to properly address the injuries. For instance, if one vomits pus and blood, it indicates that the blood is rising instead of descending. A headache that feels as if it will break suggests that blood cannot nourish the yang, and yang is on the verge of being extinguished by yin. Constant heart agitation indicates that heart blood is exhausted, and kidney water is not ascending to nourish. Abdominal pain that feels like death signifies extreme cold in the abdomen, with cold invading the kidneys, and the fire of life is at risk of escaping externally. Or, there may be a feeling of chest binding, indicating retained undigested food in the stomach and an impending collapse of stomach qi. Experiencing even one of these symptoms is already quite difficult to save from; how much more so when all appear together? Death is assured.[133p]
    I wish to rescue her with a single prescription; why is that? It is because she is suffering from a postnatal affliction, which does not require complicated considerations; simply correcting the main issue will cause the affliction to recede. I use the Buddha's Hand Powder, adding more ginseng along with cinnamon and schizonepeta. There is no need to treat the various symptoms individually; all symptoms will surely disappear by themselves.

    For the ingredients: two taels of angelica sinensis, one tael of ligusticum wallichii, three taels of ginseng, two qian of schizonepeta, and one qian of cinnamon. One dose will show effect, and with a second dose, complete recovery will follow. The Buddha's Hand Powder is originally a sacred formula for treating postpartum issues, and with the addition of ginseng, its efficacy is increased. It promotes new growth and eliminates the old, disperses pathogens and restores balance, relieves pain and eases the mind, enhances appetite and aids digestion, hence its miraculous effectiveness.

    After childbirth, if one is afflicted by the lesser yang wind pathogen, they may speak incoherently and feel constant agitation; if made worse by palpitations, it can be fatal. This relates to the lesser yang gallbladder channel—if there is no fluid in the gallbladder, it cannot nourish the heart, and without blood in the heart, it cannot sustain itself, leading to confusion and incoherent speech.[134p]
    Restlessness and palpitations arise due to an absence of blood. Without blood to replenish the blood, how can it not lead to death? It is unknown that when the gallbladder suffers from evil, if it does not express, blood cannot be generated; however, if it merely expresses, blood will dissipate even more. Thus, balancing the two is difficult, making it hard to treat. Yet it is not that it cannot be treated at all; I use the method of the Buddha's hand for an adjusted treatment, and it can restore health.

    For this, I use two taels of Angelica, one tael of Chuanxiong, one tael of ginseng, one tael of stir-fried jujube seeds, three qian of Ophiopogon, a handful of bamboo shavings, one qian of realgar, and five qian of processed rehmannia, boiled in water and taken as medicine. This formula of Angelica and Chuanxiong generates blood to nourish the heart, alongside ginseng, jujube seeds, Ophiopogon, bamboo shavings, and realgar—all of which are calming medicines. Processed rehmannia is a marvelous remedy for strengthening the kidneys, requiring harmony from above and below, thus supporting the kidneys together. With this, how can restlessness not be dispelled, and palpitations not stabilize, while the nonsensical talk ceases?

    For those after childbirth who suffer from the Yangming wind evil, with great panting and sweating, this too cannot be treated. The wind evil enters the Yangming, transforming cold into heat, thus causing great panting and sweating. Normally, for a healthy person with this illness, one would use the Baihu decoction; yet for a postpartum woman with depleted blood and energy, how could this be appropriate? Nevertheless, by greatly replenishing the postpartum woman's blood and energy, while also treating the Yangming evil, it may indeed lower the fire, and the great panting and sweating might very well subside.

    The prescription used is a formula to replenish deficiency and lower fire: one tael of Ophiopogon, five qian of ginseng, five qian of root of Scrophularia, fourteen leaves of mulberry, and five fen of perilla seeds, all boiled and taken as medicine.[135p]
    This formula contains ginseng and ophiopogon to replenish qi, with adenophora to reduce fire, mulberry leaves to stop sweating, and perilla seeds to calm wheezing. It supports the righteous without attacking the邪, and repels邪 without harming the righteous; this truly has remarkable efficacy.

    After childbirth, if one experiences the邪 of the yang ming, leading to insanity and yang collapse, this is an irremediable condition. Cases of mania are often due to excess heat, but postpartum mania is a result of deficiency heat. While excess heat can be drained to calm mania, how can deficiency heat be drained to resolve it? Nevertheless, I believe recovery is possible precisely because of the yang collapse. Yang collapse often indicates qi deficiency; even in cases of excess heat, qi remains deficient. Therefore, when draining excess heat, it is essential to also use ginseng, especially since postpartum conditions are primarily deficient. Generally, for yang collapse, using a remedy that stops sweating can restore vitality.[136p]
    I will not focus on calming the mania for now; first, I will stop the sweating. Then, I will use the formula for collecting yang: three taels of ginseng, thirty pieces of mulberry leaves, two taels of lily bulb, one tael of rehmannia, five qian of sweet wormwood, boiled in water and taken as a decoction. After one dose, the sweating will cease; after two doses, the mania will be settled, but do not use a third dose. After the second dose, only use ginseng, ophiopogon, north five-flavor, angelica, and chuanxiong to regulate; this will naturally bring peace. This formula can only urgently rescue the vital yang in critical situations and should not be taken as a divine recipe for childbirth. Although sweet wormwood is nourishing, it tends to disperse more than it nourishes; it merely borrows from its dispersing properties to drive out the fire in the stomach, serving as a temporary measure. If used excessively, it could harm the energy and blood of the postpartum woman. Therefore, after two doses, it is essential to switch to another formula.

    If a woman in her last month of pregnancy suddenly experiences a lesser yin condition, she must urgently warm herself with a large dose of ginseng and white atractylodes; if not effective, she may die. This is according to the writings of Zhongjing. It seems that without ginseng and white atractylodes, there are no other remedies to save her.[137p]
    I believe that merely using ginseng and white atractylodes is not entirely sufficient. If ginseng and white atractylodes do not work, one should urgently add aconite, cinnamon, and dried ginger; it may respond, like an echo. I have now composed a formula called "Universal Life-Saving Decoction": one tael of ginseng, one tael of white atractylodes, one qian of aconite, and five fen of licorice, to be boiled in water and taken. This can effectively treat anyone affected by the cold pathogens of the Lesser Yin channel.

    For a woman who has given birth, after three to four days up to six or seven days, if she suddenly exhibits curled hands and feet, high breathing, gasping, nausea, and abdominal pain, it is critical to act. This symptom arises from the cold evil of the Lesser Yin, which forces the true yang inside to rise to the upper part, creating a pseudo-heat above and true cold below. If treated improperly, it could prove fatal. Urgently apply the "Calming Breathing and Expelling Cold Powder": two taels of ginseng, five qian of ophiopogon, two qian of cinnamon, three taels of white atractylodes, five fen of evodia, boiled in water and taken. One dose stops the wheezing, and two doses stop the pain. This formula is also a holy medicine for replenishing qi and reversing rebellion, and a divine method for expelling cold and stopping wheezing. However, if taken incorrectly, it may often lead to failure; it is essential to prepare the medicine properly and wait until it is mildly warm before taking it, as the medicinal properties are hot while the illness is profoundly cold – one must follow the nature of the medicine.

    After half a month, approaching a full month post-partum, if the same symptoms occur again, the previous formula should not be used. Instead, one should change to the "Postpartum Protection Decoction": five qian of ginseng, five qian of poria, one qian of aconite, five qian of white atractylodes, one tael of angelica, one tael of cooked rehmannia, five qian of cornelian cherry, five qian of ophiopogon, and one qian of achyranthes, to be boiled in water and taken.[138p]
    After a woman has given birth for half a month or is about to reach a month, her blood and qi are not as greatly depleted as immediately after childbirth; therefore, less of ginseng can be used. In replenishing yang, one can also use medicinals that nourish yin, such as Fuzi to dispel cold, without fear of yin stagnation being a problem.

    If a woman has blue hands and feet and her entire body appears black after childbirth, it is beyond salvation. This condition indicates severe yin-cold and toxic energy. There is essentially no way to restore life, yet to see someone who is not dead and provide no remedy would be to miss the chance to prepare a prescription and attempt to save her. I now propose a prescription, named the Blue Dispersing Black Decoction: four taels of ginseng, four taels of Atractylodes, one qian of Fuzi, one tael of Angelica, three qian of cinnamon, and decoct in water for consumption. When this prescription is taken, the bluish color of the hands and feet will diminish, and the body will not remain black, thus there will be a chance for life; otherwise, she will still die. The poison is deeply rooted and cannot be resolved, while the cold is bound and cannot be released.

    If after childbirth the feet are purely blue and there is pain under the heart, although this condition is somewhat less serious than the previous one, the attack of cold poison on the heart is consistent, thus it also leads to death. In the past, using this prescription has often yielded good results, and the difficulty is not as great as with those whose entire body has turned black. This condition arises from the bottom upwards; dispersing the cold below can relieve the cold above, making it easier to achieve results.

    If after childbirth there is a minor yin condition with an invasion of evil, and the kidney water rises, causing vomiting and diarrhea, this is also a sign of death. Originally, the kidney water in a woman post-delivery is depleted; how then does it rise to cause vomiting? The overflow of kidney water is due to the decline of kidney fire. Fire dispels cold, and water likewise follows cold to increase its flow.[139p]
    It is already difficult to save a common person with this condition; how much more so for a woman who has just given birth? Yet I believe there is a way to rescue her, which lies in the preservation of kidney essence. One should urgently use supplementary yang medicine, incorporated into the tonic for yin, to draw fire back to its source, allowing the water to naturally descend instead of surging upwards.

    The prescription consists of: ginseng (5 qian), white atractylodes (1 liang), cooked rehmannia (5 qian), cornelian cherry (1 liang), poria (1 liang), aconite (1 qian), cinnamon (2 qian), and plantago seeds (1 qian), all decocted in water to be taken as a single dose, preventing the kidney essence from overflowing. This formula greatly replenishes the fire of the gate of life, while also nourishing the water, thus allowing the water to return to its source and the fire to have vitality. Together, they combine and achieve mutual completion.

    If after four or five days postpartum, one suddenly feels the onset of wind pathogen causing convulsions, this is a deadly sign. Convulsions are often associated with Heat, yet how could it be heat causing convulsions postpartum? This heat is actually vacuous heat. To treat convulsions while the body is weak, one cannot disperse the evil; to clear heat while the body is weak, one cannot use coolness. Thus, it is often considered difficult to treat and deemed a fatal sign, when in fact not all is hopeless.

    I now prescribe a formula, named the “Turning Convulsion to Tranquility Postpartum Recipe”: angelica (1 liang) and ginseng (1 liang), aconite (1 qian), decocted in water for a single dose, will stabilize the convulsions and allow life to sustain. The convulsions postpartum arise from the deficiency of yang, while the yin and blood have been consumed, compounded by the invasion of cold evil. I utilize ginseng to restore the original energy from the ethereal void, angelica to generate blood from the residual stagnation, and aconite to expel the external evil, thus restoring what is correct and dispersing the evil, leading to the generation of blood and the resolution of convulsions.[140p]
    After childbirth, if one suffers from the condition of the Jueyin, experiences vomiting, and has fullness in both sides, there will certainly be blood in the stool, which is a sign of a condition that cannot be treated. This is due to liver injury and the blood descending. If there is no blood and the blood is further injured, how can survival be expected? If I must try to save her, what methods can I rely on? It is indeed due to the blood in the stool. If the liver is affected by wind evil and does not descend, then the evil remains in both sides, turning into a condition of the abdomen and heart. Now, since blood has entirely moved towards the large intestine and exited, this indicates that the evil in the liver has dispersed. I will clear the fire of the large intestine, which seems to be effective. However, a postpartum woman should be warmed and nourished, not subjected to purging; using cool medicines to eliminate the fire is not a way to rescue a postpartum woman. One does not know that excess fire indicates insufficient water; if water is greatly replenished, then the fire will naturally diminish and disappear. Therefore, I will use the Pinggan Jiuxue Decoction.[141p]
    One tael of Angelica, five coins of Chuanxiong, one tael of Ophiopogon, and one coin of powdered Notoginseng, boil in water and take. With one dose, the bleeding stops; the fullness in the sides also fades. How could there be vomiting or bloody stools?

    In cases of postpartum diarrhea combined with reversal of qi, restlessness preventing sleep, or continuous reversals, these are signs of imminent death. Diarrhea leads to the loss of yin, and reversal to the loss of yang, making recovery difficult; moreover, restlessness indicates the blood cannot nourish the heart. If reversals continue, sweating will become excessive—can one avoid death under such conditions?

    I seek life amidst death, and for this, only ginseng and Angelica are my remedies. This formula is called Ginseng and Angelica Decoction, with each using two taels of ginseng and Angelica, and one coin of Schizonepeta; boil in water and take.

    Using ginseng and Angelica to replenish qi and blood fosters new growth; with the old blood stopping, the new blood increases, thus naturally providing the nourishment needed for the heart, stabilizing the reversals, calming the heart, and alleviating the restlessness.[142p]
    When a postpartum woman contracts the evil of wind, it is crucial to treat her illness promptly. If the woman experiences this condition, her old blood has already been expended, and her new blood has yet to form. This creates a significant weakness in her body, making it easy for her to be afflicted by evil influences. When wind and cold invade, the evil quickly scatters, leading to a state of coldness and potential death in an instant.

    The recommended treatment includes one tael each of Angelica and Ginseng, five qian of Chuanxiong, and ten qian each of Schizonepeta, Cinnamon, and Motherwort. The effectiveness of this formula lies in the equal amounts of Ginseng and Angelica; Ginseng strengthens the qi, while Angelica promotes blood production. Once the qi and blood are restored, the wind pathogens can be easily dispelled.

    For those who are very weak, caution is necessary when using slightly toxic herbs, as the evil can easily emerge if the body's defenses are weak and the pores are left unsealed. Among the ingredients, Schizonepeta is particularly excellent; it not only easily expels the evil but also aids in bringing back the old blood into the channels and supports the new blood to restore balance, ensuring the success of treatment. Motherwort is especially beneficial in obstetrics; can there be any doubt? This approach emphasizes the need to fortify qi and blood first, with dispelling the evil being a secondary consideration – another effective method.[143p]
    Heat Stroke

    After childbirth, if one suddenly experiences heat stroke, with cholera-like vomiting and diarrhea, the method should not be in rescuing but rather, there are effective medicines that can save a life, yet one must never use schisandra. The prescription for dispelling heat and invigorating post-natal recovery includes one ounce of ginseng, two ounces of angelica, one ounce of chuanxiong, two qian of cinnamon, and one qian of sweet wormwood, which should be decocted in water and taken as one dose to achieve recovery. The essence here is that postpartum women should only replenish their qi and blood; once the qi and blood are restored, the heat will naturally disperse. Moreover, the prescription contains ingredients that dispel cold and alleviate heat. Thus, its efficacy is indeed remarkable.

    Some may wonder why cinnamon, associated with heat, is used against heat stroke. They fail to realize that postpartum women are usually very deficient in qi and blood and overall cold; upon feeling the heat, they experience discomfort, which is not due to intense heat penetrating deeply, but rather a slight encounter with the heat and their inherent cold clashing, resulting in cholera-like symptoms. Therefore, cinnamon is employed to warm their cold deficiency, while sweet wormwood is used to alleviate the mild heat. When these are incorporated into a substantial remedy for replenishing qi and blood, it allows for a controlled response without any chaotic symptoms. This is the brilliance of the prescription that achieves such efficacy; why should there be any doubt?[144p]
    Edema

    When a postpartum woman experiences edema, resulting in swelling of the face, hands, feet, and a feeling of fullness in the heart, it is a condition that cannot be treated. However, this swelling is not due to water affecting the body; rather, it is caused by a deficiency of vital energy. Treating it as if it were due to water and dampness would lead to death. Therefore, I do not treat water and dampness; instead, I focus on strengthening the spleen, which can lead to recovery. The prescription consists of a medicinal decoction to assist with the distribution of fluids: two taels of white atractylodes, three taels of ginseng, five qian of poria, one tael of coix seed, five fen of dried tangerine peel, and three fen of radish seeds, to be decocted in water and taken. This formula's ingredients—poria, coix seed, ginseng, and white atractylodes—are all excellent for strengthening the spleen. The dried tangerine peel and radish seeds are slightly included to alleviate the feeling of fullness. When the spleen's energy is strong, water and dampness will naturally dissipate; as water and dampness move, the fullness will gradually disappear, leading to a reduction in swelling as well. However, this formula requires ample food intake to show effective results; one should not expect immediate effects from a single dose.[145p]
    Dysentery

    Dysentery after childbirth, accompanied by vomiting, is undoubtedly a sign of impending death. This is because dysentery depletes yin, which even a healthy person should avoid; how much more for a woman who has just given birth, whose qi and blood are greatly deficient? Furthermore, the addition of vomiting signifies that there is fire in the stomach, disrupting the balance and further weakening qi and blood. This will inevitably lead to the cessation of stomach qi; if not death, what is to follow? However, if stomach qi has not completely ceased, rescue is still possible. I have a method that does not require taking medicine: simply crush one field snail, add a small amount of musk and one part of Evodia rutaecarpa powdered finely, and place it over the navel. This will stop the vomiting, and one may rejoice in this new lease of life. The field snail is very effective in promoting water discharge and reducing fire. Dysentery is fundamentally a heat condition compounded by dampness. A postpartum woman’s dysentery arises from qi and blood deficiency, so one should not recklessly use medicines that clear heat and dispel fire, as this would worsen the deficiency. Therefore, using external treatment with the field snail is quite clever. If vomiting occurs again, quickly take one tael of angelica, three qian of white peony, one qian of licorice, three fen of bitter orange, and three fen of betel nut, and decoct in water for drinking. Three doses will eliminate the dysentery, after which one can use a ginseng decoction for recovery.[146p]
    Yellowing of the Body

    If a postpartum woman has a body that is completely yellow, it indicates dampness and heat that are stagnant and not dispersing. Seeking to treat the yellowing while causing further depletion of blood and qi, and intending to tonify the deficiency while worsening the damp yellow condition, leads to a dead end in treatment, leaving the physician helpless and resigned to the patient's death. Nevertheless, since the formation of dampness and heat originates from deficiency, it is not impossible to treat yellow disease by tonifying the deficiency; however, it is essential to simultaneously address both issues as part of a proper method. I have a remedy that is highly effective for those who turn yellow due to deficiency, which is not limited to postpartum women. This remedy is called "Tonifying the Deficiency and Dispelling Yellow Decoction": one tael of white atractylodes, two taels of coix seeds, five qian of plantago seeds, five qian of poria, one qian of schizonepeta, and five fen of gentian. Boil these in water to consume. For the average person who is not postpartum, use three qian of gentian. The brilliance of this formula lies in its ability to strengthen the spleen and promote water metabolism without depleting qi. It both tonifies the deficiency and removes dampness; when the dampness is removed, the yellowing will not persist, which has never been the case otherwise.[147p]
    Title: Edema Due to Dampness, Difficulty in Urination

    The postpartum woman experiences dampness affecting her lower channels, resulting in swelling, with minimal urine output—this is a critical condition. Water enters the abdomen and necessarily moves towards the bladder for elimination. If urine doesn't pass through the bladder and instead accumulates in the channels of the womb, it conflicts with the normal flow and leads to death. How can I save her? I will treat the bladder accordingly. The bladder's ability to transform water relies on the kidney's function. If a postpartum woman is severely deficient in qi and blood, then the kidney qi is also weak. A deficiency in kidney qi leads to a weakened qi in the bladder, hence the bladder fails to metabolize water, and water accumulates in the uterus instead of dispersing. It starts with urgency and progresses to swelling, ultimately resulting in minimal urine output.

    Now, I will not only treat the bladder but also the kidneys first. When kidney qi is sufficient, bladder qi will function independently, and the water pathway will naturally be unblocked. The formula used is a water-dispersing decoction consisting of one liang each of white atractylodes, coix seed, ginseng, and cooked rehmannia, three qian each of poria and plantago seeds, five qian of shan zhu yu, and five fen of cinnamon, which should be decocted and taken. This formula nourishes the kidneys while also benefiting the heart, as the uterus is connected to the heart above and the kidneys below. By nourishing both the heart and kidneys, the qi of the uterus will be unblocked, and it will no longer retain water to be diverted to the bladder.
