Furthermore, the bladder, due to the unobstructed kidney qi, will be able to transform water and facilitate its passage through the intestines to exit via the anus. This method is truly effective, not merely a random prescription.[148p]
    Shortness of Breath

    When a postpartum woman is overwhelmed by water and has persistent shortness of breath, it is indeed a sign of imminent death. However, if treated correctly, she is not beyond saving. A postpartum woman, weakened and susceptible to excess water, cannot be fully treated for the water condition. Even if she suffers from persistent shortness of breath, which appears to be caused by water, merely treating the water is insufficient. This treatment may worsen the shortness of breath. Therefore, what is the appropriate course of action? One should bolster the spleen’s vitality so it is not afraid of water; if the spleen is not overwhelmed, the shortness of breath will not arise.

    The prescription involves a remedy called Shining Comforting Breathing Pill, composed of one ounce of ginseng, two ounces of astragalus, one ounce of ophiopogon, three qian of poria, and one qian of perilla seeds, to be decocted in water for consumption. This formulation employs ginseng to replenish qi and strengthen the spleen, astragalus to support the waist and bolster the spleen, and ophiopogon to nourish the lungs and reinforce the spleen. Poria and perilla seeds serve merely as adjuncts to promote the movement of water and alleviate shortness of breath. Indeed, treating shortness of breath requires profound skill. A strong spleen means abundant earth; when earth is abundant, water dares not overflow, preventing the emergence of swelling and shortness of breath.[149p]
    Excessive Sweating and Thirst after Childbirth

    After giving birth for half a month, if there suddenly occurs a profuse sweating like rain, accompanied by thirst, dry tongue, fever, and restlessness, it resembles symptoms of typhus, indicating imminent death. If treated as typhus, there will surely be no escape from death. This condition is due to internal fluids being depleted, and blood failing to nourish the heart; without the cooling yin, the yang cannot transform, leading to sweating, loss of yang, and body heat. Thus, although the mouth is thirsty, there is no desire to drink; the tongue may be dry, yet the fetus is still slippery. In severe cases, it can cause restlessness without leading to madness, which distinguishes it from typhus.

    At such a time, one must urgently use two taels each of ginseng, angelica, and astragalus, thirty leaves of mulberry, one qian of five-flavored root, and five qian of flower lily, decocting and taking it as medicine. This formula is named "Sweat-Catching Pill." Ginseng, angelica, and astragalus greatly replenish vital energy and blood, while lily and five-flavored root clear while containing moisture; mulberry leaves help stop sweating, and together have miraculous effects. This type of excessive sweat can only be controlled by nourishment; without moisture, it won't be contained. Hence, one dose stops the sweat, and two doses consolidate it, bringing back the dead to life—this is precisely what this formula achieves.[150p]
    Expelling Ascaris

    The act of expelling ascaris after childbirth is a sign of an untreated condition, as the stomach qi is almost extinguished, and the worms cannot find a place to settle. Ascaris does not reside in the stomach during great cold or great heat. If a woman vomits ascaris post-delivery, it must occur after a fainting spell. The expulsion of ascaris will happen thoroughly, not merely one or two worms by chance. Moreover, if they come out in clusters, that is truly a severe condition. Why do I wish to save the person? It is precisely because the expulsion of ascaris indicates that there is still a chance for survival. When the vital organs are failing, the worms also become quiet. However, if they are being expelled, it means there is still qi in the stomach pushing them out. If I stabilize the stomach qi, the worms will settle down and the person can survive. The method used is the Anhuai Jiechan Decoction: one liang each of ginseng, poria, and cedar nuts, three qian of white peony, one qian of cinnamon, and five fen of shenjiao (fermented grain), boiled in water and taken as a single dose, and the ascaris will be regulated. This formula uses ginseng and poria to generate stomach qi and cedar nuts, white peony, and cinnamon to kill the worms, which is why it works so effectively.[151p]
    Pus and Blood Stains

    After childbirth, if one vomits pus and blood and develops spots, this is a condition that occurs in rare cases, and generally, it is not curable. However, if treated appropriately, some may survive. This condition arises because in the summer months, one is affected by the heat, which fails to dissipate. Upon giving birth, the internal fire and toxins become pronounced, and due to a weak constitution, the fire cannot fully manifest at once, leading to the vomiting of pus and blood, and causing spots to form on the body as a result of disarray.

    Typically, after childbirth, it is not advisable to use cooling medicines to treat spots, yet in such cases, one has no choice but to use cooling medicines as a temporary measure. I have now formulated a prescription called "Transforming Fire and Rescuing Childbirth Decoction": 1 tael each of Angelica and Scrophularia, 5 qian each of Ligusticum, Ginseng, and Ophiopogon, 3 qian of Schizonepeta, and 1 qian of Cimicifuga, to be decocted in water and taken. After one dose, the pus and blood will stop; after the second dose, the spots will lessen; and by the third dose, the spots will fade. Do not exceed three doses.

    After three doses, switch to "Buddha's Hand Powder", take large doses with plenty of fluids to prevent future complications. Otherwise, one risks developing a cold condition. My formula is not excessively cooling; nonetheless, it can turn cooling, so if one notices spots, it is unwise to immediately use yellow pharmacological agents to alleviate immediate issues, as that may lead to cold and result in death. Can cooling medicines be used lightly? Therefore, it is better to use my formula to gradually reduce the spots and slowly lower the blood issues, rather than relying on aggressive medicines for quick relief.[152p]
    Comprehensive Collection of Ancient and Modern Literature, Volume 392

    Postpartum Conditions in Women

        Formulas

Four Substance Decoction: This is commonly used to treat various ailments in women after childbirth, with adjustments as needed. The key ingredients are prepared as follows: Rehmannia, Ligusticum, Peony, and Angelica, with quantities adjusted as detailed later. decoct and consume with water. For postpartum agitation, add half a tael each of Poria and Polygala to the Four Substance Decoction. For postpartum stagnant blood and heart distress, add unconventional bark and Peony to the Four Substance Decoction. For postpartum tidal fever, add Atractylodes, Northern Bupleurum, Licorice, Peony, and unconventional bark to the Four Substance Decoction. If there is heat, add Scutellaria; if sweating is excessive, add floating wheat. For postpartum abdominal pain with blood clots attacking the intestines, add Mugwort, Myrrh, and good wine to the Four Substance Decoction. For postpartum eye conditions, add Northern Asarum, Notopterygium, Schizonepeta, Chrysanthemum, Licorice, Equisetum, and both types of Cinnabar to the Four Substance Decoction. For postpartum edema, breathlessness, abdominal distension, and a water-hen sound in the throat, add Peony, Schizonepeta, Atractylodes, Mulberry bark, red adzuki beans, large abdominal peel, apricot kernels, Pinellia, Aristolochia, ginger, scallion, and mint to the Four Substance Decoction. If there is a loss of speech or voice, add Terminalia, Ginseng, Sandalwood, and Hundred Herb Decoction to the Four Substance Decoction. For returning to health after childbirth and replenishing blood to treat various ailments, add ginger to the decoction for consumption. If postpartum blood clots do not disperse, or if blood loss is excessive with lingering lochia, add Cornus for decoction with the Four Substance Decoction.[153p]

    Yang organs require fewer uses of evodia; yin organs require more. For women after childbirth, Siwu Decoction can be taken once or twice daily. For postpartum dysentery and wind, Siwu Decoction should be supplemented with myrrh, dragon bone, evodia, costus root, cinnamon, astragalus, peony root, white atractylodes, ginseng, licorice, ze lan, black pepper, and patchouli. If postpartum shock causes qi stagnation and various accumulations of stagnant blood, with minimal evil substances within a month, leading to disease from stagnant blood that may cause bloating or pain in the chest and diaphragm, or chills and fevers with limb pain, blend Siwu Decoction with cayenne, myrrh, and white atractylodes into fine powder, and take with light vinegar soup or belted boy's urine liquor. If postpartum wind and evil arise, or if one develops a cold after childbirth with headache and fever, pains in the bones and joints, take one tael of Siwu Decoction, and add one fen each of schizonepeta, gastrodia, cyperus, gypsum, and patchouli; take three qian with one cup of water, simmering until reduced by a third. For postpartum fever and headache, add one tael of gypsum and half a tael of licorice to Siwu Decoction. For postpartum fatigue with fever and irritability, add fresh rehmannia to Siwu Decoction. For postpartum abdominal bloating, add three qian each of bitter orange and cinnamon to Siwu Decoction. If there is persistent abdominal pain following postpartum lochia, add peach kernel, red sandalwood, and eucommia to Siwu Decoction. For alternating chills and fever after childbirth, enhance Siwu Decoction with half a tael each of bupleurum and ophiopogon.[154p]
    Heishensan, also known as Wujinsan, is used to treat women suffering from postpartum issues such as persistent lochia, retained placenta, chest fullness, and abdominal pain. It addresses blood circulation problems with symptoms like faintness, blurred vision, and inability to speak. It treats various postpartum ailments.

    According to the commentary on Wujinsan, this formula addresses eighteen conditions that can arise after childbirth:

    1. Death of the fetus due to heat illness.
    2. Difficult labor.
    3. Retained placenta.
    4. Blood circulation issues.
    5. Dry mouth and chest discomfort.
    6. Alternating chills and fever.
    7. Numbness and swelling.
    8. Hallucinations.
    9. Inability to speak within the month after giving birth.
    10. Abdominal pain and diarrhea; combine with medication to stop diarrhea and regulate qi.
    11. Body aches.
    12. Excessive bleeding.
    13. Stagnation of blood and qi causing cough.
    14. Cold and heat causing chest pain, with menstruation delayed.
    15. Abdominal distention and vomiting; take Zhusha pills followed by a ginger vinegar decoction with seven pills every two to three days.
    16. Black discoloration around mouth and nose leading to nosebleeds; this condition cannot be treated.
    17. Wheezing and shortness of breath; cannot be treated if severe.
    18. Stroke.

    Ingredients include: 熟干地黄 (Rehmannia Root), 炒蒲黄 (Fried Typhae Pollen), 当归 (Angelica), 干姜灰 (Dried Ginger), 桂心 (Cinnamon Heart), 芍药 (Peony), 甘草 (Licorice) each four taels, and half a sheng of fried black beans with skins removed.

    These should be finely ground into a powder, with a dosage of two qian taken with half a cup each of wine and water, prepared as a decoction. This formula is particularly effective for those with a predominance of cold symptoms.[155p]
    Da Chengqi Decoction is used to treat postpartum excessive lochia, difficulty in defecation, restlessness, fever, a pulse that is slightly strong, worsening in the afternoon, loss of appetite, and when eating leads to confusion in speech, with improvement by night. It also treats gastrointestinal fullness.

    Ingredients:

    • 4 taels of Da Huang (Rhubarb)
    • ½ jin of Hou Po (Magnolia Bark), roasted
    • 5 pieces of Zhi Shi (Bitter Orange), roasted
    • 3 heaping doses of Mangxiao (Glauber’s Salt)

    Preparation: Use 10 sheng of water. First, boil the Hou Po and Zhi Shi, and take 5 sheng of the decoction, discarding the residue; then add the Da Huang and boil down to 2 sheng, removing the residue; finally, add the Mangxiao and bring to a gentle boil, allowing to warm, and administer. It can be adjusted according to bowel movements.[156p]
    Xiao Chai Hu Decoction is used to treat postpartum stagnation, difficulty in defecation, vomiting with inability to eat, excessive sweating, convulsions, exposure to wind, restlessness in the limbs, headaches, and other symptoms. It consists of three taels each of Huang Qin, Ren Shen, Gan Cao, and Sheng Jiang, half a jin of Chai Hu, half a sheng of Ban Xia, and twelve pieces of Da Zao. Boil with one dou and two sheng of water to obtain six sheng, strain, and then reboil to obtain three sheng. Warm and take one sheng, three times a day.

    Zhu Ye Decoction (from "Huo Ren Shu") is also known as Zhu Ye Fang Feng Decoction. It treats postpartum windstroke, fever, headache, and coughing with a flushed face. It consists of a handful of Zhu Ye, three taels of Ge Gen, one tael each of Fang Feng, Jie Geng, Gui Zhi, Ren Shen, Gan Cao, fifteen pieces of Da Zao, and five taels of Sheng Jiang. Chew and boil with one dou of water to obtain two and a half sheng, dividing it into three warm doses to induce sweating. If the neck is stiff, add one piece of Fu Zi broken to the size of a bean during the decoction, and skim off the foam. If there is vomiting, add half a sheng of Ban Xia. One version uses Gui Xin instead of Gui Zhi.[157p]
    Bai Tou Weng, Gan Cao, and Ejiao Decoction: Treats severe postpartum diarrhea. Ingredients: Bai Tou Weng, Gan Cao, and Ejiao, each 2 taels; Qin Pi, Huang Lian, and Bo Pi, each 2 taels. Combine these six ingredients, add 7 sheng of water, boil to obtain 2.5 sheng, then add Ejiao until completely dissolved. Divide into three doses to be taken warm.

    Da Huang and Gan Sui Decoction: Treats postpartum accumulation of water and blood in the blood chamber. Ingredients: Da Huang 4 taels; Gan Sui and Ejiao, each 2 taels. Combine these three ingredients, add 3 sheng of water, boil to obtain 1 sheng, take all at once; the blood should be expelled.

    Yang Dan Decoction (from "Huo Ren Shu"): Treats postpartum women with cold symptoms, fever, chills, sweating, dry vomiting, a firm feeling below the heart, slight headache, lasting over ten days. Ingredients: Gui Xin and Shao Yao, each 3 taels; Gan Cao and Huang Qin, each 2 taels. Grind to the size of millet, take 5 qian each time with 1.5 bowls of water, one jujube, and three slices of ginger, simmer until reduced to one bowl, take warm clear juice. If there is excessive sweating, omit Gui Xin; if vomiting, add one piece of fried Fu Zi. If thirsty, omit Gui Xin and add 3 taels of She Lou. If there is diarrhea, omit Shao Yao and Gui Xin, adding 3 taels of Gan Jiang. If there is palpitations, omit Shao Yao and add 4 taels of Fu Ling. For those with weakness and urgency in the interior, use Zheng Yang Dan Decoction, adding Ejiao and honey during preparation is preferable. If the pulse is floating, tight, without sweating and fever is present, it should not be administered.[158p]
    Lamb Soup

    Used to treat postnatal weakness, shortness of breath, excessive sweating, and abdominal cramps.

    Ingredients:

    • Trimmed fatty lamb meat, 3 pounds
    • Angelica, cassia heart, and licorice, each 2 taels
    • Ligusticum, 3 taels
    • Peony, ginger, each 4 taels
    • Rehmannia, 5 taels

    Instructions: Grind the above ingredients and add 1.5 dou of water. First, boil the meat and separate 7 sheng of broth, then add the remaining herbs and simmer to obtain 3 sheng of liquid. Strain out the dregs and divide into three doses. If symptoms do not improve, repeat the process.

    Alternative recipes:

    • One variant uses 1 pound of spring onion.
    • For slight internal heat, add 1 tael each of Huangqin and Mai Dong.
    • If bowel movements are irregular, add 1 tael of Dahuang.
    • For difficult urination, add 1 tael of Huizi.
    • For coughing or reversing qi, add 1 tael of Wuweizi.
    • Another variant omits Angelica and uses spring onion.
    • A variant omits Peony and uses spring onion.
    • A variant omits Ligusticum and uses 1 sheng of fermented soybeans.

    Lamb Astragalus Soup

    Used to treat postnatal fatigue.

    Ingredients:

    • Lamb meat, 2 pounds
    • Astragalus, 3 taels
    • 30 jujubes
    • Rehmannia, licorice, Angelica, cassia heart, Mai Dong, Poria, Peony, each 2 taels

    Instructions: Grind the ingredients and add 2 dou of water. Simmer the lamb to obtain 1 dou of broth, remove the meat, add all herbs, and simmer to extract 2 sheng of liquid. Strain out the dregs and divide into three doses to be taken three times a day.[159p]
    Deer Meat Soup treats postpartum fatigue and weakness.
    4 pounds of deer meat, 2 taels each of dried Rehmannia, licorice, angelica, astragalus, peony, Ophiopogon, Poria; 1 tael each of ginseng, angelica sinensis, ginger; 1 sheng of pinellia, and 20 jujubes. Boil with 2 dou 5 sheng of water, extract 1 dou 3 sheng of broth, remove the meat, add the herbs and simmer to obtain 5 sheng, strain out the residue, divide into four doses, taking three during the day and one at night. In an alternative formula, remove Ophiopogon and add domicile herb, gentian, Scutellaria, cinnamon, and donkey-hide gelatin to treat postpartum wind, fatigue, headaches, and fever.

    Zhang Bone Paste treats postpartum fatigue, exhaustion from overwork, insufficiency, and imbalance in the internal organs due to heat and cold.
    One deer bone, 3 taels each of Polygala, astragalus, peony, dried ginger, Siler, Poria, and magnolia bark; 2 taels each of angelica, citrus peel, licorice, and domicile herb, 4 taels each of cinnamon and ginger. Boil with 3 dou of water, extract 2 dou of broth from the deer bone, add the herbs and simmer to obtain 5 sheng, strain out the residue, and divide into five doses.

    Angelica and Peony Soup treats postpartum weakness and adverse effects from improper diet.
    1.5 taels of angelica, 1 tael each of peony, ginseng, cinnamon, ginger, dried Rehmannia, and licorice; 20 jujubes. Crush the ingredients, boil with 7 sheng of water, extract 3 sheng of broth, strain out the residue, and divide into three doses, taking three times a day until finished.[160p]
    Almond Soup: Cures postpartum deficiency and qi stagnation.
    Ingredients: 2 liang of bitter almonds, tangerine peel, white peony, and ginseng; 10 liang of fresh ginger; 1 sheng of perilla leaves and pinellia; 2 liang of ophiopogon; and 4 liang of cassia bark.
    Preparation: Use 12 sheng of water, boil until obtaining 2.5 sheng, strain, and divide into five doses.

    Milk and Honey Soup: Treats postpartum injuries from the seven injuries, deficiency, and insufficient qi; also treats kidney cold and fatigue.
    Ingredients: 7 sheng of cow’s milk (or goat’s milk if objectionable), 1.5 sheng of white honey; 3 liang each of angelica, ginseng, and ligusticum; 20 jujubes; and 2 liang each of licorice and cassia bark.
    Preparation: Grind all herbs, add to the milk and honey, boil to obtain 3 sheng, strain, and divide into four doses.

    Five Stone Decoction: Treats postpartum cold deficiency and the seven injuries, addresses chills and fevers, body pain, fatigue, nourishes the kidneys, and treats various ailments.
    Ingredients: 2 liang each of amethyst, belladonna, white quartz, red lead stone, gypsum, poria, white atractylodes, cassia, and ligusticum; 6 liang of garlic chives; 1 liang each of ginseng and angelica; 8 liang of fresh ginger; and 20 jujubes.
    Preparation: Grind the five stones into powder, chop all herbs, use 12 sheng of water, boil to obtain 3 sheng, strain, and divide into six doses. For stroke, add 2 liang each of kudzu root and ligusticum; for diarrhea, add 1 liang of dragon bone.[161p]
    Three Stone Decoction: For the same symptoms as before.
    Ingredients: Purple quartz, fresh ginger, angelica, ginseng, licorice - each 120 grams; white quartz, zongru - each 75 grams; poria, dried rehmannia, cinnamon - each 120 grams; pinellia 250 grams; 15 jujubes. Grind the three stones into powder. Combine with the other ingredients, add 15 liters of water, boil down to 3 liters, strain, and divide into four doses. If treating a stroke, add 240 grams of kudzu root.

    Bamboo Leaf Decoction: For postpartum heart vexation that is unresolved.
    Ingredients: Fresh pale bamboo leaves, ophiopogon - each 240 ml; licorice 80 grams; fresh ginger, poria - each 120 grams; 14 jujubes; 450 grams of wheat. Boil the bamboo leaves and wheat in 15 liters of water, take 8 liters, add the other herbs, and boil down to 3 liters, strain, and divide into three doses. If there are symptoms of heart palpitations, add 120 grams of ginseng. If the person has little appetite and no gas, add 450 grams of japonica rice. If there is reversed energy, add 120 grams of pinellia.[162p]
    Danzhuru Decoction: Treats postpartum weakness and restlessness, headache, shortness of breath, and disturbances in the heart and mind. Ingredients: 1 sheng of Danzhuru, each 5 he of Maidong and wheat, 1 liang of licorice, 3 liang of fresh ginger, and 14 dates. Combine with 10 dou of water, boil Danzhuru and wheat to extract 8 sheng, then remove the dregs. Add all other herbs and boil to extract 2 sheng, removing dregs again. Divide into two doses; those weak should take it in three parts. If ginseng is included, use 2 liang; if ginseng is not included, use 1.5 liang of Poria. Both ginseng and Poria treat restlessness of the heart and mental disturbances, calming the spirit. If symptoms persist after one dose, prepare another. If there is qi counterflow, add 2 liang of Banxia. Alternative: omit ginger and use dried kudzu. Another alternative: omit dates and use 3 liang of gypsum.

    Shuqi Decoction: Treats postpartum heat, restlessness in the chest, joint pain, headache, and fever, especially worse in the afternoon, resembling mild malaria. Ingredients: 1 liang of Shuqi leaves, cinnamon heart, licorice, and Huangqin; 2 liang of Zhimu and Shaoyao; 5 liang of Huangqi and 1 jin of Shengdi. Combine with 10 dou of water, boil to extract 3 sheng, and divide into three doses.

    Shaoyao Decoction: Treats postpartum heat with headache and abdominal cramping. Ingredients: 5 liang each of white peony, dry Rehmannia, and oyster shells; 3 liang of cinnamon heart. Combine with 10 dou of water, boil to extract 2.5 sheng, remove dregs, and divide into three doses to be taken throughout the day. If there is overall heat, add 2 liang of Huangqin.[163p]
    Licorice Decoction: Treats wind stroke in the sickroom, with a strong back that cannot turn, called wind spasm. Ingredients: Licorice, Radix Rehmanniae, Ophiopogon, and Ephedra, each ten taels; Chuanxiong, (Herbal name), Huangqin, each three taels; fifty pieces of apricot kernels, and half a pound of Pueraria. These nine ingredients should be chewed, boiled with one and a half dou of water and five sheng of wine, then cook Pueraria to obtain eight sheng, strain, add all the medicines, boil to obtain three sheng, strain, and divide for two doses. An alternative formula includes three taels of Qianhu.

    Duhuo Decoction: Treats postpartum wind stroke where the mouth is locked and cannot speak. Ingredients: Duhuo, and fresh ginger, each five taels; Fangfeng, Qinghao, Gui heart, Baizhu, Licorice, Danggui, Fuzi, Pueraria, each two taels; Fangji, one tael. These should be boiled in one dou and two sheng of water to obtain three sheng, strain, and divide for three doses.

    Duhuo Wine: Treats postpartum wind stroke. Ingredients: One pound of Duhuo, three taels of Gui heart, and five taels of Qinghao. These should be soaked in one and a half dou of wine for three days, drink five he, gradually increasing to one liter; do not drink in excess, take as desired.

    Minor Duhuo Decoction: Treats wind obstruction a hundred days postpartum, where the mouth cannot open; also treats blood and qi pain and kidney deficiency from overwork. Ingredients: Half a pound of Duhuo, six taels each of Pueraria and fresh ginger, and two taels of Licorice. These should be boiled in nine sheng of water to obtain three sheng, strain, and divide for four doses; a slight sweat is beneficial.[164p]
    Five Stones Decoction: Treats postpartum stroke, sudden onset of illness with locked jaw, foaming at the mouth, convulsions, dizziness and confusion; also for damp bi syndrome, weakness, bodily spasms, and various diseases in pregnancy. Ingredients: Zhongru, red stone resin, gypsum, white quartz, oyster, ginseng, huangqin (Scutellaria baicalensis), baizhu (Atractylodes macrocephala), licorice, (Herba Polygoni) luogen root, chuanxiong (Ligusticum chuanxiong), guixin (Cinnamon), fangji (Stephania tetrandra), danggui (Angelica sinensis), and dried ginger, each 1 liang; ge gen (Pueraria lobata) 4 liang; du huo (Angelica duhuo), purple quartz, each 3 liang. Grind the five stones into powder, add the above herbs, with 1 dou 4 sheng of water, boil to get 2.5 sheng, divide into five doses, taken three times a day and twice at night. An alternative recipe includes 2 liang each of talc and karst stone, and twenty jujubes.

    Four Stones Decoction: Treats postpartum stroke, sudden onset of illness with locked jaw, convulsions, confusion and fullness; also for acute and chronic wind conditions, toxic bi syndrome, and bodily spasms; as well as wind ailments during pregnancy and various women's diseases. Ingredients: Purple quartz, white quartz, gypsum, red stone resin, each 3 liang; du huo (Angelica duhuo), fresh ginger, each 6 liang; ge gen (Pueraria lobata) 4 liang; cinnamon 7; chuanxiong (Ligusticum chuanxiong), licorice, peony (Paeonia), huangqin (Scutellaria baicalensis), each 2 liang. Grind these herbs, add to 1 dou 2 sheng of water, boil to obtain 3.5 sheng, then strain and divide into five doses.

    Three Herbs Huangqin Decoction: Treats women who catch wind while lying down, with irritability and heat in the limbs, but without headaches. Ingredients: Huangqin (Scutellaria baicalensis), bitter ginseng, each 2 liang; dry rehmannia 4 liang.[165p]
    Here is the translated text:

    Take eight sheng of water, boil down to two sheng, strain out the dregs, and mix with one sheng for cold or warm consumption, taking it twice daily; it often induces vomiting or expels worms.

    Fangfeng Wine: Treats postpartum stroke.
    Fangfeng and Duhuo, each two jin; Shihu five liang; Nüwei, Guixin, and Yinyi, each one liang. Combine with three dou of wine, soak for three nights, initially take one ge, gradually increase to three or four ge, twice a day.

    Fushen Decoction: Treats sudden heart distress postpartum, or uncertain thoughts, muddled perception, and erroneous speech caused by heart deficiency.
    Fushen four liang; Ren Shen, Fu Ling, Shao Yao, Gan Cao, Dang Gui, Guixin, each three liang; Sheng Jiang eight liang; thirty Da Zao. Combine with one dou of water, boil down to three sheng, strain out the dregs, divide into three doses, take twice a day and once at night.

    Yuan Zhi Decoction: Treats sudden heart distress postpartum, uncertain thoughts, erroneous speech, confusion, and unawareness of feelings.
    Shao Yao one liang; Yuan Zhi, Mai Dong, Ren Shen, Gan Cao, Dang Gui, Guixin, each two liang; Fu Ling five liang; Sheng Jiang six liang; thirty Da Zao. Combine with one dou of water, boil down to three sheng, strain out the dregs, divide into three doses, take three times a day; for those who are weak, take four doses. This condition postpartum is due to heart deficiency. If there is no Dang Gui, use Xiong Dan instead. If the person has counterflow qi in the heart, use Sanxia three liang.[166p]
    Fu Ling Decoction treats postpartum severe heart palpitations that are unstable, with erroneous speech, a dazed state, and a feeling of confusion in the heart, all caused by a deficiency of the heart.

    Ingredients:

    • Poria 250 grams
    • Licorice 100 grams
    • Peony 100 grams
    • Cinnamon heart 100 grams
    • Angelica 100 grams
    • Fresh ginger 300 grams
    • Ophiopogon 1 liter
    • 30 dates

    Prepare by boiling with 10 liters of water until you obtain 3 liters, then strain it. Divide into three doses to be taken three times a day. If Angelica is unavailable, you may use Szechuan lovage. If heart palpitations are severe, add 100 grams of ginseng and 100 grams of Polygala. If there is restlessness with shortness of breath, add 1 liter of fresh bamboo leaves, using 10.3 liters of water; boil the bamboo leaves to extract 10 liters and mix with the medicine. If there is mild cold, add 150 grams of Angelica and 100 grams each of Ephedra and Cinnamon heart, using 15 liters of water. If there’s tightness in the neck and back, add 150 grams each of Angelica and Kudzu, and 100 grams each of Ephedra and Cinnamon heart, along with 400 grams of fresh ginger, using 15 liters of water.

    Calming the Heart Decoction treats heart palpitations and instability postpartum, causing confusion and unawareness, erroneous speech, restlessness with shortness of breath, unstable emotions, all stemming from heart deficiency.

    Ingredients:

    • Polygala and Licorice each 100 grams
    • Ginseng, Poria, Angelica, Peony each 150 grams
    • Ophiopogon 1 liter
    • 30 dates

    Prepare by boiling with 10 liters of water until obtaining 3 liters, then strain it. Divide into three doses to be taken three times a day. If there is significant restlessness with shortness of breath, add 200 grams of light bamboo leaves, using 12.2 liters of water; boil the bamboo leaves to obtain 10 liters and mix with the medicine. If there is a lack of energy in the chest, increase Licorice to 150 grams.[167p]
    Gancao Pills treat postpartum heart deficiency, insufficiency, palpitations, anxiety of the heart and spirit, shortness of breath, or if there is disorientation and unawareness. The ingredients are: Gancao, Yuan Zhi, and Ao Po each three taels; Ren Shen, Mai Men Dong, Gan Jiang, and Fu Ling each two taels; Ze Xie and Gui Xin each one tael; fifty jujubes. Mix into a fine powder, and with honey roll into pills the size of beans. Take twenty pills with wine, four to five times a day, and twice at night, slightly increasing as needed. If Ze Xie is unavailable, substitute with Bai Zhu. If there is internal cold, increase Gan Jiang.

    Ren Shen Pills treat severe postpartum deficiency, palpitations, anxiety, disorientation, fear, sleeplessness at night, and restlessness with little energy. The ingredients are: Ren Shen, Gancao, and Fu Ling each three taels; Mai Dong, Ao Po, Ze Xie, Shu Yu, and Gan Jiang each two taels; fifty jujubes; and one tael of Gui Xin. Mix into a fine powder, and with jujube paste roll into pills the size of a carp. Take twenty pills with wine before meals, three times a day, and once at night, slightly increasing as needed. If Yuan Zhi is available, incorporate two taels. If there are symptoms of wind, add three taels of Dang Gui and Du Huo.[168p]
    Great Distant Aspirations
    Treats postnatal deficiency, with anxiety and palpitations, a restless spirit, a disoriented mind, abdominal cramping pain, restless nights, and shallow breathing. It nourishes and stabilizes the heart and spirit; it also treats deficiencies.
    Ingredients:

    • Dried Ginger: 4 taels
    • Distant Aspirations
    • Licorice
    • Cinnamon Heart
    • Poria
    • Ophiopogon
    • Ginseng
    • Angelica
    • White Atractylodes
    • Alisma
    • Notopterygium
    • Dried Rehmannia: 3 taels each
    • Dioscorea
    • Ass Hide Gelatin: 2 taels each

    Grind the above into powder, mix with honey, and form into pills the size of beans. Take 30 pills with warm wine on an empty stomach, three times a day, increasing gradually if needed. If there is great deficiency with body cold and slight body fluids, add 3 taels of Cow Milk.

    Dried Rehmannia Decoction
    Treats pain and fullness in both sides after childbirth, also alleviates various ailments.
    Ingredients:

    • Dried Rehmannia: 3 taels
    • Peony: 3 taels
    • Angelica: 2 taels
    • Cattail Pollen: 2 taels
    • Fresh Ginger: 5 taels
    • Cinnamon Heart: 6 taels
    • Licorice: 1 tael
    • Dates: 20 pieces

    Boil with 10 servings of water until 1.5 servings remain, strain, and divide into three doses to be taken three times a day.

    Peony Decoction
    Treats postpartum abdominal pain.
    Ingredients:

    • Peony: 6 taels
    • Cinnamon Heart: 3 taels
    • Fresh Ginger: 3 taels
    • Licorice: 2 taels
    • Honey Syrup: 8 taels
    • Dates: 12 pieces

    Boil with 7 servings of water until 4 servings remain, strain, melt in the honey syrup, and divide into three doses to be taken three times a day.[169p]
    Angelica Soup: Treats postpartum extreme weakness, sharp abdominal pain, and cold extremities.
    Ingredients: 2 taels of Angelica, 2 taels of Peony, 5 taels of fresh ginger, 1 pound of lamb.
    Preparation: Use 8 sheng of water. Cook the lamb until tender, taking the broth to decoct the herbs to yield 3 sheng. Adjust for coldness and warmth, take 7 xiaos (approximately a serving), up to three times. One variation includes licorice. Another variation omits Peony, named Minor Lamb Soup.

    Lamb Soup: Treats postpartum and severe cold-induced extreme weakness, shortness of breath, abdominal pain, and mild wind.
    Ingredients: 2 pounds of fatty lamb, 3 taels each of Poria, Astragalus, and dried ginger, 2 taels each of licorice, Angelica, cinnamon root, and ginseng, 7 xiaos of Ophiopogon, 5 taels of Rehmannia, and 12 dried dates.
    Preparation: Chop all ingredients, use 2 dou of water, cook the meat to yield 1 dou, remove the meat and boil the herbs to get 2.5 sheng, divided into four doses, taken thrice during the day and once at night. One variation omits lamb, and substitutes with venison. Another variation omits dried ginger.

    Lamb Angelica Soup: Treats postpartum abdominal pain, sharp pain below the heart, inability to eat, alternating chills and fever as from a stroke, and fatigue.
    Ingredients: 3 pounds of lamb, 3 taels each of Angelica, Scutellaria, Chuanxiong, Fangfeng, licorice, and Peony, and 4 taels of fresh ginger.
    Preparation: Use 1 dou and 2 sheng of water, first cook and reduce to half. Add the remaining herbs to obtain 3 sheng, strain, and divide into three servings, taken three times a day until finished. One variation substitutes Astragalus for Scutellaria and Atractylodes for Peony, named Major Lamb Soup. Another variation replaces Fangfeng with cinnamon root and adds 17 dried dates. Another alternative swaps Astragalus for Scutellaria and ginseng for Fangfeng.[170p]
    Lamb and Eucommia Tea: Treats back pain and cough after childbirth.
    4 pounds of lamb, sautéed Eucommia, 3 taels each of Aster root, Angelica, Atractylodes, Cinnamon heart, 8 taels of ginger, and 2 taels each of Asarum, winter flower, ginseng, Magnolia, Chuanxiong, Schisandra, Fuzi, Achyranthes, licorice, and Astragalus; 30 jujubes. Use 1.5 dou of water, boil the meat to extract 1 dou of broth, remove the meat, stew the herbs to obtain 3.5 sheng, filter out residue, divide into five doses, take three times a day and twice at night.

    Lamb and Rehmannia Tea: Treats abdominal pain three days post childbirth, replenishes the center, benefits the organs, strengthens energy, dissipates blood stasis.
    3 pounds of lamb, 2 sheng of raw Rehmannia cut, 2 taels each of Angelica, licorice, Chuanxiong, ginseng, and Cinnamon heart, 3 taels of peony. Use 2 dou of water, boil the meat to get 1 dou of broth, remove the meat, stew the herbs for 3 sheng, divide into four doses, take three times a day and once at night.

    Inner Nourishment Chuanxiong Tea: Treats women who are weak and emaciated after childbirth.
    4 taels each of Chuanxiong and dried Rehmannia, 5 taels of peony, 2 taels of Cinnamon heart, 3 taels each of licorice and dried ginger, 40 jujubes. Use 1 dou and 2 sheng of water, boil to obtain 3 sheng, filter out residue, divide into three doses, take three times in one day until finished. If experiencing cold after three doses, mildly down, add 3 taels of Fuzi.[171p]

    Great Nourishment Angelica Decoction

    This is used to treat postpartum deficiency and insufficiency, abdominal cramping, or blood in the urine with lower abdominal pain, or internal injuries from a fall from a height.
    Ingredients:

    • Angelica (Dang Gui) - 3 taels
    • Chinese Teasel (Xu Duan) - 3 taels
    • Cinnamon (Gui) - 3 taels
    • Ligusticum (Xiang Fu) - 3 taels
    • Dried Ginger (Gan Jiang) - 3 taels
    • Ophiopogon (Mai Dong) - 3 taels
    • Licorice (Gan Cao) - 2 taels
    • White Atractylodes (Bai Zhi) - 2 taels
    • Peony (Shao Yao) - 4 taels
    • Evodia (Wu Zhu Yu) - 1 sheng
    • Rehmannia (Gan Di Huang) - 6 taels
    • Dates (Da Zao) - 40 pieces

    Preparation: Steep the herbs in one dou of wine overnight; the next morning, add one dou of water, boil to extract five sheng, strain, divide into five doses, take three doses during the day and two at night.

    Evodia Decoction

    This treats women who have previously experienced coldness, chest fullness and pain, or sharp pain in the heart and abdomen, or vomiting with little food, or swelling or coldness, or diarrhea, with faint breathing nearing cessation, worsened after childbirth.
    Ingredients:

    • Evodia (Wu Zhu Yu) - 3 taels
    • Siler (Fang Feng) - 12 zhu
    • Platycodon (Jie Geng) - 12 zhu
    • Dried Ginger (Gan Jiang) - 12 zhu
    • Licorice (Gan Cao) - 12 zhu
    • Asarum (Xi Xin) - 12 zhu
    • Angelica (Dang Gui) - 12 zhu
    • Rehmannia (Gan Di Huang) - 18 taels

    Preparation: Boil the herbs in four sheng of water to extract one and a half sheng, strain, and divide into two doses.

    Cattail Pollen Decoction

    This is for postpartum lingering ailments, with slight shortness of breath, abdominal pain, headache, residual blood not yet resolved, and abdominal distention.
    Ingredients:

    • Cattail Pollen (Pu Huang) - 5 taels
    • Rehmannia (Di Huang) - 5 taels
    • Fresh Ginger (Sheng Jiang) - 5 taels
    • Glauber’s Salt (Mang Xiao) - 2 taels
    • Ligusticum (Xiang Fu) - 1 tael
    • Cinnamon Heart (Gui Xin) - 1 tael
    • Peach Kernels (Tao Ren) - 20 pieces
    • Dates (Da Zao) - 15 pieces

    Preparation: Boil in nine sheng of water to extract two and a half sheng, strain, add Glauber’s salt, and divide into three doses, taking three times a day until finished.[172p]
    Stubborn Sauce Soup: Treats postpartum abdominal pain radiating to the waist, with sensations like being pierced by a needle or knife.

    Ingredients:

    • 3 taels of Stubborn Sauce
    • 7 pieces of Cinnamon
    • 1.5 taels each of Chuanxiong and Angelica
    • 1 tael of each ingredient

    Preparation:
    Use 2 sheng of clear wine and 4 sheng of water, boil gently to obtain 2 sheng, strain, mix with 7.5 ounce of warm water, take three times a day before meals.

    Chuanxiong Soup: Treats postpartum abdominal pain.

    Ingredients:

    • 2 taels each of Chuanxiong and Licorice
    • 1.5 taels each of Puhuang and Nüwei
    • 3 zhu each of Peony and Rhubarb
    • 18 taels of Angelica
    • 1 tael each of Cinnamon, Peach Seed, Astragalus, and Qianhu
    • 1 sheng of Raw Rehmannia

    Preparation:
    Use 10 sheng of water and 2 sheng of wine, combine and boil to obtain 3 sheng, strain, divide into four doses, take three times a day and once at night. One variation uses Huangqin instead of Huangqi.

    Dried Rehmannia Soup: Treats postpartum excessive lochia, removes various ailments, and replenishes deficiencies.

    Ingredients:

    • 2 taels each of Dried Rehmannia, Chuanxiong, Cinnamon, Astragalus, and Angelica
    • 1 tael each of Ginseng, Fangfeng, Poria, Xixin, Peony, and Licorice

    Preparation:
    Use 10 sheng of water, boil to obtain 3 sheng, strain, divide into three doses, take twice a day and once at night.

    Peach Seed Soup: Treats fluctuating cold and heat postpartum and excessive lochia.

    Ingredients:

    • 5 taels of Peach Seed
    • 2 taels each of Astragalus, Angelica, and Peony
    • 8 taels each of Fresh Ginger, Compound Digestive Lard, and Chaihu
    • 2 sheng of Wu Zhu Yu

    Preparation:
    Use 10 sheng of wine and 2 sheng of water, combine and boil to obtain 3 sheng, strain, mix with warm water, take 1 sheng three times a day.[173p]
    Zelan Decoction: Treats lingering lochia after childbirth, persistent abdominal pain, urgent pain in the lower abdomen, pain radiating to the waist and back, and shortness of breath. Ingredients: 3 taels of Ze Lan, 3 taels of Dong Gui, 3 taels of Di Huang, 3 taels of fresh Ginger, 1 tael of Shao Yao, 1.5 taels of Gan Cao, and 10 dates. Combine with 9 sheng of water, boil down to 3 sheng, strain, and divide into three doses to be taken three times a day until finished.

    Gan Cao Decoction: Treats residual blood after childbirth, reversed rushing to the heart and chest, cold extremities, dry fetus, abdominal distension, and shortness of breath. Ingredients: 3 taels of Gan Cao, 3 taels of Shao Yao, 3 taels of Gui Xin, and 3 taels of Ejiao, plus 4 taels of Da Huang. Combine with 10 dou of flowing water, boil down to 3 sheng, strain, dissolve Ejiao in it, and take in two doses. Upon taking the first dose, the face will immediately regain color. Finish the 3 sheng within one day and night, which will expel 1 to 2 sheng of bad blood from the lower abdomen, leading to immediate recovery. It should be treated as if it were a new childbirth.

    Da Huang Decoction: Treats persistent lochia after childbirth. Ingredients: 1 sheng of Wu Zhu Yu, 3 taels each of Da Huang, Dong Gui, Gan Cao, fresh Ginger, Dan Pi, and Shao Yao. Combine with 1 dou of water, boil down to 4 sheng, strain, and divide into four doses to be taken in one day until finished. One variation adds 2 taels of Ren Shen, and is called Ren Shen Da Huang Decoction.[174p]
    Chai Hu Decoction: This treats postpartum alternating chills and fever, and persistent lochia. Use 8 taels of Chai Hu and fresh ginger, 50 pieces of peach kernel, and 3 taels each of angelica, astragalus, and peony. Also, use 2 sheng of Wu Zhu Yu. Combine with 10.5 liters of water, boil down to 3 liters, strain, and take 1 liter before meals, three times a day.

    Pu Huang Decoction: This treats residual ailments after childbirth, with retained blood, distended abdomen, shortness of breath, inability to eat, heart and flank pressure, frequent irritability, and pain in hands and feet, along with heat accumulation in the stomach. Use 10 taels of Pu Huang, and 1 tael each of rhubarb, Glauber's salt, licorice, and Huang Qin, along with 30 large dates. Combine with 2.5 liters of water, boil down to 1 liter, and consume in the morning until noon. If symptoms persist, stop with half a bowl of cold porridge. If it doesn’t work, take some warm drink instead. For weak individuals, cut the dosage in half.

    Tong Jing Bi Decoction: This treats residual ailments after childbirth, with retained blood causing problems, and stagnation of qi leading to pain in the chest and abdomen. Use 18 zhū of burnt copper mirror powder, 2 taels each of peony and cyperus, fresh rehmannia, varnish, and Glauber's salt, and 2.5 taels of rhubarb, along with 30 large dates. Combine with 3.5 liters of water, boil down to 1 liter, strain, and mix in the burnt copper mirror powder, dividing into three doses.[175p]
    Small Copper Mirror Nasal Decoction: Treats conditions as previously described.
    Take ten zhū of copper mirror nose, ground to a powder. Two liang each of rhubarb, licorice, scutellaria, mirabilite, dried rehmannia, and fifty pieces of peach kernel. Combine with six sheng of wine, boil down to three sheng, strain, add the mirror nose powder, and divide into three doses.

    Gardenia Decoction: Treats lingering bleeding and pain in the lower abdomen after childbirth.
    Thirty pieces of gardenia fruit, two liang each of angelica, peony, five he of honey, and five liang of fresh ginger, and one liang of sheep fat. Use one dou of water, boil the gardenia to extract six sheng, then add angelica, peony, ginger, honey, and sheep fat to the gardenia juice, decoct to obtain two sheng. Take three doses in one day until finished.

    Fresh Dihuang Decoction: Treats lingering blood in the abdomen from three to seven days postpartum, with severe abdominal pain and obstructed breathing.
    Five liang of fresh rehmannia, three liang of fresh ginger, one liang and a half each of rhubarb, peony, poria, asarum, cassia, angelica, licorice, and scutellaria, and twenty pieces of jujube. Combine with eight sheng of water, boil down to two and a half sheng, strain, and divide into three doses to be taken three times daily until finished.[176p]
    Dahuang Ganqi Decoction: Treats blood after childbirth, with severe abdominal pain.
    Ingredients: Dahuang, Ganqi, Gandi Huang, Guixin, Ganjiao, each 2 liang.
    Method: Use 3 sheng of water and 5 sheng of clear wine, boil to yield 3 sheng, strain, and take 1 sheng warm. The blood should be expelled. If not healed, take another 1 sheng the next morning. Repeat for three doses; the illness will be completely healed.

    Jiao La Decoction: Treats various colors of diarrhea within three days after childbirth.
    Ingredients: Ejiao, Huangbo, each 1 liang; Danggui, 1.5 liang; Huanlian, 2 liang; Chenlin rice, 1 sheng; wax (like chess pieces), three pieces.
    Method: Use 8 sheng of water, boil rice until bubbling, remove rice, then boil to yield 2 sheng, strain, add Ejiao and wax until melted, divide into four doses to be taken within one day.

    Zelan Decoction: Treats lingering symptoms after childbirth, with cold, frozen pus, abdominal urgency with alternating cold and heat in the chest, yellowish urine, and constipation.
    Ingredients: Zelan, Shigao, each 24 zhu; Yuan Zhi, 20 zhu; Danggui, Gan Cao, Houpo, each 18 zhu; Gao Ben, Xiongshao, each 15 zhu; Ganjiao, Renshen, Jiegeng, Gandi Huang, each 12 zhu; Sangpi, Mazi Ren, each 0.5 jin; Baizhu, Shupepper, Baizhi, Baizi Ren, Fangfeng, Shanzhuyu, Xixin, each 8 zhu.
    Method: Use 1 dou 5 sheng of water, first boil Sangbai Pi to yield 7.5 sheng, remove, then add all other herbs, boil to yield 3.5 sheng, strain, and divide into three doses.[177p]
    Luo Decoction: Treats frequent urination and thirst after childbirth. Ingredients: 2 liang each of Luo root, Ophiopogon, and Licorice; 2 liang each of Ginseng and Fresh Ginger; 15 dates; 20 pieces of silkworm excrement. Combine with 7 sheng of water, boil to obtain 2.5 sheng, and divide into three doses.

    Chicken Liver Soup: Treats difficulty in urination after childbirth. Ingredients: 2-3 chicken livers, 3 chicken intestines (washed); 2 liang each of Magnolia Bark, Angelica, Licorice, Rehmannia, and Ginseng; 5 liang of Fresh Ginger; 4 liang of Ephedra; 20 dates. Combine with 10 sheng of water, boil with the livers, intestines, and dates to obtain 7 sheng, filter, then add all other herbs and simmer to yield 3 sheng, divided into three doses.

    Stone Rush Decoction: Treats sudden dysuria after childbirth, including qi, blood, and stone dysuria. Ingredients: 2 sheng of Thistle Seed; 2 liang each of Stone Rush, Huangqin, Tongcao, Licorice, and Bai Zhu; 5 liang of Elm Bark; 30 dates. Combine with 8 sheng of water, boil to obtain 1.5 sheng, divided into three doses. One version uses Peony and omits Bai Zhu. Another version has no Licorice or Fresh Ginger. A third version omits Ginger and dates.[178p]
    Kuygen Decoction: Treats postpartum dripping.

    • 2 liang of mullein root, 1 sheng of plantago seed, some burnt hair ash, 1 liang each of rhubarb, cassia twig, and talc, 3 liang of beardless grass, 6 liang of fresh ginger, and 7 he of winter melon juice. Boil with 7 sheng of water until reduced to 2.5 sheng. Divide into three doses. (One version does not use winter melon juice.)

    Mao Gen Decoction: Treats postpartum dripping.

    • 1 jin of white mao root, 4 liang each of coix seed and poria, 2 liang each of land wheat and ginseng, 3 liang of fresh ginger, and 1 liang each of peach gum and licorice, plus 100 carp teeth. Boil with 10 dou of water until reduced to 2.5 sheng. Divide into three doses.

    Talc Powder: Treats postpartum dripping.

    • 5 liang of talc, 4 qian each of beardless grass, plantago seed, and mullein seed. Mix these four ingredients, finely sieve, and take with a little vinegar water, starting with a pinch and gradually increasing to two pinches.

    Wujia Wine: Treats postpartum emaciation and coldness in the abdomen.

    • 1 jin of five-flavor bark, 1 liang each of snake bed fruit and eucommia, 2 liang each of goji berries, dried rehmannia, and salvia, 3 liang of dried ginger, 0.5 jin of cow's milk, and 4 liang of asparagus root. Place in a silk bag and soak in 3 sheng of wine for three nights. Take 5 he per dose twice daily, gradually increasing to 10 he.[179p]
      Angelica Decoction: Treats postpartum wind in the viscera, with swelling and pain in the lower regions.
      Ingredients: Angelica, Duhuo, Bai Zhi, Dihu, each three taels; Baijiao, Alumen, each two taels. Boil with one and a half dou of water to obtain five sheng. Use slightly warm water to wash the lower regions, three times daily.

    Increased and Decreased Ze Lan Pills: Treats a hundred diseases after childbirth, regulates blood and qi, and replenishes deficiency and fatigue.
    Ingredients: Ze Lan, Licorice, Angelica, Chuanxiong, each forty-two zhui; Houpu, Jiao Ben, Wutou, each half tael; Fuzi, Dried Ginger, Bai Zhu, Bai Zhi, Gui Xin, Xixin, Mai Dong, each one tael; Fangfeng, Ren Shen, Niu Xi, each thirty zhui; Bai Zi Ren, Gan Di Huang, Shi Hu, each thirty-six zhui. Grind to a fine powder, mix with honey to form pills the size of a walnut. Take fifteen to twenty pills on an empty stomach or with wine.

    Great Replenishing Angelica Pills: Treats postpartum weakness and insufficient energy, with little breath in the chest, abdominal tension and pain, or pain radiating to the lower back, or excessive vaginal discharge, with continuous bleeding and energy depletion, insomnia day and night.
    Ingredients: Cinnamon, Peony, each two taels; Angelica, Chuanxiong, Xu Duan, Dried Ginger, Ejiao, Licorice, each four taels; Bai Zhu, Wuzhuyu, Fuzi, Bai Zhi, each three taels; Dried Di Huang, ten taels. Grind to a fine powder, mix with honey to form pills the size of a walnut. Take twenty pills with wine, three times a day, and once at night, can increase to fifty pills. One variant adds one sheng of True Puhuang for exceptional effects.[180p]
    Zelan San
    For treating postpartum wind deficiency.
    Ingredients: 9 parts Zelan, Dried Rehmannia, Gypsum, Red Resin, Raifort (Muscari), Deer Antler, White Atractylodes, and Chuanxiong, each 8 parts; Galls, Sichuan Pepper, Bai Zhu, each 5 parts; Cinnamon, Licorice, and Angelica, each 7 parts; Mugwort, Chuanxiong, and Houpo, each 4 parts; Ginseng 3 parts; Yu Yu Liang and Fangfeng, each 10 parts.
    These 22 ingredients are to be ground finely, taken with wine as a spoonful, thrice daily, increasing as desired.

    Xiao Zelan Yuan
    For treating postpartum weakness, fatigue, and cold, with emaciation.
    Ingredients: 2 taels 6 qian of Zelan, 1 tael 18 qian each of Angelica and Licorice; 1 tael each of Chuanxiong, Bai Zi Ren, Fangfeng, and Poria; 18 qian each of White Atractylodes, Sichuan Pepper, Galls, Chuanxiong, Bai Zhu, Cinnamon, Mugwort, Ginseng, and Food Mukdenia; 2 taels of Gypsum.
    Grind these into a fine powder, mix with honey to form pills the size of walnuts, take 20 pills with wine, thrice daily, gradually increasing to 40 pills. For a version without Poria and Gypsum, substitute with Peony and Dried Ginger.[181p]
    Mudan Yuan: For women after childbirth, if the blood stasis does not dissipate, after taking various decoctions to promote blood flow and if residual ailments remain, this formula should be taken.

    Ingredients:

    • Three liang of Mu Dan Pi (tree peony root)
    • Two liang each of Shao Yao (peony root), Yuan Shen (scrophularia root), and Tao Ren (peach kernel, ground)
    • Two liang of Dang Gui (angelica), Gui Xin (cinnamon heart)
    • Fifty each of Feng Chong (red worm) and Shui Zhi (leeches)
    • One liang each of Qi Xiao (larva), Qu Mai (dichroa), Chuan Xiong (sichuan lovage), and Hai Zao (seaweed)

    Grind these into a powder, mix with honey to form pills the size of a jujube, take fifteen pills with wine, increasing to twenty. If there is a lot of blood, use as a powder and take with a spoonful; the abdomen should feel like it is boiling, and the blood will naturally transform into water. If urination is scant and red, omit Gui Xin and use one liang of Di Fu Zi (ground plantain).

    Yu Yu Liang Yuan: For treating women post-childbirth with accumulated cold causing hard lumps.

    Ingredients:

    • Yu Yu Liang (a type of grain)
    • Bones of lizi fish
    • Wu Zhu Yu (fresh zanthoxylum)
    • Gui Xin (cinnamon heart)
    • One liang of (upside down silkworm)
    • Two and a half liang of Shu Jiao (pepper)
    • One liang and six zhu each of Dang Gui, Bai Zhu (Atractylodes), Xi Xin (asarum), Gan Di Huang (rehmannia), Ren Shen (ginseng), Shao Yao, Chuan Xiong, and Qian Hu (peucedanum)
    • Eighteen zhu each of Bai Wei (stemona), Zi Wan (aster), and Huang Qin (baical skullcap)
    • Three liang of Gan Jiang (dry ginger)
    • Six zhu of alum

    Grind these into a powder, mix with honey to form pills the size of a jujube, take twenty pills on an empty stomach with wine, or drink them down, twice daily, and if the dosage is not sufficient, increase it.[182p]
    Mumeng Yuan, also known as Zilan Pill, treats twelve conditions in women following childbirth, such as postnatal discharge and infertility, which are all due to wind and cold. This is particularly effective if it has been less than a hundred days since childbirth, and there is still retained blood in the womb. Symptoms include pain in the lower abdomen after prolonged sitting, caused by damp cold invading the body, leading to lumps that can feel as small as a chicken egg or as large as a fist. These lumps can throb or feel like bugs biting or needles pricking. There is anxious breathing, fullness in the sides, inability to eat, undigested food, blocked flows of qi, pain radiating to the lower back, nausea with shortness of breath, sweating, cold pain in the lower abdomen, and cavity pain; there may be spontaneous urination, misalignment of the birth canal, leading to infertility, back and hip pain, heaviness in the limbs, full-body swelling, intermittent symptoms, constipation, and dripping urination. Menstrual flow may be obstructed or resemble rotten flesh with various colors similar to bean juice. These are ominous signs.

    The ingredients are: Mumeng, Houpu, Noxious stones, Dried ginger, (various worms), Peony root, Sichuan pepper, Huangqin, Qianhu, Platycodon, White poria, Xixin, Chuanxiong, Ginseng, Wuzhuyu, Tingli, and Cinnamon heart, each weighing eighteen zhu; Da-Huang two and a half liang; Fuzi one liang and six zhu; and Danggui ten liang.

    Grind these into a fine powder, mix with honey to form pills the size of walnuts, and take three pills with empty stomach and wine three times daily. If symptoms persist, add more until five or six pills are consumed, and expel the root of the illness if there are excretions resembling fish roe in green, white, yellow, or red.[183p]
    Gui Zhi Fu Zi Decoction treats postnatal wind deficiency, persisting sweating, difficulty in urination, slight urgency in the limbs, and difficulty in bending and stretching.

    Ingredients:

    • 3 taels of cinnamon twig (Gui Zhi)
    • 3 taels of peony root (Shao Yao)
    • 3 taels of fresh ginger
    • 1.5 taels of licorice (Gan Cao)
    • 1 piece of aconite (Fu Zi)
    • 30 jujubes (Da Zao)

    Prepare by boiling with 7 sheng of water until 3 sheng remains, then divide into three doses.[184p]
    Ejiao Pills: Treats postpartum weakness and coldness, abdominal cramps, and persistent diarrhea.
    Ingredients: 4 taels of Ejiao, 2 taels each of ginseng, licorice, dragon bone, cinnamon heart, white atractylodes, dried rehmannia, coptis, angelica, and prepared aconite.
    Prepare into powder, form into pills the size of a walnut, and take 20 pills with warm wine, three times a day.

    Cinnamon Heart and Oyster Soup: For women after childbirth, treating headaches and body heat, as well as abdominal cramps and pain.
    Ingredients: 3 taels of cinnamon heart, 5 taels each of calcined oyster, white peony, and dried rehmannia, and 2 taels of skullcap.
    Grind to a bean-sized powder, take 5 qian with one and a half bowls of water, boil down to one bowl, strain, and take warm.

    Raw Dried Rehmannia Decoction: Treats damage from pregnancy affecting qi and blood, or disorders causing miscarriage, leading to abdominal pain post-miscarriage.
    Ingredients: 1 tael of roasted raw dried rehmannia, half tael each of rhubarb, white peony, poria, angelica (fried), siler, licorice (roasted), skullcap, and cinnamon heart.
    Masticate, take 5 qian with one and a half bowls of water, add smashed ginger and jujube, and boil down to one bowl, strain, and take warm at any time.[185p]
    Ginseng Soup: Treats excessive bleeding after a miscarriage, heart palpitations, tremors, dizziness, with feelings of cold or heat, and abdominal distention and pain. Ingredients: Ginseng, baked Ophiopogon, baked raw Rehmannia, Peony, Astragalus, washed and baked Angelica, white Poria, and roasted Licorice, each one tael. Directions: Grind the ingredients, take three qian, add one cup of water, and simmer until reduced to seven parts. Strain and take warm before meals.

    Ginseng and Astragalus Soup: Treats qi deficiency with continuous bleeding after a miscarriage. Ingredients: Ginseng, stir-fried Astragalus, Angelica, stir-fried Atractylodes, stir-fried White Peony, Artemisia leaves, each one qian; stir-fried Ejiao, two qian. Directions: Prepare one dose, decoct in water for consumption.

    Angelica Wine: Treats inability to expel blood after miscarriage. Ingredients: Fragrant roasted Angelica and stir-fried Peony, both two taels. Directions: Grind the ingredients, take three qian with a spoon, add one cup of clear wine, mix with one portion of fresh Rehmannia juice, and simmer gently in a silver vessel until reduced to seven parts. Strain and take warm to resolve the retained blood.

    Wujin Powder: Treats inability to expel retained blood after miscarriage and addresses various blood-related ailments. Ingredients: Good inkstick, two-inch pieces, burned to red, dipped in good vinegar seven times, then burned again to red, cooled and ground, two taels; Myrrh, and Kirin's Blood, each two and a half qian; Musk, one qian. Directions: Grind to a fine powder, each dose mixed with warm wine, take one qian. If there is blood confusion in the heart, add children’s urine mixed with wine for two qian.[186p]
    Red and Blue Flower Decoction: Treats menstrual bleeding after an abortion. Heart feels heavy and confused, unable to recognize others. Slightly simmer red and blue, for men with fevers, grind the red ink, and mix powdered yellow lake with papers in equal parts. The fine powder on the right, mix with half a small cup of boy's urine and take two coins of it, effective immediately.

    White Honey Decoction: Treats aftermath of abortion with dark blood not expelled. Two taels of white honey, one cup of freshly squeezed raw Rehmannia juice, half a cup of wine. Combine the juice and wine, simmer in a copper vessel until boiling for five to seven times, add honey and stir evenly. Split into two doses, take three doses, and many ailments can be cured.

    Pig Lard Decoction: Treats bleeding after abortion with intense heart pain and discomfort. Seven parts of pig lard, three parts of white honey, and two ounces of fresh Rehmannia, chopped. First, combine lard and Rehmannia, simmer until red, remove Rehmannia, add honey and stir, split into two warm doses to be taken in succession.

    Angelica Decoction: Treats premature abortion and retained placenta. Sliced and stir-fried Angelica, half a tael of ox knee soaked in wine and roasted, two taels of wood fire, and two taels of talcum ground. Roasted winter This is mixed, two parts of mallard flower heads. Grind to a powder, take three coins, with one and a half cups of water, simmer until reduced to seven parts, remove the residue and take warm. The residue can be taken again, and the following is for dosage.[187p]
    Di Huang Decoction: Treats the same conditions as above.

    Stir-Fried Puhuang: 2.5 qian each of ginger, sliced and stir-fried; 5 taels of Di Huang, sliced and stir-fried. Combine these with three sheng of wine, boil in silverware until reduced to two sheng, strain, and divide into three doses. If not taken, prepare more.

    Puhuang Wine: Treats the same conditions as above.

    Stir-Fried Puhuang, one he, 14 pieces of Huai Zi, ground into powder. Combine with three sheng of wine, boil until reduced to two sheng, strain, and divide into two warm doses. If not taken, prepare more.

    Tangkui and Chuanxiong Decoction: Treats postpartum blood stasis, abdominal pain, or fever and chills. Equal parts of: Tangkui, Chuanxiong, prepared Di Huang, stir-fried Bai Shao, Honghua, Yanhusuo, Xiangfu, stir-fried Qingpi, Zelan, Mudanpi, and Taoren. Boil with water and mix in half a small cup each of children's urine and wine to take.

    If pressing on the abdomen relieves pain, this is a blood stasis issue; the remedy is this medicine or Shixiao San. If pressing does not cause pain, this indicates blood deficiency; Four Substances and Ren Shen, Ling, and Bai Zhu are advised. If pain is accompanied by vomiting, this indicates stomach deficiency; Six Gentleman Decoction is recommended. If pain leads to diarrhea, this is spleen deficiency; Six Gentleman with Er Shen Pill is indicated.[188p]
    Xiongxuan Decoction: Treats abdominal pain due to miscarriage.
    Ingredients: Xiongxuan, Peony, White Atractylodes, Charred Gelatin, Honey-fried Licorice, each 1 tael. Grind together, take 3 qian, add to one cup of water, along with Mugwort leaves, Glutinous Rice, and Fresh Ginger, simmer until reduced to six parts, and take before meals. There is a variation without White Atractylodes, using Ginseng instead.

    Shengyu Decoction: Treats blood deficiency with restlessness, unsettled sleep, or heat in the palms.
    Ingredients: Steamed Mature Rehmannia mixed with wine for half a day; Raw Rehmannia mixed with wine; each 7.5 qian of Chuanxiong and Ginseng; 5 qian each of Angelica and Charred Astragalus. Combine, decoct with water and take.

    Danggui Blood Tonifying Decoction: Treats symptoms of heat in the muscles, redness of the eyes and face, thirst leading to drinking, continuous day and night; pulse is large and weak, with no response upon pressure—this indicates a combination of pulse and blood deficiency. If one mistakenly takes Baihu Decoction, it will be fatal.
    Ingredients: 3 qian of Angelica, 1 tael of Astragalus.[189p]
    Right, decoct in water.

    Four Substance Two Lian Decoction treats blood deficiency with heat, conditions like sores in the mouth and tongue, or a state of comfort during the day but heat at night. Ingredients include: One qian each of Angelica (danggui), Chuanxiong, cooked Rehmannia, and Peony, along with one qian each of Hu Huang Lian and Xuan Huang Lian. Decoct as a single dose, and take with water.

    Complete Blood Activating Decoction treats heavy blood loss after childbirth, and also nourishes and replenishes blood, promotes blood formation and benefits Yang. It is also effective for spontaneous sweating, night sweating, weakness in the limbs, dry mouth, and fever with headaches. Ingredients include: Three fen of safflower, five fen each of Vitex and Asarum, one qian each of cooked and raw Rehmannia, one and a half qian each of Chuanxiong and dried main root, along with two qian each of Fangfeng, Qianghuo, and Duhuo, and brown sugar, along with two qian of Angelica (washed in wine), Chaihu and Kudzu each two qian, and three qian each of Bai Shao and Sheng Ma. The above (mouthful) should be chewed. Each dose is five qian, with two bowls of water, decoct until reduced to one bowl, strain, and take warm before meals.

    Clear Soul Powder treats postnatal blood circulation issues. Ingredients include: Two and a half qian each of Eupatorium and Ginseng, five qian of Chuanxiong, and one liang of Schizonepeta. Grind the above into a powder, mix with half a bowl of warm wine and half a bowl of hot soup, and adjust one qian for rapid oral administration. Once swallowed, it helps to open the eyes; calm breathing aids in recovery. One variation of this formula includes two qian of licorice.[190p]
    Lai Su San: For excessive effort during labor, causing stagnation of qi and blood, leading to loss of consciousness. Ingredients include: Mu Xiang, not exposed to fire; Shen Qu; Chuan Ju Hong; Huang Qi; Bai Shao Yao; E Jiao; Ge Fen fried; Mai Yao fried; fresh ginger, fried black, each one qian; a handful of glutinous rice; half a qian and five fen of washed and soaked ramie root; and five fen of honey-fried licorice. Prepare as one dose with two zhong of water, decoct until one zhong remains. Stir and administer orally, to be taken continuously until recovery.

    Du Xing San: For postpartum blood circulation issues, leading to confusion and loss of consciousness, with feelings of pressure in the heart. Ingredients: Wu Ling Zhi, two Liang, half raw and half fried. Grind this into a fine powder; each dose is two qian, mixed with warm wine for consumption. For individuals with clenched jaws, pry open the mouth and administer through the throat, and recovery will be immediate. An alternate formula adds Jing Jie powdered and is mixed with child urine for consumption. If there is persistent excessive bleeding, add Dang Gui, decocted with child urine and wine, taken without time restrictions.

    Duo Ming San, also known as Xue Jie San: For postpartum blood circulation issues where blood enters the heart, resulting in disorder of speech, forgetfulness, loss of ambition, and various postpartum illnesses. Ingredients: Mo Yao and Xue Jie in equal parts. Grind these into a fine powder. Immediately after childbirth, mix half a cup each of child urine and fine alcohol, bring to a brief boil, and administer two qian of the powder. Wait a while before taking more. This will ensure that any stagnant blood continues to descend rather than rising, preventing various illnesses.[191p]
    Honghua San: Used for postpartum blood disorders such as blood fainting, blood circulation issues, and excessive menstrual bleeding, as well as long-term blood deficiencies. Ingredients: Dried lotus leaves, red peony root, angelica, safflower, and fried cattail pollen, all in equal parts. Grind to a fine powder. Each dose is half a tael, simmered with wine and taken warm. If the afterbirth does not descend, use a drink made with elm bark.

    Huazhuishi San: Used for afterbirth failure leading to life-threatening conditions, marked by heat in the heart and severe postpartum distress. Ingredients: Flower cluster stone one jin, colored sulfur four taels, both ground finely. Mix these two thoroughly, place them in a clay pot sealed with paper and clay, dry it, and heat it in a setting made of square bricks with bagua and five elements characters inscribed on them. Surround the bricks with charcoal and ignite from within; allow the heat to rise gradually. Once the fire dies down and cools, remove the mixture, grind it finely, sift it, and store in ceramic ware for use as prescribed.

    Heye San: Treats postpartum lochia not descending, abdominal pain, and mental distress. Ingredients: Dried lotus leaves two taels, ghost arrow feather, peach kernel ground, Liu Jiniu, and cattail pollen each one tael. Grind to a coarse powder. Each dose is three qian, mixed with one large cup of child's urine, two slices of ginger, and one fen of raw rehmannia, crushed and simmered until reduced to six portions, then strain and take warm without a specific time.[192p]
    Mohi Pills - Treats postpartum lochia that suddenly ceases, causing chills and fever, with sharp pains in the navel and abdomen like being pierced by a needle. This is due to an imbalance of cold and heat, or the obstruction of qi from excessive thinking or movement, leading to a buildup of blood in the meridians. Use equal parts of cinnamon heart and peony root, half a tael of peach kernels (peeled, tipped, stir-fried, and ground to a paste), each two and a half qian of myrrh ground, one tael of angelica, and thirty pieces each of stir-fried and legless raw silkworm and charred leeches. Mix these into a powder, make into pills the size of peas, and take three qian with vinegar soup.

    Dushi Decoction - Treats excessive blood loss postpartum, with sharp pain in the heart and abdomen followed by bleeding that does not cease for a long time. It also treats long-standing red and white vaginal discharge that other medications have failed to address. Use one whole dose of Guanzhong (not ground), simply knead to remove hair and flowers, and soak with good vinegar, gently roasting to bring out the fragrance until it cools. Grind to a fine powder and mix with two qian of rice drink, to be taken on an empty stomach before meals.

    Oyster Powder - Treats persistent postpartum lochia, heart discomfort, shortness of breath, weakness in the limbs, heaviness in the head, restlessness with heat, loss of appetite, pale complexion, and thin body. Ingredients include calcined oyster, Chuanxiong, cooked rehmannia, white poria, and dragon bone, each one qian, and half a tael of each of the following: rehmannia root, angelica, mugwort leaves (stir-fried in wine), ginseng, schizandra, and earth riser. Add three and a half qian of licorice. Grind these into powder, take two qian with one middle bowl of water, three slices of ginger, and one date, boil until reduced to six parts, strain, and take before meals.[193p]
    Seven Qi Powder for Dispelling Pain in Postpartum Heart.
    Ingredients:

    • Yan Hu (Yan Hu Su)
    • Xiao Ying Xiang
    • Shao Yao (Paeonia lactiflora)
    • Stir-fried Gan Qi
    • Zhi Ke (Bitter Orange) - each 2 qian
    • Huang Lian (Coptis chinensis)
    • Shi Chao Pu (Acorus tatarinowii)
    • Xiang Fu
    • Su Ye (Perilla leaves) - each 1.5 qian
    • Mo Yao (Myrrh)
    • Ru Xiang (Frankincense) - each 1 liang
    • Gan Cao (Licorice root) - 6 fen
      Combine these into a powder, divide into two doses. Each dose should be taken with 1.5 bowls of water and three slices of ginger, decoct to 70% and take on an empty stomach.

    Dai Yan Mi Decoction for the same condition.
    Ingredients:

    • Sheng Gan Di Huang (Rehmannia)
    • Dang Gui (Angelica sinensis)
    • Du Huo (Angelica pubescens)
    • Wu Zhu Yu (Evodia)
    • Shao Yao (Paeonia lactiflora)
    • Gan Jiang (Dried ginger)
    • Gan Cao (Licorice root)
    • Gui Xin (Cinnamon heart)
    • Xiao Cao (Little grass) - each 1 liang
    • Xi Xin (Asarum) - 0.5 liang
      Combine these into a powder. Each dose is 0.5 liang, with 3 bowls of water, decoct to 1 bowl, strain, and take warm.

    Commentary: Postpartum heart pain is not necessarily due to blood pain. If it’s the Yan Mi Decoction, how can one use Shu Di Huang (prepared Rehmannia)? Shu Di Huang nourishes blood; how can it ease pain? This formula originated from "Qian Jin," using Sheng Gan Di Huang and Wu Zhu Yu in a ratio of five liang combined with three liang of Gan Jiang. The Xi Xin is for treating retained cold in the lower jiao. The basic formula is 1 liang, but reduce by 0.5 liang. Treatment should be adjusted according to the depth of the illness; modifications should not be arbitrary. However, for blood conditions, Yan Mi Decoction is not as effective as Shi Xiao San.[194p]
    Lost Laughter Powder: For postpartum children with remaining blood rushing to the heart, causing anxiety, abdominal pain, and a desire to die, this remedy will provide immediate relief when all other medicines fail.

    Ingredients: Five Spirit Fat and Cattail Pollen, equal parts.

    Grind the above into a powder. First, mix two qian with strong vinegar to cook into a paste. Add one cup of water and simmer until reduced to seven tenths. Take while hot before meals; it has proven effective.

    Jing Shui Lu Sichuan Pepper Decoction: For treating heart pain due to great cold after childbirth.

    Ingredients: 2 sheng Sichuan pepper, 3 liang peony root, half a sheng pinellia, 2 liang angelica, 2 liang cinnamon, 2 liang ginseng, 2 liang licorice, 2 liang poria, and 5 sheng ginger juice, with 1 sheng honey.

    Chop the above ingredients and boil with 9 sheng of water until it reaches a rolling boil. Add the other herbs and continue cooking until reduced to 3.5 sheng. Strain out the solids, then add ginger juice and honey, and simmer again to obtain 3 sheng. Take in doses of 5 to 6 he.[195p]
    Heishensan: For treating postpartum blood clots and pain in the lower abdomen due to stagnant meridians.

    Ingredients: 1 pound of cooked Rehmannia, 0.5 pound of aged ginger. Combine these two ingredients, fry until dry, and grind to a powder. Take 2 qian mixed with black plums and take regularly with wine. If the meridians are blocked, take black plums and schizonepeta with wine.

    Heibaisan: For treating severe headaches in infants after childbirth.

    Ingredients: Seven times calcined black zinc and ground; calcined cold water stone and ground to a powder. Collect both, and when pain occurs, take 1.5 qian mixed with rice drink. If the pain stops, do not take more; if it does not stop, take again.

    Dingzh wan: For treating postpartum confusion.

    Ingredients: 1.5 ounces each of ginseng and Poria; 1 ounce each of calamus and distant zizi. Combine these, make into pills the size of a walnut, and take 60 to 70 pills with warm water.

    Ningzhigao (also known as Ning Shen Gao): For treating excessive postpartum blood loss, anxiety of the heart, confusion, difficulty sleeping.

    Ingredients: 0.1 ounce each of cinnabar ground, fried jujube seeds, ginseng, Poria (wood removed), amber ground, and myrrh ground. Combine and grind to a powder. Take 0.1 ounce with a strong decoction of jujube and lampwick tea on an empty stomach.

    For another method, if there is no Poria or amber, make pills the size of a bullet and dissolve one in mint tea.

    For a further method, if there is red Poria but no Poria, use as directed.[196p]
    Rhinoceros Horn and Rehmannia Decoction treats postpartum nosebleeds.

    • 7.5 qian of peony,
    • 0.5 jin of fresh rehmannia,
    • 1 liang of rhinoceros horn shavings,
    • 1 liang of clean peony bark.

    Prepare by grinding these ingredients; each dose is 5 qian, decoct in water. If rhinoceros horn shavings are unavailable, replace with licorice. For those with symptoms resembling mania due to heat, add 2 liang of scutellaria.

    Schisandra Berry Pill treats postpartum fecal retention.

    • 3 liang each of
      • ginseng,
      • fried white atractylodes,
      • fried schisandra,
      • fried rhubarb,
    • 2.5 liang each of
      • fried yam,
      • fried white poria,
    • 1 liang each of
      • fried evodia with soup,
      • fried morinda root,
      • well-cooked meat pulp,
    • a small amount of dragon bone.

    Grind these into a powder, form into pills the size of tung seeds. Take over 100 pills each time, with clear broth or rice soup before meals.

    Solidifying Bladder Drink treats postpartum bladder damage, with continuous dripping of urine.

    • 3 feet of natural yellow silk (do not use dyed),
    • Cook with charcoal ash drain juice until extremely soft, then wash with clean water to remove ash.
    • 0.5 liang of yellow wax,
    • 1 liang of honey,
    • 2 qian each of
      • reed root,
      • fried madder powder.

    Add 2 sheng of water, decoct to 1 cup, and take on an empty stomach. Do not make noise while taking it, as making noise will prevent efficacy.[197p]
    Supplementary Medicine Drink: Treats the same conditions as above.
    Take one foot of yellow raw silk, one qian each of peony root and alisma rhizome. Boil in two cups of water until the silk is soft like syrup. Consume without making noise.

    Rehmannia Powder: Treats lingering bad blood postpartum and abdominal pain.
    Take two qian each of raw rehmannia and angelica, half a liang of ginger (cut and roasted until charred). Grind everything into a fine powder. Take two qian each time, mixed with ginger wine on an empty stomach.

    Angelica and Dragon's Blood Pill: Treats women postpartum when lochia does not discharge, leading to clots, chest oppression, and lower abdominal pain.
    Use two liang each of angelica and dragon's blood (ground separately), two liang of peony root, and two liang of coix seed (fried), four liang of myrrh. Grind them into a fine powder, mix with vinegar to form pills the size of a phoenix nut. Take fifty pills each time, with warm wine before meals.

    Lotus Stem Powder: Treats postpartum blood rushing to the heart, blood stasis causing abdominal pain, and slow discharge of lochia.
    Take freshly harvested lotus stem, one liang each of safflower and angelica, and half a liang each of peony root and catkin (roasted between paper). Grind into a fine powder. Take two qian each time, mixed with salt wine, and a spoonful of boy’s urine.

    Bitter Orange and Peony Powder: Treats postpartum abdominal pain and fullness, preventing sleep.
    Roast bitter orange until black (but not burnt), and use equal parts of peony.[198p]
    The two ingredients on the right are ground into a powder. Take a small spoonful daily, three times a day, and this is aimed at treating abscesses and pus, consumed with millet porridge.

    Tang of Angelica and Astragalus: Treats postpartum lower back pain, difficulty turning sides, spontaneous sweating with strong heat, robust body, and shortness of breath.

    • Astragalus and Peony each two taels, Angelica three taels.

    The ingredients on the right are prepared into powder; take four qian each dose, with four slices of ginger, boiled in water and taken warm.

    Chuanxiong Powder: Treats postpartum headaches.

    • True Tian Tai Black Drug Skin and Chuanxiong in equal parts.

    The above is ground into a fine powder; take three qian each dose, with a red-hot weighing hammer quenched in wine for adjustment.

    Xiongfu Powder: Treats postpartum stagnant blood causing blockages and ineffective headaches from other medications.

    • One large Fuzi, one bowl of strong vinegar, roasted evenly over fire, dipped in vinegar to remove the skin and umbilicus. Chuanxiong one tael.

    The above is ground into powder; take one qian each dose, mixed with clear tea.
