    Duhuo Jisheng Decoction: Treats liver and kidney deficiency, prolonged exposure to damp and cold areas, or washing feet in the wind, with damp toxins attacking internally, causing weakness and pain in both shins, or purplish skin and flesh, heavy and cramped legs and knees, and also specifically treats postpartum beriberi.

    • Duhuo three taels, Sangjisheng, as if there is no way to continue, Duzhong (stir-fried), Bai Fuling, Niuxi soaked in wine, Guogui not exposed to fire, Xixin, Fangfeng, Chuanxiong, Angelica, Ren Shen, Shudizhi washed in wine, Peony, Qinjiao each two taels, Gan Cao (stir-fried) one tael.

    The above are chewed; take four qian each dose, with five slices of ginger, boiled in water and taken warm.[199p]
    Jia Jian Si Wu Decoction (from "Baoming Ji")
    Used to treat postpartum headaches, blood deficiency, phlegm disorders, cold shock, all of which can cause headaches.

    • 1.6 liang of Atractylodes
    • 1 liang each of Qiang Huo, Chuan Xiong, Fang Feng, and Xiang Fu (roasted)
    • 1 liang of Bai Zhi
    • 1 liang of Dang Gui
    • 1 liang of Gan Cao
    • 2.5 liang of Shi Gao
    • 1.5 liang of Xi Xin

    Grind all the above into a coarse powder. Use 1 liang each time, decoct with water and take hot, regardless of the time. If there is sweating, it indicates blood deficiency causing headaches; add 3 liang of Bai Shao, 1.5 liang of Gui Zhi, and decoct with ginger. If headaches are caused by phlegm disorders, add 3 liang of Ban Xia, 1 liang of Fu Ling, and decoct with ginger. If headaches are due to heat shock, add 3 liang of Bai Zhi, 2 liang of Shi Gao, and 1 liang of Zhi Mu. If headaches are due to cold shock, add 3 liang of Tian Ma, 1.5 liang of Fu Zi, and 3 slices of ginger, then decoct and take.

    Cheng Tong Fang (from "Zhun Sheng")
    Used to treat postpartum weakness, blood stagnation, whole body pain, and body heat with headaches.

    • 0.5 liang each of Niu Xi, Dang Gui, Gui Xin, Bai You, Huang Qi, Du Hua, and fresh ginger
    • 0.25 liang each of Gan Cao and Chai Hu

    Grind the above into a fine powder. Each time, take 0.5 liang, with 3 shan (cups) of water, decoct until reduced to 1.5 shan, and take before eating.[200p]
    Fángfēng Táng - Used for postpartum wind stroke, characterized by stiffness and urgency in the neck and back, and fullness in the chest with shortness of breath.
    Ingredients: Fángfēng, Dúhuó, Gégēn, each 250 grams; Dāngguī, Rénshēn, Bái Sháoyao, Gāncǎo (roasted), each 100 grams.
    Preparation: Grind as needed, with each dosage being 24 grams, using one and a half bowls of water, along with two jujubes split open. Simmer until reduced to one bowl, strain and take warm, without regard to timing. One variation includes dried ginger.

    Chuānxióng Sǎn - Used for postpartum wind stroke, with body and back stiff and tight, as if bound by a rope.
    Ingredients: Chuānxióng, Qiānghuó, Zǎorén, Antelope horn shavings, Bái Sháoyao, each 200 grams; Sāngbái Pí, 75 grams; Fángfēng, 50 grams.
    Preparation: Grind as needed, with each dosage being 30 grams, using two large bowls of water, simmered down to one and a half bowls. Strain and take without regard to timing, three doses daily.

    Jīngxiào Fúlíng Táng - Used for postpartum wind deficiency headache and slurred speech.
    Ingredients: Fúlíng, Fángfēng, and Dried Gēgēn, each 24 grams; Màidōng, 50 grams; Bái Sháoyao, Huángqīn, each 18 grams; Rhinoceros horn shavings, 16 grams; Gāncǎo (roasted), 100 grams.
    Preparation: Grind as needed, with each dosage being 30 grams, using one and a half large bowls of water, simmered down to one bowl. Strain and take warm, without regard to timing.[201p]
    Hua Tuo’s Wind Dispersing Remedy

    This treats women suffering from postpartum apoplexy, where they are mute, with their hands and feet convulsing like a bow, or from blood stasis after childbirth, being in a state of unconsciousness, their limbs stiff, or experiencing abdominal distention, vomiting, and diarrhea as if about to die.

    The tips of Schizonepeta are lightly roasted. Grind these into a powder, and take three quarts with bean dregs and wine, or with the urine of a child. If mute, use it to administer through the mouth by pricking the gums, and if the gums are also numb, administer it through the nose; its effects are miraculous. Generally, during the postpartum period, there can be significant dizziness, which leads to sweating and the loosening of the pores, making it easier for apoplexy to occur.

    Zhang Zhongjing states: This formula has been highly praised in many texts; Yao Sentan in "Collected Verified Formulas" recommends taking it with wine, calling it the Divine Powder, stating it will take effect immediately. The Chen family’s formulation is called "Ancient Worship Powders." Xiao Cunjing's formula uses ancient coins boiled in soup, referred to as "One Pinch of Gold." Wang Kuang's "Directions for Confusion" adds equal parts Angelica and is brewed for drinking. Xu Shuwei's "Famous Recipes" mentions this medicine as having remarkable divine effectiveness.

    One postpartum woman who slept for a long time woke up feeling dazed as if drunk and unaware of her surroundings. The doctor used this medicine and combined it with the dispersing powder, advising her to sleep afterwards and inevitably scratch her head with her left hand. It truly worked. Jiu Yin in "Treasures of Childbirth" states: This illness often arises from anger harming the liver, from pent-up anxiety, or from sitting in the grass and catching wind, so it is urgently necessary to take this medicine. Dai's "Essentials of Diagnosis and Treatment" calls it the "Single Walking Powder," and Jia Sidao favored it, copying it and calling it "Rebirth Elixir."[202p]
    Jihui Shangdan
    For treating postpartum collapse and extreme weakness leading to wind.
    Ingredients:

    • Frankincense
    • Five Spirit Gum
    • Sulfur
    • Yuanjing Stone (refined very finely)
    • Fried Ejiao (Donkey-hide Gelatin)
    • Roller Cypress
    • Mulberry Mistletoe
    • Aged Tangerine Peel (white parts removed)
      Each in equal parts. Combine the first four ingredients and grind together, lightly roast in a gold or stone utensil until slightly fragrant, avoiding scorching. Grind very finely again, then mix in the remaining ingredients into a powder, and blend thoroughly. Use the juice of fresh Rehmannia to form pills the size of walnuts. Take twenty pills each time, washed down with warm wine or Angelica wine, before meals.

    Ge Gen Decoction
    For treating postpartum stroke, mouth locked, collapsing to the ground, dizziness, excessive phlegm, and urgent breathing, as well as other disorders post-childbirth.
    Ingredients:

    • Kudzu Root (Ge Gen) 6 taels
    • Fresh Ginger 6 taels
    • Notopterygium 4 taels
    • Angelica (Dong Quai) 3 taels
    • Honey-fried Licorice 2 taels
    • Cinnamon Twig 2 taels
    • White Poria 2 taels
    • Gypsum 2 taels
    • Ginseng 2 taels
    • Atractylodes 2 taels
    • Siler 2 taels
    • Chuanxiong (Ligusticum) 2 taels
      Combine and grind into a powder. Take 5 qian (approximately 15 grams) with 2 cups of water, boil down to 1.5 cups, strain, and take warm. There is no specific time; take two doses per day.[203p]
      Geng Ge Decoction: Treats postpartum stroke, with closed mouth and inability to speak.
    • 2 liang of Du Huo
    • 1.5 liang of Geng Ge
    • 1.5 liang of fresh ginger
    • 0.5 liang of honey-fried licorice
      Combine the above ingredients, grind them, and take 1 liang each time, with 2 large cups of water. Boil down to 1 large cup, strain, and take warm at any time.

    Tian Ma Powder: Treats postpartum stroke with closed mouth.

    • 7.5 qian of Tian Ma
    • 0.5 liang each of:
      • white Fu Zi (processed)
      • Tian Nan Xing (processed)
      • stir-fried scorpion
      • Ban Xia (washed with ginger juice seven times)
        Grind the above into a fine powder, take 1 qian each time, mix with fresh ginger and mint wine, and pour to open the mouth, at any time.

    Dang Gui Powder, also known as Jiao Jia Powder. Treats postpartum stroke in women, with tight jaws, loss of consciousness, drooling, and seizures of hands and feet.

    • Dang Gui (remove the pith)
    • Jing Jie seed (equal parts)
      Grind the above into a fine powder, take 2 qian each time, with 1 cup of water and 0.5 cup of wine, boil down to 1 cup, strain and pour, gently opening the tight jaws. Administer slowly, as just swallowing will often revive the patient, proving very effective upon multiple uses.[204p]
      Fulonggan Powder treats postpartum stroke, where the mouth is locked and unable to speak, along with pain in the waist and back. Use one and a half taels of Fulonggan and half a tael of dried ginger, both powdered finely. Take two qian each time, mixed with warm wine, without regard to time, two doses per day.

    Jiaojia Powder treats various ailments before and after childbirth, where women's vital energy and blood are insufficient, the meridians are unbalanced, abdominal pain occurs, and excess gas with insufficient blood leads to lumps, as well as postpartum spasms and pain in the hypochondrium. Use one sheng of fresh Rehmannia juice and twelve taels of fresh ginger juice. First, stir-fry the Rehmannia juice with ginger residue, then stir-fry ginger juice with Rehmannia residue, slightly dry and powder finely. Take three qian each time, mixed with warm wine. It is also suitable for common abdominal pain and especially essential postpartum.

    Antelope Horn Powder treats postpartum stroke with body retraction. Use seven and a half qian of antelope horn shavings, seven and a half qian of Angelica, one tael each of Ligusticum, Saposhnikovia, and Ephedra (with stems removed), and half a tael each of ginseng, red peony, Asarum, and cassia bark. Prepare by grinding and combining these ingredients. Take eight qian each time, boiled with one and a half large cups of water and five slices of ginger until reduced to one large cup. Strain and take warm, without regard to time.

    Duhuo Decoction treats postpartum stroke and is suitable for patients who cannot take other medicines. Use three taels of Sichuan Duhuo, finely chopped.[205p]
    Take three sheng of the right water, boil down to one sheng, divide for consumption. For those drinking wine, the wine can also be boiled with the water.

    For a medicinal solution of white moss, it treats the same ailments.
    Three liang of white yam skin, finely cut.
    Boil as described above, following the method for Du Huo.

    For the Qianquan chicken droppings liquor, it treats various postpartum ailments, including postpartum stroke, lockjaw, contractions, and stiffness in the waist and back with frequent bending. Take three sheng of black chicken droppings, and two sheng of soybeans.
    First, stir-fry the soybeans until the sound ceases, then stir-fry the chicken droppings until yellow. Use one sheng of wine to first moisten the chicken droppings, then extract the juice to moisten the soybeans, taking one sheng at a time, up to four or five doses for severe cases, highly effective.

    For soybean purple liquor, it treats dizziness from stroke, fear of wind, spontaneous sweating, vomiting of cold water, and various postpartum maladies, or stroke-related cramps, back stiffness, lockjaw, persistent irritability, tight pulse which is untreated. The "Xiao Pin Fang" states: it is for postpartum strokes with heaviness in the back and lockjaw, or general restlessness, or heaviness and itchiness in the body, vomiting, and persistent staring.
    Use one and a half liang of Du Huo without its roots, half sheng of soybeans, and three sheng of wine. First, soak the Du Huo in the wine, boil once or twice until bubbling, then separately stir-fry the soybeans until they smoke heavily, and quickly add them into the wine. Seal tightly and let cool, then remove the beans. Take one to two he for each dose; if slight sweating occurs, it can be cured. Ten doses daily. This remedy can dispel wind and dissipate blood stasis, useful for injuries during pregnancy that result in fetal death in the womb; taking this will lead to recovery.[206p]
    Soybean Soup
    Treats postpartum stroke, where one falls to the ground and loses consciousness; also treats conditions involving wind during pregnancy, as well as various ailments between reed grass.
    Ingredients: 5 sheng of yellow roasted soybeans, 8 taels each of angelica and kudzu root, 6 taels of dichroa.
    Preparation: Grind the above, take 5 qian with 2 bowls of wine, cook until reduced to 1.5 bowls, strain, and take warm. No specific timing; take three times daily.

    Antelope Horn Decoction
    Treats postpartum fullness of qi, hard abdomen, distended sides, heart burn, thirst for water, and conditions resembling severe convulsion or stroke.
    Ingredients: 0.5 tael of powdered antelope horn, 8 qian each of windbreaker, notopterygium, and platycodon, 1 tael 2 qian each of prepared and poached cassia, bupleurum, and rhubarb.
    Preparation: Grind the above, take 5 qian with 1.5 large bowls of water, cook until reduced to 1 bowl, strain, and take warm. No specific timing. Additionally, take Rehmannia wine: cut 1 sheng of rehmannia, roast to darken, place in a ceramic bottle with 3 sheng of hot wine, seal tightly and reduce by half through boiling, consume as desired.

    Windbreaker and Angelica Powder
    Treats postpartum convulsions.
    Ingredients: 1 tael each of windbreaker, angelica, chuanxiong, and rehmannia.
    Preparation: Grind the above, take 1 tael with 3 bowls of water, cook until reduced to 2 bowls, and take warm.[207p]
    Fangfeng Decoction: It treats wind deficiency heat, stiff neck and back, limbs and joints not moving correctly, confusion and wild speech, coming and going without a set time, not being self-aware. It also treats weak and sluggish edema in the feet, showing significant effect. This medicine is mild and does not drain the body.

    Ingredients:

    • Qin jiao (Gentiana macrophylla) - 2 taels
    • Du huo (Angelica pubescens) - 2 taels
    • Mahuang (Ephedra) cut from the joints - 2 taels
    • Banxia (Pinellia ternata) processed with water and cut into slices - 2 taels
    • Fangfeng (Saposhnikovia divaricata) - 2 taels
    • Shengma (Cimicifuga) - 1 tael
    • Fangji (Stephania tetrandra) - 1 tael
    • Baizhu (Atractylodes macrocephala) - 1 tael
    • Shigao (Gypsum) calcined - 1 tael
    • Baishao (Paeonia lactiflora) - 1 tael
    • Huangqin (Scutellaria baicalensis) - 1 tael
    • Gancao (Licorice) - 1 tael
    • Danggui (Angelica sinensis) - 1 tael
    • Yuanzhi (Polygala tenuifolia) - 1 tael
    • Renshen (Ginseng) - 1 tael

    Grind the above into coarse powder and mix with the Banxia slices. Each dose is 4 qian (a measurement unit), with 2 medium cups of water, 7-8 slices of ginger, simmered to one cup, strained to take 6 fen of the clear liquid, adding a small amount of musk powder, to be taken warm after meals before bed.

    Zengsun Chaihu Decoction: It treats postpartum anomalous symptoms, spasms in hands and feet, and thick saliva causing mental dullness.

    Ingredients:

    • Chaihu (Bupleurum) - 3 qian
    • Huangqin - 1.2 qian
    • Renshen - 1.5 qian
    • Gan cao (roasted) - 1.5 qian
    • Banxia (soaked) - 1.5 qian
    • Zhimu (Anemarrhena asphodeloides) - 1 qian
    • Shigao - 2 qian
    • Huangqi (Astragalus) - 2.5 qian

    Grind the above finely and divide into two doses, with 2 cups of water, 3 slices of ginger, and 2 dates, simmered until 8 fen remains, taken without time restrictions.

    Qin Jiao Decoction: After the previous illness has been resolved, take this medicine to dispel wind evils.

    Ingredients:

    • Qin jiao - 1.7 qian
    • Baishao - 1.7 qian
    • Chaihu - 1.7 qian
    • Huangqin - 1.2 qian
    • Fangfeng - 1.2 qian
    • Gan cao (roasted) - 1.3 qian
    • Renshen - 1 qian
    • Banxia - 1 qian

    Grind the above finely and divide into two doses, each with 2 cups of water, 3 slices of ginger, simmered until 8 fen remains, taken far from meals.[208p]
    Xiong Yao San: Treats postpartum stroke, with sudden muscle cramps and pain in the limbs, and stiff neck and back. Ingredients: Fang Feng, Niu Bang Zi, each one tael; Xiong Yao, fried Suan Zao Ren, Qiang Huo, Dang Gui, Ling Yang Jiao Jie, each seven and a half qian; Gui Xin, Chi Shao Yao, each half a tael. Prepare by grinding the ingredients. Take eight qian each time with one and a half large cups of water, simmer until reduced to one cup, strain, and take warm at any time.

    Fang Ji Gao: Treats postpartum stroke, with muscle cramps in the limbs, and bodily paralysis. Ingredients: Han Fang Ji, peeled, half a pound; Yin Yu, five ounces. Prepare by grinding, soak the herbs in five sheng of wine overnight, obtain one pound of pig fat, and cook on both strong and gentle fire, three up and three down until it becomes a paste. Spread on paper flowers to apply to the affected area, and keep massaging the paste with warm hands.

    Qi Zhen San: Treats inability to speak postpartum. Ingredients: Ren Shen, Shi Chang Pu, Sheng Di Huang, Chuan Xiong, each one tael; Fang Feng, Chen Sha, ground separately, each half tael; Xi Xin, one qian. Prepare as a very fine powder. Take one qian each time with mint tea at any time. Add one qian of Gan Cao, known as Ba Zhen San.

    Hu Shi Gu Feng San: Treats postpartum inability to speak with closed eyes. Ingredients: Sheng Bai Fan, one qian. Prepare as a powder and mix it with hot water. Another formulation treats inability to speak postpartum.[209p]
    Ginseng
    Stone lotus flesh without removing the heart
    Stone acorus, each in equal parts
    Take five qian each dose, decoct in water.

    Blood Expulsion and Heart Reinforcement Decoction
    For loss of voice and inability to speak after childbirth.
    Safflower, red peony root, fresh rehmannia root, balloon flower, perilla leaves, former root, poria, fangfeng, bull gall, south star anise, coptis, and powdered kudzu, each two qian;
    Angelica three qian; mint, ginseng, and cimicifuga each one qian and five fen; half summer two qian and five fen; licorice one qian.
    Grind these into a powder, divide into two doses, each time take one and a half bowls of water, three slices of ginger, and decoct to seven parts, take on an empty stomach. The residue can be decocted again for consumption.

    Great Sage Zelan Powder
    For women's cold deficiency in the blood sea, long absence of children; as well as postpartum blood stasis attacking the heart, causing spasm of the mouth, if the fetus dies in utero, opening the mouth to administer the medicine will ensure a smooth delivery. Also treats miscarriage, abdominal cramping and pain, transverse presentations, retained placenta, blood blockages, blood stasis, and bleeding; blood entering the limbs, various blood organ disorders; as well as various wind conditions, or cold damage with nausea and coughing, alternating cold and heat, ulcers all over the body, headache and nausea, disrupted meridians, red or white discharge, foul smelling milk, cold deficiency of the womb, repeated miscarriages, irregular bleeding, leading to illness; maidens with blocked menses should also take this. Regular consumption warms the womb, harmonizes blood and qi, improves complexion, expels cold wind, and alleviates manifold ailments.[210p]
    Ingredients for Decoction:

    • 2 taels each of Ze Lan leaves and gypsum (fine powder)
    • 1 tael each of Chi Wu (flexible cedar) roots, Bai Fu Ling (white poria), Fang Feng (siler), and thick peel of Hou Po (magnolia bark)
    • Stir-fried fresh ginger, Xi Xin (asarum), and Bai Zi Ren (arborvitae seeds) slightly roasted
    • 1 tael of Jie Geng (balloon flower) and 7 times washed and dried Wu Zhu Yu (Evodia fruit)
    • 1 tael each of Wu Wei Zi (schisandra) and Ren Shen (ginseng)
    • 7.5 qian of Jiao Ben (dried herb), Bai Zhi (angelica), and Chuan Jiao (Sichuan pepper) (seeds removed, lightly fried)
    • 1 tael of Bai Zhu (white atractylodes), Huang Qi (astragalus), Gan Jiang (dried ginger, roasted), and Chuan Wu Tou (Sichuan wolfsbane, roasted and peeled)
    • 7.5 qian of Dan Shen (salvia)
    • 7.5 qian each of Wu Yao (black peony), Zhi Gan Cao (honey-fried licorice), Chuan Xiong (Sichuan lovage), Shao Yao (peony), and Dang Gui (angelica)
    • 2.5 taels of Rou Gui (cinnamon)
    • 0.5 tael each of Bai Wei (white grass) and A Jiao (donkey-hide gelatin, fried)
    • 1.5 tael of raw, dried Di Huang (rehmannia)

    Preparation:
    Grind the above into a fine powder. Take 2 qian mixed with hot wine on an empty stomach, preferably before sleep. For urgent conditions, take three times daily without time restriction.

    Postpartum Madness Prescription:
    For treating postpartum blood failing to circulate to the heart, with symptoms of fever, frantic speech, and restlessness, pulse is weak and large.

    Ingredients:

    • Dried lotus leaves
    • Raw, dried Di Huang
    • Peony root, all in equal parts

    Preparation:
    Boil these three ingredients into a concentrated decoction. Mix in 2 qian of raw Pu Huang (cattail pollen) before serving; one dose will secure a resolution.

    Miao Xiang San:
    For treating postpartum disorientation of the spirit, with confused speech and visions of ghosts and deities.

    Ingredients:

    • Raw, dried Di Huang
    • Dang Gui, both in equal parts.[211p]
      The following two ingredients, when decocted into a soup and taken, will have an immediate effect.

    Wujin San treats madness, chaotic speech, and hallucinations of gods and spirits that may occur three to five days or half a month after childbirth. The formula includes:

    • 1 qian of Angelica
    • 1 qian of Polygala
    • 1 qian of Chuanxiong
    • 1 qian of Jujube seed
    • 1 qian of Atractylodes
    • 1 qian of Red peony
    • 1 qian of Cyperus
    • A separate grind of Cinnabar
    • 1 qian of Rehmannia
    • 1 qian of Notopterygium
    • 1 qian of Saposhnikovia
    • 1 qian of Achyranthes
    • 1 qian of Tianma
    • 1.5 qian of Chenpi
    • 1.5 qian of Angelica dahurica
    • 2 qian of Poria
    • 3 qian of Pinellia
    • 9 fen each of Scorpion, Ophiopogon, Ginseng, and Licorice.

    The above is to be ground into a powder, made into two doses, boiled with one and a half bowls of water, three slices of ginger, and three spring onions, then decocted into one bowl, taken warm at any time.

    Sifang Buxin Decoction treats post-partum confusion and disorientation. The formula includes:

    • 5 qian of Angelica
    • 4 qian each of Chuanxiong, Radix Rehmanniae, and White peony
    • 4 qian of Poria
    • 4 qian of Pinellia
    • 4 qian of Platycodon
    • 3 qian of Chenpi
    • 1 qian of Licorice.

    The above is to be ground into a powder, divided into six doses, each to be mixed with one bowl of water, three slices of ginger, boiled down to seven parts, and taken warm on an empty stomach. The residue can be decocted again. If there is heat, add two qian of fried Huanglian with wine. If there is no heat, do not add.

    Tiao Jing San, also known as Xiao Tiao Jing San, treats postpartum edema. The formula includes:

    • 1 qian each of Myrrh, Amber (ground finely), and Cinnamon bark
    • 2.5 qian each of Peony and Angelica
    • 5 fen each of Musk and Asarum.

    The above is to be made into a powder, with five fen taken each time mixed with a little ginger juice and warm wine. One version includes two qian of Licorice.[212p]
    Fu Shen San: Treats postpartum blood evil, with the heart and spirit in a daze, speech losing its measure, and restless sleep. Ingredients: 1 tael of Fu Shen (Poria), peeled; 7.5 qian each of Ren Shen (Ginseng), Long Chi (Dragon Teeth) ground, Hu Po (Amber) ground, Chi Shao Yao (Red Peony), Huang Qi (Astragalus), Niu Xi (Achyranthes); 1.5 taels of Sheng Di (Rehmannia); 0.5 tael of Gui Xin (Cinnamon Heart). Grind these to a powder; each dose is 3 qian, boiled in a cup of water until reduced to 7 parts, taken warm after straining, at any time.

    Bai Zi Ren San: Treats postpartum wild speech and chaos, caused by internal deficiency and failing blood, with evil qi attacking the heart. Ingredients: Bai Zi Ren (Biota Seed), Yuan Zhi (Polygala, heart removed), Ren Shen (Ginseng), Sang Ji Sheng (Mulberry Mistletoe), Fang Feng (Siler), Hu Po (Amber, separately ground), Dang Gui (Angelica) roasted, Sheng Di Huang (Rehmannia) roasted, Gan Cao (Licorice) of equal parts. Coarsely grind these. First take 1 slice of white sheep heart, boil it in 1.5 cups of water until reduced to 9 parts, remove the sheep heart, add 5 qian of the powdered herbs, and boil until reduced to 6 parts, strain and take at any time.

    Bai Fu Ling San: Treats postpartum palpitations, speech irregularities, and a confused spirit. Ingredients: 1.5 taels each of Bai Fu Ling (White Poria), Shu Di Huang (Cooked Rehmannia), Ren Shen (Ginseng); 1 tael each of Yuan Zhi (Polygala), Bai Shao Yao (White Peony), Huang Qi (Astragalus), Gui Xin (Cinnamon Heart), Dang Gui (Angelica) roasted, Gan Cao (Licorice) toasted; 7.5 qian each of Shi Chao Pu (Acorus) and Sang Ji Sheng (Mulberry Mistletoe). Coarsely grind these, and for each dose take 8 qian, boiled with 1.5 cups of water, 5 slices of ginger, 3 dates, and 5-7 pieces of bamboo leaves, boil until reduced to 1 cup, strain and take warm, at any time.[213p]
    Shu Gan Di Huang San treats postpartum heart weakness, anxiety, and unsettled spirit.

    Ingredients:

    • Two liang of cooked dried rehmannia
    • One liang each of astragalus, white peony root, and dragon's teeth, ground
    • Seven and a half qian each of ginseng, poria without wood, and qinghao
    • Half a liang each of cassia heart, siler, and honey-fried licorice

    Preparation:
    Grind the above, using five qian for each dose. Add one and a half large bowls of water, five slices of ginger, and three jujubes. Boil until reduced to one large bowl, strain, and take warm at any time. If there's no astragalus, substitute with jingjie.

    Chan Ru Qi Baosan is taken after the initial delivery to harmonize blood and energy, replenish weakness, stabilize the spirit, and calm anxiety.

    Ingredients:

    • Two qian each of cinnabar, cassia heart, angelica, chuanxiong, ginseng, white poria, and burnt antelope horn
    • One qian of dried ginger

    Preparation: Grind into a fine powder. For each dose, take one qian, and mix with two qian of qinghao wine for postpartum women. For those who do not drink alcohol, use clear rice soup. If feeling anxious and overheated, use a decoction of wheat kernel without heart. If there's discomfort and pain in the heart area, mix with child’s urine. If feeling heat and discomfort in the chest, reduce ginger and cassia. If feeling cold in the chest, add ginger and cassia. If there's abdominal pain, add angelica. If feeling oppressed in the heart, add antelope horn. If feeling weak and timid, add cassia heart. If lacking appetite or feeling nauseous, add ginseng. If feeling vexed, add poria. Adjust according to feeling, take three times a day and once at night.[214p]
    Hai Zang Da Xiao Mudan San is used to treat blood deficiency and wind, with symptoms such as dizziness, lack of appetite, hands and feet feeling hot and restless, pain and stiffness in limbs and joints, discomfort in the chest, irregular bowel movements, disharmony between yin and yang, anxiety, and palpitations that may come and go.

    Ingredients include:

    • Dan Pi, Chuan Xiong, and Zhi Ke (fried) each one liang
    • Chen Pi, Yan Hu, Gan Cao, Qiang Huo, and Ban Xia (washed in decoction) each half a liang
    • Shao Yao, Mu Xiang, and He Zi meat each seven qian and half
    • Dang Gui one and a half liang
    • San Leng (fried), Gan Jiang (processed), and Gui Xin each five qian
    • Bai Zhu (fried) three qian

    The above ingredients should be ground into a fine powder. The dosage is two qian per serving, mixed with one and a half cups of water, boiled for five to seven bubbles, and taken warm before meals. This formula nourishes blood, dispels wind, eliminates phlegm, strengthens the spleen and stomach, regulates blood and energy, and addresses symptoms of energy deficiency with chills and heat. It is highly effective.[215p]
    Yuan Zhi Pill: Treats postpartum organs that are deficient, heart palpitations, unsettled mind, acute abdominal pain, and occasional fear causing restlessness during sleep.

    Ingredients: Yuan Zhi, Mai Men Dong, Huang Qi, fried Dang Gui, Ren Shen, Bai Zhu, Du Huo, Bai Fu Ling, Bai Zi Ren, Shi Chang Pu, cooked dry Di Huang, Shan Zhu Yu, Gui Xin, Zhong Ru Fen, and broken A Jiao, each one liang.

    Grind to a fine powder, mix with honey, and pound 500 to 700 times into balls the size of a phoenix seed. Take thirty pills each time, washed down with warm wine, no time restriction, taken twice daily.

    Bai Fu Ling Pill: Treats postpartum heart deficiency, palpitations, and unsettled spirit.

    Ingredients: Bai Fu Ling and cooked Di Huang, each one liang; Ren Shen, Gui Xin, Yuan Zhi, Shi Chang Pu, Bai Zi Ren, and amber, each half liang.

    Grind to a fine powder, mix with honey, and pound 300 to 200 times into balls the size of a phoenix seed. Take thirty pills each time, washed down with porridge or drink, no time restriction. This formula employs gui with no heat, suitable for those with a slow and weak pulse.

    Ren Shen Powder: Treats postpartum organ deficiency, heart shock, and disordered speech.

    Ingredients: Ren Shen and Mai Men Dong, each eight qian; Niu Huang, Bai Wei, each two qian; Fu Shen, Du Huo, Fang Feng, roasted Gan Cao, Yuan Zhi, raw Di Huang, Zhu Sha ground, Tian Zhu Huang ground, Long Chi ground, each four qian; Long Nao and Musk, each one qian, ground finely.

    Grind to a fine powder, take two qian each time, mixed with mint wine, no time restriction.[216p]
    Liu Po Di Huang San treats blood deficiency with excessive fright, as well as postpartum complications due to retained blood. Ingredients: cinnabar, amber, myrrh, all finely ground; equal parts of angelica. Take two qian each dose, mixed with warm water, three times a day.

    Fu Ling San addresses postpartum delirium, unstable spirit, confusion, heart qi deficiency, and wind pathogen. Ingredients: white poria, poria spirit can also be used; three liang each of raw rehmannia, distant thoughts, white wood, dragon teeth; two liang and five qian each of windbreak, ginseng, and exclusive herb. Grind them together, use one large jin of silver and one斗 and five sheng of water, boil down to seven sheng, then add the herbs, boil down to three sheng, take warmed in three doses. Avoid eating cabbage, pork, or cold food. Another recipe for postpartum wind pathogen resulting in confusion of spirit, use an additional two liang of jing jie and one liang two qian and a half of licorice.

    Additionally, for postpartum weakness, if there are heavy sweating and diarrhea leading to potential death due to severe deficiency, especially for those with a stroke causing incoherent speech and confusion, this should be taken: two liang each of ginseng, poria, notopterygium, and jujube, and one sheng of bamboo juice. Use six sheng of water to boil down to three sheng, add bamboo juice, and further simmer down to two sheng and a half, divided into three doses.[217p]
    Bào Dǎn Wán treats postpartum blood deficiency with shock entering the heart, as well as mania, gout, or when a maiden's meridians are obstructed by disturbance.

    Ingredients:

    • 2 taels of mercury
    • 1.5 taels of black lead
    • 1 tael of cinnabar (finely ground separately)
    • 1 tael of frankincense (finely ground separately)

    Instructions:
    First, melt the black lead in a crucible, add the mercury to form granules. Next, add the cinnabar and sprinkle in the grounded frankincense, mixing while hot with a willow wood pestle until uniform. Form into pills the size of a lotus seed. Take one pill with a decoction of gold, silver, and mint on an empty stomach; ensure to sleep without disturbance, and one will feel at ease upon waking.[218p]
    Amber Powder: Treats postpartum stroke, confusion, slurred speech, irritability of the mind, and unresponsive limbs. Ingredients: Amber, and each of the following herbs: Fu Shen (1 tael), Yuan Zhi (1 tael), Shi Chang Pu (1 tael), Huang Qi (1 tael), Fang Feng (1 tael), Du Huo (1 tael), Ren Shen (1 tael), Mai Men Dong (1 tael), Xiong Dan (1 tael), Sang Ji Sheng (1 tael), Chi Shao Yao (1 tael), and Antelope Horn Powder (1 tael). Also, honey-fried licorice (2.5 qian). Grind all ingredients and take 5 qian each time, boil with 1.5 cups of water until reduced to 1 cup, strain, and take warm without restriction on timing.

    Yuan Zhi Powder: Treats postpartum stroke, confusion, disordered speech, irritability, and restless sleep. Ingredients: Yuan Zhi (1 tael), Fang Feng (1 tael), Mai Men Dong (1 tael), Gui Xin (1 tael), fried Jujube Seed (1 tael), Sang Ji Sheng (1 tael), fried Dang Gui (1 tael), Fu Shen (1 tael, wood removed), Antelope Horn Powder (1 tael), Du Huo (1 tael), each at 7.5 qian; honey-fried licorice (0.5 tael). Grind and take 5 qian, preparing similarly to the previous method.

    Tian Ma Pill: Treats postpartum stroke, confusion, slurred speech, and unresponsive limbs. Ingredients: Tian Ma (1 tael), powdered cinnabar (1 tael), Fang Feng (1 tael), Qiang Huo (1 tael), fried Scorpion (0.5 tael), blast-fried Bai Fu Zi (0.5 tael), fried Wu Ling Zhi (0.5 tael), fried Jiang Can (7.5 qian), fried male sparrow droppings (2.5 qian), and finely powdered Niu Huang (2.5 qian). Grind to a fine powder, mix with glutinous rice and form pills the size of a walnut. Take 20-30 pills, washed down with peppermint liquor, twice daily.[219p]
    Chenshi Yanzhi Pill: This formula is for postpartum wind, resolves wind and phlegm, calms the spirit and settles the heart. Ingredients: 5 parts each of realgar, Acorus calamus, and licorice; 1 ounce each of ginseng, Poria without wood, Chuanxiong, Chinese yam, iron powder, Ophiopogon, Asarum, Gastrodia, and Banxia, soaked in decoction; 1 ounce of Nanjing star and Bai Fuzhong. Grind to a fine powder, cook with ginger juice, form into pills as large as mung beans, wrap each in vermilion powder, and take 30 pills before bedtime, swallowed with ginger soup.

    Heart Disturbance and Abdominal Pain Formula: For treatment of residual blood after childbirth, rushing to the heart, and troubled abdominal pain. Ingredients: equal parts of Chuanxiong, raw dried Rehmannia, zhiqiao, and peony. Grind to a fine powder, take a small spoonful with wine twice a day.

    Classic Efficacious Formula: To treat postpartum qi deficiency, cold obstructing the blood, stasis of blood and qi, and fullness in the heart and abdomen. Ingredients: 2 ounces of peony, 1 ounce each of angelica, cinnamon, Chuanxiong, Wu Yao, and dried tangerine peel, and 1 ounce of fresh ginger. Crush the ingredients, boil with 3 liters of water until reduced to 1 liter, strain and take on an empty stomach.

    Jinhuang Powder: For treating postpartum evil blood rushing to the heart, resulting in restlessness. Ingredients: 0.5 ounce each of Corydalis and cattail pollen, and 2.5 drams of cassia heart. Grind to a fine powder, mix with brewed and cooled plum soup, and take 2 drams.[220p]
    Myrrh Pill - Treats postnatal restlessness and retained harmful blood.

    Ingredients: Myrrh, galangal, Corydalis, stir-fried lacquer tree, angelica, cinnamon heart,牛膝 (ox knee), peony root, and dried ginger, all in equal parts.

    Preparation: Grind the above into a fine powder, mix with vinegar to make a dough, and form into pills the size of a Chinese parasol seed. Take 10 to 15 pills with a decoction.

    Bamboo Leaf Decoction - Treats postnatal shortness of breath and inner agitation.

    Ingredients: Bamboo leaves (finely chopped), Ophiopogon, wheat (each one sheng), licorice (one liang), fresh ginger (two liang), and twelve jujubes.

    Preparation: Chop, then boil the bamboo leaves and wheat in ten dou of water until reduced to eight sheng. Strain out the dregs, add the remaining herbs, and boil down to three sheng. Strain and take warm. If there’s heart palpitations, add two liang of ginseng. If there’s weakness, add five he of glutinous rice.

    Sweet Bamboo Shoot Decoction - Treats postnatal inner deficiency with heat and shortness of breath.

    Ingredients: One sheng of sweet bamboo shoots, one liang each of ginseng, Poria, and licorice, and three liang of Scutellaria.

    Preparation: Chew, then boil in six sheng of water to reduce to two sheng. Strain and divide into three servings for three times a day.

    Garlic White Decoction - Treats postnatal chest heat and counterflow of qi.

    Ingredients: One liang each of garlic white, pinellia, licorice, and ginseng; half a jin of Ophiopogon; and two liang of lily root.

    Preparation: Chew, then boil in thirteen sheng of water until reduced to four sheng. Strain and divide into five doses, taken three times during the day and twice at night. If heat is severe, add one liang of Anemarrhena. For very severe heat, add one liang each of Anemarrhena and gypsum.[221p]
    Paeonia and Gardenia Decoction treats postpartum vexation and inability to lie down. Ingredients: five qian each of peony, angelica, and gardenia; half a he of fragrant bean. Prepare as the Gardenia Decoction. For postpartum febrile conditions, it may also work for changes after labor. Although this formula is said to follow Qi’s method, it's not as reliable as Zhang Jing’s Sour Jujube Decoction.

    Cooked Rehmannia Decoction treats persistent thirst after childbirth, with symptoms of weak breath, weak legs, dizziness, and lack of appetite. Ingredients: one and a half qian of cooked rehmannia, cleaned and steamed; two qian each of ginseng, ophiopogon, and trichosanthes root; half a qian of roasted licorice. Prepare as one dose with two sheng of water, a pinch of glutinous rice, three slices of ginger, and one red date, simmer until reduced to one sheng, to be taken at any time. Another formula treats excessive thirst after childbirth.

    Four liang of ophiopogon, one sheng of sliced reed root, three liang each of trichosanthes, ginseng, licorice, and poria; twenty red dates. Boil with eight sheng of water until three sheng remain, divide into three doses, take all at once; four doses will ensure recovery. Avoid eating cabbage.

    Yang’s Huangqin Powder treats postpartum blood thirst with continuous drinking. Ingredients: equal parts of Huangqin and ophiopogon. Chew and take three qian each time with one and a half bowls of water, decoct to eight parts, remove dregs, and take warm at any time.

    Gathered Verification (of trichosanthes) Decoction treats postpartum blood thirst.[222p]

    • Luo root (4 taels), wheat门冬, earth gourd root, and ginseng (3 taels each); raw rehmannia and licorice (2 taels each); 20 jujubes. Mix these ingredients, add 8 sheng of water, boil down to 2.5 sheng, and divide into three doses. In Chanbao, there is no rehmannia or wheat门冬, and instead, it has equal parts of oyster powder.

    • Yanhusuo powder is for treating postpartum blood loss and unquenchable thirst.
      Ingredients: Curcuma, dried kudzu, cassia flower, green peel, bitter orange peel, yanhusuo - all in equal amounts. Soak in good vinegar overnight, then dry and grind into powder. Dosage: 2 qian per dose, taken with tangerine peel decoction, three times a day and once at night.

    • Mahuang root powder is for treating persistent profuse sweating after childbirth.
      Ingredients: Angelica, astragalus, mahhuang root, calcined oyster, ginseng, powdered licorice - all in equal amounts. Grind these, and the dosage is 4 qian per dose, mixed with one cup of water, boil down to 7 parts, strain, and take warm.

    • Classic effective formula for treating excessive sweating after childbirth.
      Ingredients: Astragalus (1.2 qian), white atractylodes, calcined oyster, poria, mint, wheat门冬 (heart removed), raw rehmannia (each 8 fen), 7 jujubes. Mix, add 2 sheng of water, boil down to 7 he, strain, and take on an empty stomach in two warm portions.

    • Stop Sweat Powder is for treating persistent night sweating after childbirth and for any excessive sweating issues.
      Ingredients: Calcined oyster powder, stir-fried wheat bran (to a yellow color), each in equal parts. Mix well, boil with fresh pork juice, and take 2 qian as needed.[223p]
      Ginseng Decoction
      For treating postnatal deficiencies, fever, and night sweats.
      Equal parts of Ginseng and Angelica.
      Grind them into a fine powder, take one pig kidney after removing the fat membrane, cut into small pieces, add three liters of water, half a handful of glutinous rice, and two scallion whites. Boil until the rice is cooked, then take one cup of the clear juice, add two taels of the herbs, and simmer until reduced by a third. Take warm, without regard to timing.

    Angelica and Six Yellow Decoction
    For treating heat from deficiency of qi and blood, with persistent night sweats.
    Two taels each of Angelica, prepared Rehmannia, and fried Astragalus; one tael each of raw Rehmannia, fried Coptis (blackened), fried Scutellaria (blackened), and fried Berberine (blackened).
    Prepare with water. If the symptoms do not improve, add ginseng and white atractylodes. If there is insufficient heart blood, add sour jujube seeds.

    Ginseng and Aconite Decoction
    For treating deficiency of yang qi with cold, spontaneous sweating, aversion to cold, cold extremities, diarrhea, abdominal pain, hiccup with loss of appetite, or excessive sweating with seizures.
    One tael of Ginseng and five qians of prepared Aconite.
    Prepare as a single dose, boil with ginger and jujubes, and consume slowly.

    Astragalus and Aconite Decoction
    For treating deficiency of yang qi, aversion to cold, spontaneous sweating, tight throat with phlegm, cold extremities, or vomiting and diarrhea with abdominal pain, with inability to eat, and all symptoms of deficiency with cold.
    One tael of Astragalus and five qians of prepared Aconite.[224p]
    Prepare a decoction with the following ingredients: brew with ginger and jujube water. If there is no effect, double the amount of aconite in order to achieve full efficacy.

    Jaundice Formula: For women who have given birth within four months, presenting with jaundice, fatigue in the limbs, reduced appetite, irregular menstruation, occasional fever, and a wiry pulse.

    • Ingredients: 1 tael of white atractylodes, half tael each of ginseng, gentian, peony root, raw rehmannia, reed, bupleurum, and peony, 1 qian each of chuanxiong, huangqin, and dried kudzu, and 0.5 qian of licorice.
      Distribute into twelve portions, decoct and take warm before meals.

    Luo's Rhinoceros Horn Decoction: For postpartum loss of body fluids leading to dryness, spontaneous sweating, fever, fatigue, and dry lips and mouth.

    • Ingredients: Half tael each of rhinoceros horn, ophiopogon, and white atractylodes, 7 qian each of bupleurum, ginseng, fried zhihua, root of earth, raw rehmannia, and roasted licorice, as well as angelica, poria, huangqin, and astragalus.
      Mince the ingredients. Each dose is 4 qian of the mixture, with 3 slices of ginger, 70 grains of floating wheat, decoct and take.

    One-third Decoction: For postpartum weakness with fever and prolonged discomfort.

    • Ingredients: 1.5 qian each of bupleurum, huangqin, ginseng, pinellia, chuanxiong, angelica, peony, cooked rehmannia, and licorice.
      Prepare one dose with 2 cups of water, 3 slices of ginger, and one red date, decoct to 1 cup and take at any time.[225p]
      San Fen San: Treats postnatal fever due to prolonged weakness.
      Ingredients: Chuanxiong, Danggui, Shaoyao, Shudi Huang, Baizhu, Baifuling, Huangqi each 3 grams; Chaihu, Renshen each 4.5 grams; Huangqin, Banxia, Gancao each 2.5 grams.
      Preparation: Make one dose, boil with 480 ml of water, add three slices of ginger and one jujube, simmer until reduced to 240 ml, and take before meals.

    Jia wei Xiaoyao San: Treats postnatal fever, dry mouth, thirst, and lip sores.
    Ingredients: Danggui, Baishaoyao, and Gande each 6 grams; Shudi Huang, Chuanxiong, Huangqin each 4.5 grams; Chaihu 3 grams; Renshen, Maidong each 3.6 grams; two Wumei; and 2 grams Gancao.
    Preparation: Grind and divide into two doses, boil with 480 ml of water until reduced to 360 ml, and take on an empty stomach.

    Renshen Danggui Tang (also known as Renshen Danggui San): Treats excessive blood loss postpartum, leading to blood deficiency and internal heat, resulting in chest fullness, shortness of breath, headaches, and agitation, especially in the afternoon, resembling post-illness distress.
    Ingredients: Shudi, Renshen, Danggui, Rougui, and Maidong each 6 grams; Baishaoyao (fried) 3 grams.
    Preparation: Boil with 480 ml of water, add 100 grams glutinous rice and ten bamboo leaves, and simmer to 240 ml, taking away from meals. If there's severe blood heat, add 6 grams of Shudi Huang. If feeling agitated or uncomfortable, add Dou. In one variation, use cinnamon heart instead of Rougui.[226p]
    Chai Hu Si Wu Decoction, also known as San Yuan Decoction, is used to treat postpartum fatigue and fever that has persisted for a long time, or mild chills and fever, with a deep and rapid pulse, as well as symptoms of heat entering the blood chamber. The ingredients are: 1.5 qian of Chuan Xiong, Dang Gui, Chi Shao Yao, and Shu Di Huang; 0.8 qian of Chai Hu; and 3 qian each of Ren Shen, Huang Qin, Gan Cao, and Ban Xia. Grind the above into a powder and decoct in water for oral administration. One variation uses Sheng Di and adds three slices of ginger, decocting in water for oral administration.

    Xiao Chai Hu Decoction with Sheng Di Huang is used to treat alternating chills and fever postpartum, with a wiry pulse. The ingredients are: 2 liang of Chai Hu, 5 qian of Huang Qin, 3 qian of Ren Shen, 1 liang of Ban Xia, 3 dates, and 5 qian each of Sheng Di Huang, Zhi Zi, and Zhu Dan (stir-fried). Decoct as previously described for oral administration.

    Da Diao Jing San (Great Regulation Decoction) [from "Liang Fang"] is used to treat postpartum blood deficiency, lingering lochia, qi stagnation and turbidity obstruction, disharmony of the nourishing and defensive qi, imbalance of yin and yang, aversion to cold and fever, or sweating, swelling, breathlessness, and urinary urgency. The ingredients are: 1 liang of roasted black soybeans (husk removed) and Fu Shen, and 3 qian of genuine amber. Grind into a fine powder, decoct in thick broth of black beans and perilla, and mix in 2 qian. Another recipe consists of 1 liang of black beans, 5 qian of Fu Shen, and 1 qian of amber.[227p]
    Jia Jian Wu Jin San
    For treating post-illness chill and heat resembling malaria.

    Ingredients:
    Huang Po, Chai Hu, Huang Qin, and Ma Huang, each 2.5 qian; Chen Pi, Dang Gui, Chuan Xiong, Jie Geng, Fu Ling, each 1.5 qian; Ban Xia, Cang Zhu, Bai Zhi, Gui Zhi, Qiang Huo, Cao Guo, Zhi Ke, and Gan Cao, each 0.9 qian; Bai Shao Yao and Shu Di Huang, each 1.5 qian.

    Preparation: Grind the above ingredients into a powder, divide into two doses. For each dose, use 1.5 sheng of water, three slices of fresh ginger, and three green onions, decoct until 1 sheng remains. Take at any time.

    Adjustments:

    • If sweating occurs, increase Dang Gui, Chuan Xiong, Bai Shao Yao, and Shu Di Huang.
    • If there is distention, increase Huang Po and Chen Pi.
    • If there is heat, increase Chai Hu and Huang Qin.
    • If there is cold, increase Cang Zhu, Cao Guo, and Gui Zhi.
    • If phlegm is present, increase Ban Xia, Jie Geng, and Fu Ling.
    • If there is a headache, increase Chuan Xiong, Bai Zhi, and Qiang Huo.
    • If there is diarrhea, omit Zhi Ke and Gan Cao.
    • If residual blood clots cause tidal fever and pain in the abdomen, add San Leng and E Zhu, and increase Yan Hu Suo and Ba Jiao Hui Xiang.
    • For whole body pain, add Qiang Huo and Du Huo.
    • If chills and fever alternate, add Huang Qin and Chai Hu.[228p]
      Chuan Bao Fang
      For treating postnatal chills and fever, with three to five episodes a night, speaking irrationally, sores in the mouth, frequent dry heaving, and overwhelming fatigue.
      Ingredients:
    • Ginseng, Du Huo, Bai Xian Pi, Ge Gen, Fang Feng, Qing Zhu Ru, Yuan Zhi: each 1.5 taels
    • Fu Shen: 2 taels
    • Bai Lian: 2.5 taels
    • Yuan Shen: 3 taels
    • Zhu Li: 2.5 sheng
      Take 1 pound of silver and 15 sheng of water, boil to obtain 7 sheng, add the above herbs, boil again to obtain 3 sheng, and divide into three doses while warm. Avoid fish, wine, and damp foods.

    Zhi Mu Tang
    For treating postnatal sporadic chills and fever, with chest discomfort.
    Ingredients:

    • Zhi Mu: 3 taels
    • Bai Shao Yao, Huang Qin: each 2 taels
    • Gui Xin, Gan Cao: each 1 tael
      Crush and boil with 5 sheng of water, reducing to 2.5 sheng, then divide into three doses. In one variation, omit Gui Xin and use Sheng Di Huang.

    Cao Guo Yin Zi
    For treating postpartum malaria in women, with alternating chills and fever, or predominantly fever.
    Ingredients:

    • Ban Xia Tang Pao, Chi Fu Ling, Gan Cao Zhi, Cao Guo Pao (skin removed), Chuan Xiong, Chen Pi, Bai Zhi: each 2 qian
    • Qing Pi (white removed), Liang Jiang, Zi Su: each 2.5 qian
    • Gan Ge: 4 qian
      Crush and take 3 qian with 1 large cup of water, 3 slices of fresh ginger, and 2 jujubes, boil until reduced by two-thirds. Take three doses continuously on a day of rising sun, with none feeling uneasy.[229p]
      Sheng Shu Yin Zi: Treats postpartum malaria, especially in those with significant cold.

    Ingredients: Nutmeg, black cardamom, fresh magnolia bark (remove coarse skin), pinellia tuber, dried tangerine peel, licorice, red dates (pitted), and fresh ginger, all in equal parts.

    Preparation: Finely grind these eight ingredients and mix well. Use half fresh and half wrapped in moist paper to be roasted until fragrant and well cooked. Remove the paper and mix with the other half that is fresh. Each dose is five qian (a weight measurement), with two bowls of water, decocted until reduced by seven parts. Take one dose before meals and one after.

    Shizi Soup: Treats postpartum weakness, cough, and fatigue, with fluctuating chills and fever resembling malaria, along with limb pain and pallid yellow complexion, referred to as "Ru Lao."

    Ingredients: Chopped green onion whites, japonica rice, angelica root, fragrant bean, peony root, each two liang (a weight measurement), and two pig kidneys (fatty membranes removed and broken into four parts).

    Preparation: Grind these ingredients and divide into two doses. Use three sheng (measurement of water), boil to obtain a small bowl of liquid, strain out the dregs, and divide into three doses. If the formula lacks peony root, it can be replaced with ginseng; if it lacks fragrant bean, substitute with anemarrhena.

    Pig Kidney Congee: Treats Ru Lao with fever, joint pain, and headaches.

    Ingredients: One pig kidney and one cup of japonica rice.[230p]
    Cut the pig's kidneys, removing the white membrane, into pieces like willow leaves. Mix with salt and wine. First, boil polished rice with scallions and pepper to make congee, then season with salt and vinegar. Lay the kidneys at the bottom of a bowl and cover them with hot congee, creating a hollow space which is to be consumed.

    Ginseng and Turtle Shell Powder (from "Effective Recipes"):

    This treats exhaustion due to insufficient recovery after childbirth within a hundred days, where the body becomes weak, and the blood and qi are still deficient. This may occur from poor management or excessive labor, resulting in symptoms like weakness, alternating between lying down and getting up, indigestion, occasional coughing, dizziness, irregular hair loss, nocturnal sweats, chills and fevers resembling malaria, tightness in the back, and difficulty remaining in bed. Taking this remedy is highly effective.

    Ingredients: Ginseng, cinnamon heart, angelica, mulberry mistletoe, white poria, ophiopogon, white peony, peach kernel, cooked rehmannia, licorice (each 0.5 tael); turtle shell and astragalus (each 1 tael); deyuan (2.5 qian); niuxi (7.5 qian).

    Grind the above into a fine powder. For each dose, use one pair of pig kidneys, remove the membranes, add two large bowls of water, half a fen of ginger, and three jujubes. Boil until reduced to one bowl, then remove the kidneys, ginger, and jujubes. Next, add 2 qian of the powdered medicine, three inches of scallion white, one black plum, and five spikes of schizonepeta, boiling until reduced to about 70%. Strain the mixture and take on an empty stomach before dinner. This remedy is remarkably effective. If lacking pig kidneys, ginger, jujubes, scallion white, black plum, and schizonepeta, consume the powdered medicine decocted in water on an empty stomach.

    From the Comprehensive Collection of Ancient and Modern Medical Texts, Volume 394.[231p]
    Postpartum Disorders

    If a woman experiences pain in the neck, limbs, and skin after childbirth, especially after sitting for long periods, speaking often, or using excessive energy, this is known as "ru la" (postpartum fatigue).

    Ingredients:

    • Cinnamon heart and scallions, each one tael
    • A pair of pig kidneys, with the fat removed
    • Angelica, peony, and fresh ginger, each two taels

    Preparation: Boil eight sheng of water with the kidneys to obtain six sheng. Add the herbs and boil until reduced to two sheng. Divide and serve warm in two doses.

    White Poria Powder treats postpartum fatigue caused by recent childbirth, prolonged sitting, excessive speaking, or physical exertion, leading to pain in the head, limbs, and symptoms resembling intermittent fever.

    Ingredients:

    • White poria, one tael
    • Angelica, Chuanxiong, cinnamon heart, white peony, astragalus, and ginseng, each half a tael
    • Dried prepared rehmannia, one tael

    Preparation: Grind finely, then boil with two cupfuls of water, adding a pair of pig kidneys (fat removed), three slices of ginger, and three dates. Simmer until reduced to one cup, remove the three ingredients, add half a tael of herbs, simmer for seven minutes, strain, and serve warm in three doses before meals.[232p]
    Hu's Peony Powder
    For treating postnatal deficiency, fever with spontaneous sweating, tendency to develop postpartum illness, or irregular menstruation due to blood and qi disturbances, or for those who are cold, weak, and emaciated.
    Ingredients:

    • White Peony Root: 0.5 taels
    • Angelica: 0.5 taels
    • Five-Leaf Geranium Bark: 2 qian
    • Rehmannia Root: 2 qian
    • Ginseng: 0.5 taels
    • Myrrh: 2 qian
    • Cinnamon Heart: 2 qian
    • Peony Bark: 3 qian

    Grind these into a fine powder. For each dose, take 2 qian with half a cup of water and half a cup of wine. For those who do not drink alcohol, use only one cup of water. Add a coin of Kaoyuan and dip it in sesame oil, then decoct for seven minutes. Strain it and take by mouth. Do not stir while cooking; do not blow on it while eating.

    Huangqi Decoction Powder
    For treating postpartum fatigue, yellow and emaciated skin, pale complexion, aversion to cold with heat symptoms, aching limbs, irritability, and headaches.
    Ingredients:

    • Soft-shelled Turtle Shell (vinegar-baked): 1 tael
    • Astragalus: 1 tael
    • Cinnamon Heart: 2 qian
    • Angelica (fried): 2 qian
    • Mulberry Mistletoe: 2 qian
    • White Poria: 2 qian
    • White Peony Root: 2 qian
    • Ginseng: 2 qian
    • Prepared Rehmannia: 2 qian
    • Asparagus Root (heart removed): 2 qian
    • Honey-fried Licorice: 2 qian
    • Achyranthes Root: 7.5 qian

    Grind these into a fine powder. For each dose, take a pair of pig kidneys, remove the fat and membranes, cut them open, and first boil with one cup of water, half a piece of ginger, and three jujubes until reduced to seven-fifths. Remove the kidneys, ginger, and jujubes, then add 5 qian of the powder and boil until reduced to four-fifths. Strain and take warm on an empty stomach before dinner. Both residues should be decocted together.[233p]
    Huangqi Jianzhong Decoction treats postpartum deficiency, heat or cold sensations, and abdominal pain. Ingredients include: 1 tael of fried Huangqi, 1 tael of fried Gui, 2 taels of fried Bai Shao, and 7 qian of fried Gan Cao. Take 5 qian mixed with ginger and jujube water, decoct and serve two to three times a day. If very deficient, add Fuzi.

    Xuliang Decoction replenishes postpartum deficiency. Ingredients include: 1 qian each of Ren Shen and Bai Zhu, 5 fen each of Huang Qin and Gui Shen Wei, 5 fen of Chuan Xiong, 3 fen of Chen Pi. Decoct this mixture in water; if there is cold, add 3 fen of Gan Jiang and 1 qian of Fu Ling.

    Sanhe Powder treats long-term postpartum weakness when acupuncture is ineffective. Ingredients include: 1 tael each of Bai Zhu, Dang Gui, Bai Shao Yao, Huang Qi, Bai Fu Ling, Chuan Xiong, and Shu Di Huang, and 1.5 taels each of Chai Hu and Ren Shen, and 6 qian each of Huang Qin, Ban Xia, and Gan Cao. Grind these into a coarse powder; take 1 tael with 1.5 measures of water, decoct and serve three times a day.[234p]
    Angelica and Lamb Soup

    This remedy is for postpartum weakness, along with abdominal pain. Use one pound of fatty lamb meat, one dou of water to wash off the fat, and simmer to obtain eight sheng. Add five taels of angelica, four taels of astragalus, and six taels of fresh ginger. Simmer these three ingredients in the meat broth to yield two sheng and five he, dividing it into four doses. In "Entering the Door," one pair of pig kidneys can replace the lamb. Another version includes seven qian of ginseng. If lochia is inadequate, add seven and three taels of cinnamon. If there’s excessive lochia, increase chuanxiong by three taels. If there’s gas, add two taels of xixin. If there’s heat, add two he of fresh rehmannia juice.

    Ten Complete Nourishing Soup

    Also known as Ten Complete Powder. This remedy treats various postpartum organ deficiencies, qi and blood vacuum, chills and fever, spontaneous sweating, night sweating, blood in stool, vomiting blood, possible diarrhea, diminished appetite, or pain in the chest and abdomen, mouth sores, blurred vision, and loose teeth.

    Ingredients include ginseng, white atractylodes, white poria, astragalus, angelica, fried white peony, mature rehmannia prepared with wine and steamed, and chuanxiong, each at one qian; peeled cinnamon and honey-fried licorice, each at five fen.

    Prepare and take as a decoction with ginger and jujube water.[235p]
    Supplementation Formula: For treating great deficiency after childbirth, with blood and qi rebelling upwards, urgent pain in the heart and abdomen, and shortness of breath.
    Ingredients: Astragalus, Atractylodes, Angelica, Ginseng, Licorice, each 2 taels; Ginger 4 taels.
    Preparation: First, take 3 jin of white lamb meat, remove the membrane, combine with 19 sheng of water, boil the meat to extract 5 sheng of broth, then add all the herbs, boil down to 3 sheng, and divide into three servings.

    Postpartum Treasure Formula: For treating postpartum wind deficiency causing emaciation and weakness, with no muscle regeneration.
    Ingredients: Astragalus, Angelica, Peony, Ginseng, each 3 fen; Cassia heart, Licorice, Chuanxiong, Ginger, each 4 fen; 12 dates.
    Preparation: Combine these nine ingredients with 7 sheng of water, boil down to 3 sheng, and divide into three warm servings.

    Another Formula: For treating postpartum deficiency disorders, joint pain, headaches, and inability to sweat.
    Ingredients: Angelica, Ginseng, Ginger, each 2 taels; Astragalus 3 taels; Glutinous rice, Chinese chives, Fermented soybean curd 3 he; 2 pig kidneys.
    Preparation: Use 15 sheng of water, first boil the pig kidneys to get 6 sheng of broth, then add all the herbs and boil down to 2 sheng, divided into three servings.

    Atractylodes Decoction: For treating a hardened and swollen abdomen resembling a pan, with a spinning sensation and water retention, termed the "qi level".
    Ingredients: Bitter Orange 1.5 taels; Atractylodes 3 taels.[236p]
    Take the above (口父) dosage, four qian each time, with one and a half bowls of water, decoct until reduced to seven parts, strain and take warm. If the abdomen becomes soft, it should disperse.

    Duo Hun San treats postpartum weakness with swelling and shortness of breath; urination will improve the condition.

    Ingredients:

    • Fresh ginger juice: three liang
    • Wheat flour: three liang
    • Banxia (Half Summer): seven pieces

    Wrap the wheat flour with the ginger juice around the Banxia to form seven cakes, and bake until scorched and cooked into powder. Mix one cake with water for effect.

    Han Fang Ji San is a medicine that promotes the movement of water and qi; weak individuals should avoid taking it.

    Ingredients:

    • Han Fang Ji: one liang
    • Zhu Ling: one liang
    • Zhi Ke: one liang
    • Sang Bai Pi: one liang
    • Shang Lu: seven and a half qian
    • Gan Cao: seven and a half qian

    Grind into a coarse powder, take four qian each time with one and a half bowls of water, three slices of ginger, decoct until reduced to seven parts, strain, and take warm on an empty stomach.

    Jia Wei Ba Wu Tang treats full-body swelling, shortness of breath, and tidal fever postpartum.

    Ingredients:

    • Ren Shen (Ginseng): three qian
    • Bai Fu Ling (White Poria): three qian
    • Shu Di Huang (Prepared Rehmannia): three qian
    • Xiao Hui Xiang (Fennel): three qian
    • Bai Zhu (Atractylodes): four qian
    • Chuan Xiong (Ligusticum): four qian
    • Dang Gui (Angelica): five qian
    • Bai Shao (White Peony): five qian
    • Xiang Fu (Cyperus): five qian
    • Gan Cao (Licorice): one qian
    • Huang Qin (Scutellaria): one qian
    • Chai Hu (Bupleurum): one qian

    Grind and divide into six or seven doses. Each dose takes half a bowl of water and three slices of ginger, decoct until reduced to seven parts, and take warm on an empty stomach. After finishing this medicine, then take the regulating menstrual pills.

    If there is abdominal pain, add three qian each of Yan Hu Suo, Gan Qi, and Zhi Ke. If there is vomiting and nausea, add two qian each of Liang Jiang and Sha Ren. If there are numbness in hands or feet, add one and a half qian of Rou Gui. If there is coughing, add Wu Wei Zi, Dong Hua, and Xing Ren.[237p]
    Qizhen San (Relieving Headache Powder)
    For treating children's head pain:
    Angelica (Dang Gui) and White Peony (Bai Shao) each 2 qian, Chuanxiong 1.5 qian, White Atractylodes (Bai Zhi), Cinnamon (Gui Xin), Cat Tail (Pu Huang), Peony (Mu Dan Pi), Yunhu Suo (Yan Hu Suo), Wulingzhi, Myrrh (Mo Yao) each 7 fen.
    Crush the ingredients and make into a paste, decoct in water, add good vinegar, and take on an empty stomach.

    Siwei Tang (Four Flavor Decoction)
    For treating postpartum blood circulation:
    Angelica (Dang Gui), Yunhu Suo (Yan Hu Suo), Dragon’s Blood (Xue Jie), and Myrrh (Mo Yao) each 1 qian.
    Grind them coarsely and decoct with children's urine. The "Entering the Door" text mentions finely ground, 2 qian, mixed with children's urine, called Siwei San.

    Buqi Yangxue Tang (Qi-nourishing and Blood-nourishing Decoction)
    For treating excessive bleeding after miscarriage:
    Ginseng (Ren Shen), Astragalus (Huang Qi), Angelica (Dang Gui), White Atractylodes (Bai Zhu), White Peony (Bai Shao) wine, Mugwort (Ai Ye), Donkey-hide Gelatin (A Jiao), Chuanxiong, Green Peel (Qing Pi), Stir-fried Cyperus (Xiang Fu), ground Sand Ginger (Sha Ren), and Licorice (Gan Cao) each 1 qian.
    Grind the ingredients, make into a paste, and decoct in water.

    Ermu San (Two Mothers Powder)
    For treating postpartum lochia flowing into the lung channel and causing cough:
    Anemarrhena (Zhi Mu), Fritillaria (Bei Mu), White Poria (Bai Fu Ling), and Ginseng (Ren Shen) each 1 qian, Peach Kernel (Tao Ren) and Apricot Kernel (Xing Ren) each 2 qian.
    Grind into a paste and decoct in water.[238p]
    Xiao Can Su Drink

    For treating postpartum blood loss entering the lungs, causing blackening of the face, wheezing, and wanting to die.

    Ingredients: Su Mu (Sappan wood), Ren Shen (ginseng)

    Take 2 taels of Su Mu, 2 bowls of water, decoct until reduced to 1 bowl, then mix in 2 qian of finely powdered Ren Shen and take.

    Xuanfu Flower Decoction (from "San Yin Fang")

    For treating postpartum cold and flu, with severe cough and phlegm.

    Ingredients: Xuanfu Flower, Chi Shao Yao (Red Peony), Jing Jie (Schizonepeta), Ban Xia Qu (Pinellia), Wu Wei Zi (Schisandra), Ma Huang (Ephedra), Chi Fu Ling (Red Poria), Xing Ren (Apricot kernel), Qian Hu (Peucedanum), Gan Cao (Licorice) each 1 qian.

    Grind these together for one dose, add 3 slices of ginger and 2 jujubes, and decoct with water to take.

    Chai Hu Po Yu Decoction (from "Ru Men," same source)

    For treating postpartum fever due to cold illnesses, with heat entering the blood chamber or lochia not flowing.

    Ingredients: Chai Hu (Bupleurum), Huang Qin (Scutellaria), Ban Xia, Gan Cao, Chi Shao Yao, Dang Gui (Angelica), Sheng Di Huang (Raw Rehmannia) each 1 qian; Tao Ren (Peach kernel), Wu Ling Zhi (Wulingzhi) each 5 fen.

    Grind these together for one patch, decoct with water to take.

    Chai Hu Fang Gui Decoction

    For treating postpartum fever, with wind-cold exterior symptoms.

    Ingredients: Dang Gui 3 qian, Chuan Xiong (Chuanxiong) 1.5 qian, Chai Hu, Ren Shen each 1 qian; Ban Xia, Chen Pi (Aged Tangerine Peel), Fang Feng (Siler) each 8 fen; Gan Cao 5 fen.

    Grind these together for one patch, add 3 slices of ginger and 2 jujubes, and decoct with water to take.[239p]
    Bamboo Leaf Wind-Preventing Decoction: Treats postpartum febrile illness, headache, and fever.

    • 24 pieces of green bamboo leaves
    • 1 qian each of wind-protecting herb, ginseng, cinnamon twig, platycodon, fang feng, dried tangerine peel, and red poria.
      Grind them together, make a paste, add 3 slices of ginger and 2 jujubes, and decoct in water to take.

    Angelica and Astragalus Decoction: Treats postpartum yin deficiency.

    • 3 qian of stir-fried astragalus,
    • 2 qian each of ginseng, angelica, and raw licorice.
      Grind them together, make a paste, decoct in water to take three times a day.

    Sulfur Decoction: Treats postpartum inability to close the Yin Gate (vaginal canal).

    • 4 liang of sulfur,
    • 1.5 liang each of wuyou and dodder,
    • 1 liang of snake bed seeds.
      Grind, and for every 5 qian, decoct in 1 bowl of water, warm and frequently fumigate and wash the affected area to help it close.

    Blood Wind Decoction: Treats postpartum wind contractions or weakness.

    • 1 liang each of chuanxiong, angelica, cooked rehmannia, white atractylodes, and white poria,
    • 7 qian each of white peony root, gentian, qianghuo, and white angelica,
    • 5 qian of wind-protecting herb.
      Use half of it to make a fine powder, mixing 2 qian with warm wine; use the other half to make honey pills the size of wutong nuts, swallowing 50 to 70 pills with warm wine.[240p]
      Peach Kernel Decoction
      Treats postpartum lochia that suddenly ceases, with heavy pain in the lower back and abdomen, or pain radiating to the legs.
    • 5 qian of peeled and ground peach kernels
    • 5 qian of su mu (sandalwood)
    • 5 qian of raw rehmannia
    • 30 pieces each of dried Cnidium (removed feet and wings) and water leeches (stir-fried)

    Grind the above into a coarse powder. For every 3 qian, use one cup of water, and simmer until reduced to two-thirds. Strain and take warm; when the harmful blood is expelled, treatment can stop. If there is great pain suggesting a deficiency, it may lead to abscesses, so one should take Wuxiang Lianqiao Soup without rhubarb, decocted in water and consumed with bamboo juice.

    Wuxiang Lianqiao Soup
    Treats lochia leading to abscesses.

    • 2 liang each of:
      • Mu Xiang
      • Chen Xiang
      • Clove
      • Frankincense
      • Sheng Ma (bugbane)
      • Du Huo (angelica)
      • Musk
      • Lian Qiao (forsythia)
      • Mu Tong (dogwood)
      • Sang Ji Sheng (mulberry mistletoe)

    Grind the above into a coarse powder. Use 2 cups of water to boil down to one cup, strain, and add bamboo juice as desired, taking it warm. One variation includes 1 liang of rhubarb.

    Peach and Cinnamon Dang Gui Pills
    Treats postpartum lochia that suddenly ceases, accompanied by rapid shifts of cold and heat, with pain throughout the navel and abdomen, feeling like needle pricks.

    • 5 qian each of:
      • Cinnamon heart
      • Red peony root
    • 2.5 qian each of:
      • Angelica
      • Peeled and ground peach kernel
      • Myrrh
    • 30 pieces each of dried Cnidium (stir-fried) and water leeches

    Grind the above into a fine powder, mix with vinegar to form dough-sized pills, and take 20 pills with vinegar soup.[241p]
    Blood Nourishing Liver Pill

    Treats postnatal left hypochondriac distension and pain, causing insomnia.

    Ingredients:

    • 1 liang of Xiangfu (Cyperus rotundus) sautéed in vinegar
    • 1 liang of Danggui (Angelica sinensis)
    • 1 liang of Chuanxiong (Ligusticum chuanxiong)
    • 1 liang of Baishao (Paeonia lactiflora) sautéed in wine
    • 1 liang of Chenpi (Citrus reticulata)
    • 1 liang of Banxia (Pinellia ternata) sautéed in oil
    • 1 liang of Baizhu (Atractylodes macrocephala) sautéed in soil
    • 1 liang of Danpi (Moutan cortex)
    • 1 liang of Shenqu (Fermented wheat) sautéed
    • 1 liang of Luobozi (Radish seeds) sautéed
    • 1 liang of Qingpi (Green tangerine peel) sautéed in oil
    • 1 liang of Honghua (Carthamus tinctorius)
    • 1 liang of Fuling (Poria cocos)
    • 8 qian of Chaihu (Bupleurum) sautéed in wine
    • 8 qian of Taoren (Peach kernel) sautéed
    • 6 qian of Caolongdan (Gentian)
    • 5 qian each of Sanleng (Sparganium) and Pojiao (Atractylodes)

    Grind the above into a powder, mix with wine to form pills the size of walnuts, to be taken with warm water, one hundred pills at a time.

    Qi Regulation and Blood Nourishing Pill

    Treats postnatal right hypochondriac swelling, with a pain like a taut string that worsens with cold.

    Ingredients:

    • 2 liang of Xiangfu (Cyperus rotundus)
    • 1 liang each of Danggui (Angelica sinensis), Chuanxiong (Ligusticum chuanxiong), Baishao (Paeonia lactiflora) sautéed in wine, Baizhu (Atractylodes macrocephala) sautéed in soil, Qingpi (Green tangerine peel) sautéed in oil, Chenpi (Citrus reticulata), Zhi Shi (Bitter orange), Wu Yao (Lindera aggregata), Hou Po (Magnolia officinalis), Shenqu (Fermented wheat), Gan Jiang (Dried ginger) sautéed until black, and Bai Jiuzi (White mustard seed)
    • 8 qian each of Sanleng (Sparganium) sautéed in vinegar and Pojiao (Atractylodes) sautéed in vinegar
    • 6 qian each of Maiya (Barley sprout) sautéed and Rougui (Cinnamon)
    • 3 qian of Muxiang (Aromatic wood)

    Grind the above into a powder, mix with vinegar to form pills the size of walnuts, to be taken with rice soup, one hundred pills at a time.[242p]
    Xiang Ling Wan (from "Ben Shi Fang")
    Treats persistent vomiting after childbirth.
    Six parts of clove and realgar, separately ground;
    One part of five-spice resin.
    Grind finely, mix with dog or pig bile; form into pills the size of adzuki beans.
    Take with a decoction of ginger and dried tangerine peel.

    Shen Zhu Gao (from Dan Xi)
    Treats urinary issues after childbirth.
    Two and a half taels of ginseng; two taels of white atractylodes; one and a half taels of astragalus;
    One tael each of dried tangerine peel and sunflower seeds;
    One tael each of cattail pollen, peach resin, talc, peach kernels, and white poria;
    Five fen of licorice.
    Grind together, create a paste; simmer with pig or sheep bladder, add herbs, then simmer again, strain, and take warm on an empty stomach.

    Mao Gen Tang (from "San Yin Fang")
    Treats urinary issues after childbirth.
    Four taels of white grass root;
    Sixteen stones from the head of a stone perch;
    Five fen each of wheat and licorice;
    Two taels of white poria; five purple shells.
    Grind the ingredients; take two fen mixed with a decoction of Akebia, or grind coarsely, three fen, and cook with lampwick for consumption.

    Sang Piao Xiao San (from Yun Qi)
    Treats urinary issues after childbirth and enuresis.
    Fifteen pieces of saussurea, roasted;
    One and a half taels each of deer antler and roasted astragalus;
    One tael each of oyster powder, ginseng, red stone resin, and magnolia bark.
    Grind together; take two fen on an empty stomach mixed with rice drink.[243p]
    Huangqi Shaoyao Decoction (from "San Yin Fang")
    For the treatment of urinary incontinence after childbirth.
    Ingredients: Astragalus (Huangqi) 1.5 qian, Angelica tail (Danggui wei) 1.5 qian, White Peony (Bai Shao) 1.5 qian, Atractylodes (Bai Zhu) 1 qian, Ginseng (Ren Shen) 0.5 qian, Dried Tangerine Peel (Chen Pi) 0.5 qian, and Honey-fried Licorice (Gan Cao) 0.3 qian.
    Grind these ingredients into a paste, decoct with water, and take on an empty stomach.

    Deqi San (from "De Xiaofang")
    For the treatment of postpartum diarrhea and retained lochia. This indicates residual blood seeping into the large intestine causing diarrhea, showing dark blue-black stool.
    Ingredients: Big Sweetscented Flag (Da Jing Jie) burned in a fire in a dish without touching oil, a small amount of Musk (She Xiang).
    Grind into a powder, take 1 qian mixed in boiling water and drink, it is remarkably effective.

    Zichang Wuren Pill (from "Zheng Zhuan")
    For the treatment of postpartum blood deficiency leading to constipation.
    Ingredients: Tangerine Peel (Ju Hong) 4 liang, Apricot Kernel (Xing Ren) 1 liang, Peach Kernel (Tao Ren) 1 liang, Platycladus Seed (Bai Zi Ren) 0.5 liang, Pine Nut (Song Zi Ren) 0.25 liang, Plum Kernel (Li Ren) 0.1 liang.
    Grind each separately into a paste, make the Tangerine Peel into a powder, mix with honey to form pills the size of a jujube, take 50-60 pills with rice drink.

    Wuwai Baizhu Powder (from Dan Xi)
    For the treatment of postpartum swelling, suitable for tonifying and promoting water movement.
    Ingredients: Atractylodes (Bai Zhu) 3 qian, Dried Tangerine Peel (Chen Pi) without white 1.5 qian, Lonicera (Mu Tong) 1 qian, Chuanxiong (Chuan Qiong) 1 qian, Red Poria (Chi Fu Ling) 1 qian.
    Grind these into powder, make into a paste with water, take as a decoction along with 25 pills.[244p]
    Buxu Decoction
    For weakness and heat before and after childbirth, modify this recipe.
    1.5 qian of ginseng and atrae,
    1 qian each of angelica, chuanxiong, astragalus, and dried citrus peel,
    0.7 qian of licorice.
    Grind these together to make a dose, add three slices of ginger, and decoct in water to take. If the heat is mild, double the amount of poria. If the heat is severe, add jujube. If the heat is extreme, add dry ginger roasted until black, which helps the other herbs enter the liver channel to generate blood.

    Bao'an Pill
    For treating various ailments before and after childbirth.
    Separate and grind 2 liang of raw dried rehmannia,
    1 liang each of silkworm cast-off paper,
    7.5 qian each of red poria, moutan root, and white peony root,
    5 qian each of chuanxiong, asarum, ginseng, cinnamon, angelica, and achyranthes,
    2.5 qian each of evodia and agarwood,
    Water calcinated stones, white atractylodes, musk, and prepared aconite, together with 5 qian each of ephedra, azolla, honey-fried licorice, notopterygium, and balloonflower.
    Grind these into a powder, form into pills the size of a marble, and swallow one with wine.

    Angelica Needs to be Powdered
    For treating postpartum blood stasis, abdominal pain, and alternating cold and heat.
    0.8 qian each of safflower and peach kernel,
    0.5 qian of licorice,
    0.6 qian of cinnamon,
    1 qian each of red peony, frankincense, cyperus, and sappan wood.
    Decoct with water and wine to take.[245p]
    Sure! Here is the translated text:

    "Shu Kliao Wu Ji San treats postpartum food stagnation with cold and heat, causing pain in the heart and abdomen. Use the following ingredients: five parts of bitter orange peel, three parts each of white atractylodes, chuanxiong, licorice, fu ling, peony, and angelica, six parts of dried tangerine peel, one part of black drug, and four parts each of dried magnolia bark and dried ginger. One and a half parts of platycodon and two parts of pinellia, and seven and a half parts of azure bark. Except for white atractylodes and cinnamon, the other twelve ingredients are to be sautéed over low heat until their color changes, cooled, and then mixed with cinnamon, atractylodes, and black drug, and decocted for consumption.

    Li Zhong Tang treats postpartum chills and heart pain. Use two qian of ginseng, white atractylodes, and dried ginger each, and one and a half qian of licorice. Decoct in water and take warm.

    Mu Bin Tang treats postpartum emotional distress, with both blood and qi affected, causing heart pain. The ingredients are equal parts of aromatic wood, betel nut, Corydalis, golden bell fruit, san leng, curcuma, dried magnolia bark, platycodon, chuanxiong, angelica, white peony, yellow qin, and licorice. Decoct in water for consumption.

    Gui Xin Tang treats those with existing cold conditions, who become very weak after childbirth, with cold obstructing the blood, causing it to congeal and fail to disperse, leading to heart pain. Use one qian each of cinnamon heart, small licorice, wuzhuyu, dried ginger, du huo, cooked rehmannia, angelica, and white peony, and three fen each of licorice and xixin. Decoct in water for consumption."[246p]
    Four Gentlemen Decoction: For postpartum chills, abdominal pain with rumbling intestines, adjust as necessary. Ingredients: Ginseng, Poria, Licorice, Atractylodes. Prepare with ginger and jujube.

    Three Whites Decoction: For postpartum distended abdomen with tight pulse. Ingredients: One ounce of white sugar, one egg white, one and a half jugs of wine. Boil down to eight parts and take warm.

    Sheng Mai Powder: For postpartum qi deficiency, thirst. Ingredients: Ginseng, Schisandra, Ophiopogon, each adjusted to two parts as needed. Grind into a powder.

    Yellow Hen Soup: For postpartum weakness and abdominal pain. Ingredients: Half an ounce each of Angelica, Atractylodes (fried), Rehmannia, Astragalus (fried), and Cinnamon, one small yellow hen (remove head, feet, intestines, and finely chop). First use seven bowls of water to boil the chicken down to three bowls. For each serving, use one bowl of the broth and four qian of the herbs, decoct three times daily.

    Nine Treasures Decoction: For postpartum Yang deficiency with cold, or when Yin pathogens invade the organs, resulting in abdominal pain, vomiting, lack of appetite, and cold extremities. Ingredients: Three qian each of Angelica and Rehmannia, half a qian each of fried Peony and Poria, one qian each of roasted Licorice, dried ginger (fried black), Cinnamon, and North fine Xixin, five fen of Evodia.
